rules.short_rule_statement: Rule The term “subject matter jurisdiction” refers to a court’s competence to hear and determine cases of a general class and subject to which the proceedings in question...
rules.source_text:
In General
Rule The term “subject matter jurisdiction” refers to a court’s competence to hear and determine cases of a general class and subject to which the proceedings in question belong. The five most common congressional grants of subject matter jurisdiction are: Federal question jurisdiction Diversity jurisdiction Supplemental jurisdiction Removal jurisdiction Legislative jurisdiction California Distinction: Subject Matter Jurisdiction in California State Courts California has one superior court in each of its 58 counties that exercises general subject matter jurisdiction over all civil matters. “Subject matter jurisdiction” refers to the state’s classification of civil cases as “unlimited” or “limited”:

rules.canonical_name: Unlimited Civil Cases – Actions In Which The Amount In Controversy Exceeds
rules.rule_statement: $35,000. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 88. ii) Limited Civil Cases – actions in which the amount in controversy is $35,000 or less. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 86(a)(1). Small Claims Cases: Limited civil actions brought by individuals in which the amount in controversy is $12,500 or less ($6,250 or less for other litigants) may be brought in small claims court. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code §§ 116.220, 116.221. Calculation of Jurisdictional Amount: The amount in controversy is determined exclusive of attorney’s fees, interest, and costs. The value of multiple claims may be aggregated under the same rules and limitations that apply to calculate amount in controversy in federal diversity claims. Motion for Reclassification: The superior court does not lose jurisdiction if an action was originally misclassified, or if a correctly classified action subsequently appears misclassified based on the pleadings or proceedings. Instead, a party, or the court on its own motion, may request reclassification. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 403.040. Notice and a hearing are required for reclassification by motion. At the hearing, the court may consider matters outside the pleadings but may not consider the merits of the action. verdict will “necessarily” fall below the $35,000.01 amount.

rules.rule_statement: Because federal courts are courts of limited jurisdiction, a federal court must presume an absence of jurisdiction until it determines that the matter falls within its rightful jurisdiction. The burden is on the party seeking to invoke the court’s jurisdiction.
rules.short_rule_statement: Because federal courts are courts of limited jurisdiction, a federal court must presume an absence of jurisdiction until it determines that the matter falls within its rightful...
rules.source_text:
Presumption
Because federal courts are courts of limited jurisdiction, a federal court must presume an absence of jurisdiction until it determines that the matter falls within its rightful jurisdiction. The burden is on the party seeking to invoke the court’s jurisdiction.

rules.rule_statement: Unlike personal jurisdiction, subject matter jurisdiction cannot be waived or agreed to by the parties. See § II. Personal Jurisdiction, infra.
rules.short_rule_statement: Unlike personal jurisdiction, subject matter jurisdiction cannot be waived or agreed to by the parties. See § II. Personal Jurisdiction, infra.

Waiver
Unlike personal jurisdiction, subject matter jurisdiction cannot be waived or agreed to by the parties. See § II. Personal Jurisdiction, infra.

rules.rule_statement: An objection to subject matter jurisdiction can be presented by any party at any stage of a proceeding (including on appeal) or may be raised by the court. However, a judgment ordinarily may not be challenged collaterally on that basis, if the issue was fully and fairly litigated and finally decided in the court that rendered the original judgment.
rules.short_rule_statement: An objection to subject matter jurisdiction can be presented by any party at any stage of a proceeding (including on appeal) or may be raised by the court. However, a judgment...
rules.source_text:
Objection To Jurisdiction
An objection to subject matter jurisdiction can be presented by any party at any stage of a proceeding (including on appeal) or may be raised by the court. However, a judgment ordinarily may not be challenged collaterally on that basis, if the issue was fully and fairly litigated and finally decided in the court that rendered the original judgment.

rules.rule_statement: The term “abstention” refers to a policy allowing federal courts to decline to exercise jurisdiction in certain circumstances in deference to a state court’s resolution of a controversy. In general, a federal court with subject matter jurisdiction is required to adjudicate the controversy despite the pendency of a similar action in a state court. A federal court may abstain from hearing a case or stay the matter pending the outcome of the state-court action if one of the following four types of abstention apply: Types of Abstention Resolution of a state-law issue by the state court would eliminate the need for the federal court to decide a federal constitutional Pullman issue. Avoidance of federal involvement with a complex state regulatory Burford scheme or matter of great importance to the state. The state action involves the imposition of a civil fine or punishment of an individual for criminal activity or contempt of court, and the Younger federal court is asked to enjoin that activity. Parallel proceedings that go beyond a mere waste of judicial Colorado River resources, such as when federal policy supports unitary adjudication of the issues.

rules.rule_statement: A federal court that lacks jurisdiction must transfer the action to another federal court in which the action could have been brought at the time of filing if the transfer is in the interests of justice. The action is treated as if it was filed in the transferee court on the date upon which it was filed in the transferor court. EXAM NOTE: Remember that under the federal rules, a transfer only occurs between federal courts.  (A state may also have its own rules allowing transfer cases among its courts.)  A removal moves a case from a state court to a federal district court, and a remand moves a removed case back to federal district court. Durfee v. Duke, 375 U.S. 106 (1963) R.R. Comm’n of Texas v. Pullman Co., 312 U.S. 496 (1941). Burford v. Sun Oil Co., 319 U.S. 315 (1943); Louisiana Power & Light Co. v. City of Thibodaux, 360 U.S. 25 (1959). Younger v. Harris, 401 U.S. 37 (1971). Colorado River Water Conservation Dist. v. United States, 424 U.S. 800 (1976). 28 U.S.C. § 1631.

rules.rule_statement: Rule Article III, Section 2 of the U.S. Constitution provides that federal judicial power shall extend to all cases “arising under this Constitution, the Laws of the United States, and Treaties made, or which shall be made, under their Authority.” Today, the general congressional grant of federal question jurisdiction is also codified by statute under § 1331. Exam Note Unlike federal diversity jurisdiction, federal question jurisdiction does not require diversity between the parties or impose an amount-in-controversy requirement.
rules.short_rule_statement: Rule Article III, Section 2 of the U.S. Constitution provides that federal judicial power shall extend to all cases “arising under this Constitution, the Laws of the United...
rules.source_text:
Federal Question Jurisdiction
Rule Article III, Section 2 of the U.S. Constitution provides that federal judicial power shall extend to all cases “arising under this Constitution, the Laws of the United States, and Treaties made, or which shall be made, under their Authority.” Today, the general congressional grant of federal question jurisdiction is also codified by statute under § 1331. Exam Note Unlike federal diversity jurisdiction, federal question jurisdiction does not require diversity between the parties or impose an amount-in-controversy requirement.

Concurrent Versus Exclusive Jurisdiction
State and federal courts have concurrent jurisdiction of federal question claims, except when Congress expressly provides that the federal courts have exclusive jurisdiction. and bankruptcy proceedings.

rules.rule_statement: No uniform, bright-line standard exists for determining whether an action arises under federal law. For the purposes of § 1331, federal law includes the U.S. Constitution, federal statutes, federal administrative regulations, and U.S. treaties. State laws incorporating standards of federal law are not considered laws of the United States under § 1331.

Scope
No uniform, bright-line standard exists for determining whether an action arises under federal law. For the purposes of § 1331, federal law includes the U.S. Constitution, federal statutes, federal administrative regulations, and U.S. treaties. State laws incorporating standards of federal law are not considered laws of the United States under § 1331.

rules.rule_statement: ii) Actually disputed; iii) Substantial; and iv) Capable of resolution in a federal court without disrupting the federal-state balance approved by Congress. Merrell Dow held that when a substantive federal statute (e.g., the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act) does not provide a federal remedy, then an alleged violation of that statute as an element of a state-law complaint does not automatically establish § 1331 jurisdiction. Instead, federal courts have discretion to determine whether a federal issue or interest is important enough in a specific case to justify the exercise of federal jurisdiction.

rules.rule_statement: The determination of jurisdiction must be made by considering only the necessary elements of the plaintiff’s cause of action. This requirement is known as the well-pleaded complaint rule. Jurisdiction over a federal question cannot be based solely on an anticipated defense. Answers and counterclaims cannot be considered when determining the existence of federal question jurisdiction.

What May Be Considered
The determination of jurisdiction must be made by considering only the necessary elements of the plaintiff’s cause of action. This requirement is known as the well-pleaded complaint rule. Jurisdiction over a federal question cannot be based solely on an anticipated defense. Answers and counterclaims cannot be considered when determining the existence of federal question jurisdiction.

rules.rule_statement: Actions for declaratory relief anticipate some future action or infringement of rights and seek a declaratory judgment to resolve uncertainty and to avoid the possibility of a future lawsuit. Most states have enacted statutes authorizing their courts to issue declaratory judgments. In an action for declaratory relief in which the complaint asserts a defense to an anticipated future action or infringement of rights, the character of the threatened action, and not of the defense, determines whether there is federal question jurisdiction.

Most states have enacted statutes authorizing their courts to issue declaratory judgments. In an action for declaratory relief in which the complaint asserts a defense to an anticipated future action or infringement of rights, the character of the threatened action, and not of the defense, determines whether there is federal question jurisdiction.

rules.rule_statement: Diversity jurisdiction requires complete diversity between opposing parties in a case. There is no diversity of citizenship if any plaintiff is a citizen of the same state as any defendant. Two or more plaintiffs may be from the same state without destroying diversity so long as no plaintiff is from the same state as any defendant. Similarly, two or more defendants may be from the same state without destroying diversity if no defendant is from the same state as any plaintiff. § 1332(a)(1). The term “state” includes the District of Columbia, Puerto Rico, and the U.S. territories. § 1332(e).
rules.short_rule_statement: Diversity jurisdiction requires complete diversity between opposing parties in a case. There is no diversity of citizenship if any plaintiff is a citizen of the same state as...
rules.source_text:
Rule Of Complete Diversity
Diversity jurisdiction requires complete diversity between opposing parties in a case. There is no diversity of citizenship if any plaintiff is a citizen of the same state as any defendant. Two or more plaintiffs may be from the same state without destroying diversity so long as no plaintiff is from the same state as any defendant. Similarly, two or more defendants may be from the same state without destroying diversity if no defendant is from the same state as any plaintiff. § 1332(a)(1). The term “state” includes the District of Columbia, Puerto Rico, and the U.S. territories. § 1332(e).

rules.rule_statement: In limited circumstances authorized by Congress, minimal diversity is sufficient to meet the diversity requirement of Article III, Section 2 of the U.S. Constitution. Minimal diversity exists if at least one plaintiff and one defendant are citizens of different states, regardless of the citizenship of other claimants. Under the Federal Interpleader Act, which allows a holder of property that is claimed by two or more persons to deposit the property with a court to determine ownership, minimal diversity is sufficient to establish federal jurisdiction. EXAM NOTE: Remember that only federal statutory interpleader permits minimal diversity.  Under federal rule interpleader, the plaintiff must establish the full requirements for federal question jurisdiction or diversity jurisdiction.  See § VI.C.1. Federal Interpleader Rule, infra. For certain class actions in which the amount at issue exceeds $5,000,000, diversity of citizenship is satisfied if minimal diversity exists between any member of the plaintiff class and any defendant. § 1332(d)(2)(A). For a civil action that “arises from a single accident, where at least 75 natural persons have died in the accident at a discrete location,” the jurisdiction rules in § 1369(a) apply. In such a multiparty action, minimal diversity is sufficient for a federal court to have diversity jurisdiction under § 1369(a) if: i) A defendant resides in a state and a substantial part of the accident took place in another state or other location, regardless of whether that defendant is also a resident of the state where a substantial part of the accident took place; ii) Any two defendants reside in different states, regardless of whether the defendants are also residents of the same state or states; or iii) Substantial parts of the accident took place in different states. Even if the above requirements are met, a district court must abstain from hearing § 1369(b) when: i) The substantial majority of all plaintiffs are citizens of a single state and the primary defendants are also citizens of that state; and ii) The asserted claims will be governed primarily by the laws of that state. The FIA also provides that:
rules.short_rule_statement: In limited circumstances authorized by Congress, minimal diversity is sufficient to meet the diversity requirement of Article III, Section 2 of the U.S. Constitution. Minimal...
rules.source_text:
Exceptions Requiring Minimal Diversity
In limited circumstances authorized by Congress, minimal diversity is sufficient to meet the diversity requirement of Article III, Section 2 of the U.S. Constitution. Minimal diversity exists if at least one plaintiff and one defendant are citizens of different states, regardless of the citizenship of other claimants. Under the Federal Interpleader Act, which allows a holder of property that is claimed by two or more persons to deposit the property with a court to determine ownership, minimal diversity is sufficient to establish federal jurisdiction. EXAM NOTE: Remember that only federal statutory interpleader permits minimal diversity.  Under federal rule interpleader, the plaintiff must establish the full requirements for federal question jurisdiction or diversity jurisdiction.  See § VI.C.1. Federal Interpleader Rule, infra. For certain class actions in which the amount at issue exceeds $5,000,000, diversity of citizenship is satisfied if minim

rules.rule_statement: In evaluating whether true diversity exists, courts will look beyond the face of the pleadings to determine the “ultimate interests.” If necessary, courts will “arrange the parties according to their sides in the dispute.” Example: A stockholder from State A files a derivative action in federal court against several State B defendants on behalf of a State B corporation. The complaint also names the State B corporation as a defendant. If the court determines that the corporation is the real party in interest who stands to benefit from success in the suit, the court will realign the corporation as a plaintiff in a stockholder’s derivative action.

“Realignment”
In evaluating whether true diversity exists, courts will look beyond the face of the pleadings to determine the “ultimate interests.” If necessary, courts will “arrange the parties according to their sides in the dispute.” Example: A stockholder from State A files a derivative action in federal court against several State B defendants on behalf of a State B corporation. The complaint also names the State B corporation as a defendant. If the court determines that the corporation is the real party in interest who stands to benefit from success in the suit, the court will realign the corporation as a plaintiff in a stockholder’s derivative action.

rules.rule_statement: Domicile is the state or country where (1) a person resides and intends to remain indefinitely or (2) a corporation was incorporated or maintains its principal place of business.

Citizenship Of Parties
Domicile is the state or country where (1) a person resides and intends to remain indefinitely or (2) a corporation was incorporated or maintains its principal place of business.

rules.rule_statement: To be a citizen of a state for the purposes of § 1332, an individual must be a citizen of the United States and a domiciliary of the state. In general, an individual is a domiciliary of the state in which the individual is present and intends to reside for an indefinite period.

Individuals
To be a citizen of a state for the purposes of § 1332, an individual must be a citizen of the United States and a domiciliary of the state. In general, an individual is a domiciliary of the state in which the individual is present and intends to reside for an indefinite period.

rules.rule_statement: Diversity jurisdiction based on citizenship status generally exists if there are one or more citizens or subjects of a foreign country (i.e., noncitizens) on one side of the lawsuit and one or more citizens of a state on the other. § 1332(a)(2).

Noncitizens Versus State Citizens
Diversity jurisdiction based on citizenship status generally exists if there are one or more citizens or subjects of a foreign country (i.e., noncitizens) on one side of the lawsuit and one or more citizens of a state on the other. § 1332(a)(2).

rules.rule_statement: There is no diversity jurisdiction if the only parties are noncitizens, even if the noncitizens are citizens of different countries. Additionally, there is no diversity jurisdiction if there are noncitizens on both sides of the lawsuit and one or more citizens of a state on only one side.

rules.source_text:
Noncitizens V. Noncitizens
There is no diversity jurisdiction if the only parties are noncitizens, even if the noncitizens are citizens of different countries. Additionally, there is no diversity jurisdiction if there are noncitizens on both sides of the lawsuit and one or more citizens of a state on only one side.

rules.rule_statement: When an action is between citizens of different states, noncitizens may generally be additional parties to the action as plaintiffs, defendants, or both. The noncitizens need not be from different countries from one another to preserve diversity. § 1332(a)(3).

Noncitizens As Additional Parties
When an action is between citizens of different states, noncitizens may generally be additional parties to the action as plaintiffs, defendants, or both. The noncitizens need not be from different countries from one another to preserve diversity. § 1332(a)(3).

rules.rule_statement: Diversity jurisdiction does not exist when an action is between a citizen of a state and a noncitizen admitted to the United States for permanent residence who is domiciled in the same state as the citizen. § 1332(a). Diversity jurisdiction does not exist over: ii) A U.S. citizen whose domicile is a foreign country. For diversity jurisdiction, such persons are considered stateless, meaning they are neither citizens of the United States nor citizens or subjects of a foreign country. The legal representative of a minor, a person who is incompetent, or an estate of a decedent is deemed a citizen of the same state as that minor, incompetent person, or decedent. § 1332(c)(2). An unemancipated minor is generally deemed a citizen of the state in which the child’s parents are domiciled. If a minor’s parents are not citizens of the same state, then the child will most likely be treated as a citizen of the state of the parent who has custody. Traditionally, a trust was treated as a citizen of the same state as the trustee. However, this rule has been placed in doubt by Carden v. Arkoma Assocs., 494 U.S. 185 (1990), which held that the citizenship of unincorporated associations is determined by the citizenship of all its members. As a general rule, diversity in a class action brought under Rule 23 is determined by the citizenship of the named members of the class bringing the lawsuit. See § VI.E. Class Actions, infra, for a discussion of class actions under Rule 23. However, minimum diversity is sufficient for certain class actions when the amount at issue exceeds $5,000,000. § 1332(d)(2)(A). See § 5.c. Aggregation of Claims, below.

Noncitizens Who Are Permanent Residents

For diversity jurisdiction, such persons are considered stateless, meaning they are neither citizens of the United States nor citizens or subjects of a foreign country. The legal representative of a minor, a person who is incompetent, or an estate of a decedent is deemed a citizen of the same state as that minor, incompetent person, or decedent. § 1332(c)(2). An unemancipated minor is generally deemed a citizen of the state in which the child’s parents are domiciled. rules.canonical_name: State Of Incorporation – Every State And Foreign State Where The Company Is...
rules.rule_statement: ii) State of principal place of business – The state or foreign state where the company has its principal place of business. § 1332(c). Diversity jurisdiction will not exist if any opposing party is a citizen of any of the states of which the corporation is a citizen. A pleader does not have the option of alleging that a corporation’s citizenship is either its state of incorporation or the state where the principal place of business is located. Both states must be listed in the pleading.

rules.rule_statement: A corporation’s state of incorporation is the state in which the corporate entity is legally established. Every corporation has at least one state of incorporation. If a corporation is incorporated in multiple places, then it is considered a citizen of each state and foreign country in which it is incorporated.
rules.short_rule_statement: A corporation’s state of incorporation is the state in which the corporate entity is legally established. Every corporation has at least one state of incorporation. If a...
rules.source_text:
Incorporation
A corporation’s state of incorporation is the state in which the corporate entity is legally established. Every corporation has at least one state of incorporation. If a corporation is incorporated in multiple places, then it is considered a citizen of each state and foreign country in which it is incorporated.

rules.rule_statement: Determining a corporation’s principal place of business is a question of fact more complicated than identifying a company’s state of incorporation. The Supreme Court has held that the “principal place of business” refers to the “nerve center” of the corporation. A corporation’s nerve center is generally the location where the high-level officers direct, control, and coordinate the activities of the corporation. Typically, the nerve center is the corporate headquarters. EXAM NOTE: Although a company’s place of incorporation may involve multiple states or foreign countries, a company’s principal place of business is in only one state or foreign country.
rules.short_rule_statement: Determining a corporation’s principal place of business is a question of fact more complicated than identifying a company’s state of incorporation. The Supreme Court has held...
rules.source_text:
Principal Place Of Business
Determining a corporation’s principal place of business is a question of fact more complicated than identifying a company’s state of incorporation. The Supreme Court has held that the “principal place of business” refers to the “nerve center” of the corporation. A corporation’s nerve center is generally the location where the high-level officers direct, control, and coordinate the activities of the corporation. Typically, the nerve center is the corporate headquarters. EXAM NOTE: Although a company’s place of incorporation may involve multiple states or foreign countries, a company’s principal place of business is in only one state or foreign country.

rules.rule_statement: A foreign corporation is deemed a citizen of the country in which it is incorporated. If the foreign corporation also has its principal place of business in the United States, then it will also have citizenship in the state where the principal place of business is located.

rules.rule_statement: A special rule applies when a plaintiff allegedly injured by an insured party brings a direct action against the liability insurer. The insurer is deemed a citizen of the state or foreign country in which its insured is a citizen, in addition to any other states or foreign countries where the insurer is a citizen. § 1332(c).

Liability Insurers
A special rule applies when a plaintiff allegedly injured by an insured party brings a direct action against the liability insurer. The insurer is deemed a citizen of the state or foreign country in which its insured is a citizen, in addition to any other states or foreign countries where the insurer is a citizen. § 1332(c).

rules.rule_statement: An unincorporated association (e.g., partnership) is a citizen of each state in which each of its members is domiciled. This rule holds for both general and limited partnerships. A limited partnership is a citizen of every state in which its general and limited partners are domiciled.

An unincorporated association (e.g., partnership) is a citizen of each state in which each of its members is domiciled. This rule holds for both general and limited partnerships. A limited partnership is a citizen of every state in which its general and limited partners are domiciled.

rules.rule_statement: As with partnerships, an LLC is considered a citizen of each state in which each of its members is domiciled.

As with partnerships, an LLC is considered a citizen of each state in which each of its members is domiciled.

rules.rule_statement: An unincorporated association may be treated as a class when “it appears that the representative parties will fairly and adequately protect the interests of the association and its members.” In that case, the citizenship of the representative parties controls for diversity jurisdiction. Rule 23.2. This exception may not be used merely to create diversity jurisdiction, nor may it be used if the association has the capacity under state law to sue or be sued as an entity.

Exception
An unincorporated association may be treated as a class when “it appears that the representative parties will fairly and adequately protect the interests of the association and its members.” In that case, the citizenship of the representative parties controls for diversity jurisdiction. Rule 23.2. This exception may not be used merely to create diversity jurisdiction, nor may it be used if the association has the capacity under state law to sue or be sued as an entity.

rules.rule_statement: Rule For a court to exercise diversity jurisdiction, the amount in controversy must exceed $75,000, exclusive of interest, costs, and collateral effects of a judgment. § 1332(a).
rules.short_rule_statement: Rule For a court to exercise diversity jurisdiction, the amount in controversy must exceed $75,000, exclusive of interest, costs, and collateral effects of a judgment. § 1332(a).

Amount In Controversy
Rule For a court to exercise diversity jurisdiction, the amount in controversy must exceed $75,000, exclusive of interest, costs, and collateral effects of a judgment. § 1332(a).

rules.rule_statement: Although interests and costs are excluded from the amount in controversy, attorney’s fees recoverable by contract or statute may be made part of the amount in controversy. Punitive damages may also be included in the amount in controversy. EXAM NOTE: Remember that the amount in controversy must exceed $75,000.  A claim for exactly $75,000 fails. The amount in controversy may be difficult to calculate in a claim seeking injunctive relief. In these cases, some courts consider only the value of the injunction to the plaintiff. Other courts determine the amount in controversy by considering the greater of:

The amount in controversy may be difficult to calculate in a claim seeking injunctive relief. In these cases, some courts consider only the value of the injunction to the plaintiff. rules.rule_statement: The party seeking to invoke federal court jurisdiction must allege that the action satisfies the amount-in-controversy requirement. In general, a plaintiff’s good-faith assertion in the complaint that the action satisfies the amount-in-controversy requirement is sufficient unless it appears to be a legal certainty that the plaintiff cannot recover the amount alleged. If an alleged amount in controversy is challenged, then the burden is on the party asserting jurisdiction merely to show that it is not a legal certainty that the claim involves less than the statutory amount. If events occur after the action has been filed that reduce the amount in controversy below the statutory minimum, then jurisdiction will not be lost as long as the original claim was made in good faith. If the plaintiff eventually recovers an amount that is less than the statutory jurisdictional amount, then that fact will not render the verdict subject to challenge on appeal for lack of jurisdiction.

rules.rule_statement: Aggregation of claims is the combining the values of several claims to determine whether the amount in controversy exceeds the threshold for diversity jurisdiction. Claims Amount in controversy Against one Aggregate value of all plaintiff’s claims defendant One plaintiff Against multiple Judge each claim separately unless defendants defendants are jointly liable Enforcing single Aggregate value of all plaintiffs’ claims title or right Multiple plaintiffs Separate and Judge each plaintiff’s claim separately distinct claims Note that a plaintiff's aggregated claims do not need to be related for the federal court to exercise diversity jurisdiction. However, the court has discretion under Rule 42 to order separate trials of the unrelated claims for convenience, to avoid prejudice, or to expedite and economize.

Aggregation Of Claims In General
Aggregation of claims is the combining the values of several claims to determine whether the amount in controversy exceeds the threshold for diversity jurisdiction. Claims Amount in controversy Against one Aggregate value of all plaintiff’s claims defendant One plaintiff Against multiple Judge each claim separately unless defendants defendants are jointly liable Enforcing single Aggregate value of all plaintiffs’ claims title or right Multiple plaintiffs Separate and Judge each plaintiff’s claim separately distinct claims Note that a plaintiff's aggregated claims do not need to be related for the federal court to exercise diversity jurisdiction. However, the court has discretion under Rule 42 to order separate trials of the unrelated claims for convenience, to avoid prejudice, or to expedite and economize.

rules.canonical_name: Aggregation Of Claims In Class Actions
rules.rule_statement: The Class Action Fairness Act of 2005 amended § 1332(d) to permit aggregation of claims in certain class actions. See § VI.E.1.e., Class Actions Under the Class Action Fairness Act of 2005, infra. Before 2005, generally, if any member of the putative class did not have a claim that met the statutory jurisdictional amount, then the amount-in- controversy requirement was not met. However, after the court’s decision in Exxon Mobil Corp. v. Allapattah Servs., Inc., when at least one representative plaintiff in a putative class has a claim that meets the statutory jurisdictional amount, other persons with claims that do not meet the jurisdictional amount can be made part of the class under the doctrine of supplemental jurisdiction. See § I.D. Supplemental Jurisdiction, infra.

Aggregation Of Claims In Class Actions
The Class Action Fairness Act of 2005 amended § 1332(d) to permit aggregation of claims in certain class actions. See § VI.E.1.e., Class Actions Under the Class Action Fairness Act of 2005, infra. Before 2005, generally, if any member of the putative class did not have a claim that met the statutory jurisdictional amount, then the amount-in- controversy requirement was not met. However, after the court’s decision in Exxon Mobil Corp. v. Allapattah Servs., Inc., when at least one representative plaintiff in a putative class has a claim that meets the statutory jurisdictional amount, other persons with claims that do not meet the jurisdictional amount can be made part of the class under the doctrine of supplemental jurisdiction. See § I.D. Supplemental Jurisdiction, infra.

rules.rule_statement: A counterclaim is a claim for relief asserted in opposition to an opposing party’s earlier claim. There are two types of counterclaims: Compulsory counterclaim Permissive counterclaim A counterclaim by a defendant against a plaintiff is not counted when determining whether the plaintiff has met the statutory jurisdictional amount. Under some circumstances, the counterclaim itself will need to meet the statutory jurisdictional amount. A compulsory counterclaim is a claim arising out of the same transaction or occurrence as the plaintiff’s claim. A compulsory counterclaim need not meet a statutory jurisdictional amount because the court will have supplemental jurisdiction over all compulsory counterclaims. See Rule 13(a); see § I.D. Supplemental Jurisdiction, infra. To determine whether counterclaims or crossclaims arise out of the same “transaction or occurrence,” courts consider whether:
rules.short_rule_statement: A counterclaim is a claim for relief asserted in opposition to an opposing party’s earlier claim. There are two types of counterclaims: Compulsory counterclaim Permissive...
rules.source_text:
Counterclaims
A counterclaim is a claim for relief asserted in opposition to an opposing party’s earlier claim. There are two types of counterclaims: Compulsory counterclaim Permissive counterclaim A counterclaim by a defendant against a plaintiff is not counted when determining whether the plaintiff has met the statutory jurisdictional amount. Under some circumstances, the counterclaim itself will need to meet the statutory jurisdictional amount. A compulsory counterclaim is a claim arising out of the same transaction or occurrence as the plaintiff’s claim. A compulsory counterclaim need not meet a statutory jurisdictional amount because the court will have supplemental jurisdiction over all compulsory counterclaims. See Rule 13(a); see § I.D. Supplemental Jurisdiction, infra. To determine whether counterclaims or crossclaims arise out of the same “transaction or occurrence,” courts consider whether:

rules.canonical_name: The Issues Of Fact And Law In The Claims Are Essentially The Same
rules.rule_statement: ii) The same evidence would support or refute the claims; iii) There is a logical relationship between the claims; and iv) Res judicata would bar a subsequent suit on either claim. The most frequently considered factor is whether there is a logical relationship between the claims. However, any one of these factors can support a conclusion that the claims arise from the same “transaction or occurrence.” A permissive counterclaim is a claim arising out of a transaction that is unrelated to the plaintiff’s claim. A permissive counterclaim must meet the statutory jurisdictional amount requirement. See Rule 13(b).
rules.short_rule_statement: ii) The same evidence would support or refute the claims; iii) There is a logical relationship between the claims; and iv) Res judicata would bar a subsequent suit on either...
rules.source_text:
The Issues Of Fact And Law In The Claims Are Essentially The Same
ii) The same evidence would support or refute the claims; iii) There is a logical relationship between the claims; and iv) Res judicata would bar a subsequent suit on either claim. The most frequently considered factor is whether there is a logical relationship between the claims. However, any one of these factors can support a conclusion that the claims arise from the same “transaction or occurrence.” A permissive counterclaim is a claim arising out of a transaction that is unrelated to the plaintiff’s claim. A permissive counterclaim must meet the statutory jurisdictional amount requirement. See Rule 13(b).

rules.rule_statement: A plaintiff is never allowed to remove a case to federal court. Removal is allowed only by defendants. See § I.E. Removal Jurisdiction, infra.
rules.short_rule_statement: A plaintiff is never allowed to remove a case to federal court. Removal is allowed only by defendants. See § I.E. Removal Jurisdiction, infra.
rules.source_text:
Plaintiff
A plaintiff is never allowed to remove a case to federal court. Removal is allowed only by defendants. See § I.E. Removal Jurisdiction, infra.

rules.rule_statement: A defendant is not permitted to remove a case to federal court if: i) A diverse defendant makes a permissive counterclaim under state law for more than the federal statutory jurisdictional amount; and ii) The plaintiff’s claim is for less than the federal statutory jurisdictional amount.
rules.short_rule_statement: A defendant is not permitted to remove a case to federal court if: i) A diverse defendant makes a permissive counterclaim under state law for more than the federal statutory...
rules.source_text:
Defendant—Permissive Counterclaim
A defendant is not permitted to remove a case to federal court if: i) A diverse defendant makes a permissive counterclaim under state law for more than the federal statutory jurisdictional amount; and ii) The plaintiff’s claim is for less than the federal statutory jurisdictional amount.

rules.rule_statement: When a diverse defendant makes a compulsory counterclaim under state law for more than the federal statutory jurisdictional amount, and the plaintiff’s claim is for less than the federal statutory jurisdictional amount, courts are split on allowing removal by the defendant. Most courts do not allow the defendant to remove the case to federal court.
rules.short_rule_statement: When a diverse defendant makes a compulsory counterclaim under state law for more than the federal statutory jurisdictional amount, and the plaintiff’s claim is for less than...
rules.source_text:
Defendant—Compulsory Counterclaim
When a diverse defendant makes a compulsory counterclaim under state law for more than the federal statutory jurisdictional amount, and the plaintiff’s claim is for less than the federal statutory jurisdictional amount, courts are split on allowing removal by the defendant. Most courts do not allow the defendant to remove the case to federal court.

rules.canonical_name: Devices To Create Or Destroy Diversity
rules.rule_statement: Federal jurisdiction is prohibited if a party uses improper or collusive devices to invoke such jurisdiction. § 1359.
rules.short_rule_statement: Federal jurisdiction is prohibited if a party uses improper or collusive devices to invoke such jurisdiction. § 1359.
rules.source_text:
Devices To Create Or Destroy Diversity
Federal jurisdiction is prohibited if a party uses improper or collusive devices to invoke such jurisdiction. § 1359.
topic_path: Devices To Create Or Destroy Diversity

rules.rule_statement: When there is a legitimate assignment of a claim, the assignee becomes the real party in interest. The assignee’s citizenship, as opposed to the assignor’s citizenship, will be determinative. Example: When an insurance company pays an insured party for damages caused by a third party and then sues the third party in a subrogation action, the insurance company’s citizenship controls for diversity purposes. The insured party’s citizenship is not relevant. However, if the assignment is done collusively to manufacture or create diversity jurisdiction, diversity jurisdiction does not exist.
rules.short_rule_statement: When there is a legitimate assignment of a claim, the assignee becomes the real party in interest. The assignee’s citizenship, as opposed to the assignor’s citizenship, will be...
rules.source_text:
Assignment Of Claims
When there is a legitimate assignment of a claim, the assignee becomes the real party in interest. The assignee’s citizenship, as opposed to the assignor’s citizenship, will be determinative. Example: When an insurance company pays an insured party for damages caused by a third party and then sues the third party in a subrogation action, the insurance company’s citizenship controls for diversity purposes. The insured party’s citizenship is not relevant. However, if the assignment is done collusively to manufacture or create diversity jurisdiction, diversity jurisdiction does not exist.

rules.rule_statement: The parties may not manufacture diversity jurisdiction by failing to join a nondiverse indispensable party. See § VI.A.2. Compulsory Joinder, infra, for the standards under Rule 19.
rules.short_rule_statement: The parties may not manufacture diversity jurisdiction by failing to join a nondiverse indispensable party. See § VI.A.2. Compulsory Joinder, infra, for the standards under...
rules.source_text:
Failure To Name Indispensable Parties
The parties may not manufacture diversity jurisdiction by failing to join a nondiverse indispensable party. See § VI.A.2. Compulsory Joinder, infra, for the standards under Rule 19.

rules.rule_statement: A party may establish or defeat diversity jurisdiction by voluntarily changing state citizenship after the cause of action arises but before the lawsuit begins. A party’s motive for changing citizenship is irrelevant, but the change of state citizenship must be genuine to be recognized. To determine whether the change is genuine, courts may consider whether the party changed domicile specifically to create or destroy diversity. However, as long as the party intends to stay in the new state indefinitely, the party’s motive will not invalidate the change in domicile. Example 1: A pedestrian from State A was struck by a driver from State A. The pedestrian had already accepted a job offer in State B, and knew that in a month, she would be moving to State B permanently. Hoping to decide the case in federal rather than state court, the pedestrian waited until after she moved to State B before filing a federal diversity claim against the driver. The pedestrian’s change of state citizenship properly established diversity jurisdiction. Example 2: Assumer the same facts as Example 1, but the pedestrian did not decide to move to State B until after the accident. Even if the pedestrian changed domicile specifically to create diversity, as long as she intends to stay in State B indefinitely, her motive will not invalidate the change in domicile or destroy diversity jurisdiction.

rules.source_text:
Voluntary Change Of State Citizenship
A party may establish or defeat diversity jurisdiction by voluntarily changing state citizenship after the cause of action arises but before the lawsuit begins. A party’s motive for changing citizenship is irrelevant, but the change of state citizenship must be genuine to be recognized. To determine whether the change is genuine, courts may consider whether the party changed domicile specifically to create or destroy diversity. However, as long as the party intends to stay in the new state indefinitely, the party’s motive will not invalidate the change in domicile. Example 1: A pedestrian from State A was struck by a driver from State A. The pedestrian had already accepted a job offer in State B, and knew that in a month, she would be moving to State B permanently. Hoping to decide the case in federal rather than state court, the pedestrian waited until after she moved to State B before filing a federal diversity claim against the driver. The pedes

rules.rule_statement: Sometimes there is a need to change parties to a lawsuit. Depending on the circumstances, two processes are in place to do so: i) Substitution – Rule 25 allows for the substitution of a party because of death or incompetence. The newly substituted party takes the place of the original party and does not have to satisfy the diversity requirement. ii) Replacement – When a party must be replaced, such as when the wrong party is named in the complaint, the original party is removed and the new replacement party must satisfy the diversity requirement. Notice the subtle distinction between substitution and replacement. Substitution calls for the substituted party to step into the shoes of the original party, whereas replacement removes one party to the lawsuit for another party. Strawbridge v. Curtiss, 7 U.S. (3 Cranch) 267 (1806). State Farm Fire & Cas. Co. v. Tashire, 386 U.S. 523 (1967). 28 U.S.C. § 1335. City of Dawson v. Columbia Ave. Sav. Fund, Safe Deposit, Title, & Trust Co., 197 U.S. 178 (1905). See, e.g., Koster v. (Am.) Lumbermens Mut. Cas. Co., 330 U.S. 518 (1947); Liddy v. Urbanek, 707 F.2d 1222 (11th Cir. 1983). Janzen v. Goos, 302 F.2d 421 (8th Cir. 1962). Janzen v. Goos, 302 F.2d 421 (8th Cir. 1962). Ed & Fred, Inc. v. Puritan Marine Ins. Underwriters Corp., 506 F.2d 757 (5th Cir. 1975). See Navarro Savings Ass’n v. Lee, 446 U.S. 458 (1980). Hertz Corp. v. Friend, 559 U.S. 77 (2010). Carden v. Arkoma Assocs., 494 U.S. 185 (1990). Americold Realty Trust v. Conagra Foods, Inc., 577 U.S. 378 (2016). St. Paul Mercury Indem. Co. v. Red Cab Co., 303 U.S. 283 (1938). Snyder v. Harris, 394 U.S. 332 (1969); Zahn v. International Paper, 414 U.S. 291 (1973). 545 U.S. 546 (2005). See, e.g., Kramer v. Caribbean Mills, Inc., 394 U.S. 823 (1969) (assignment to a nominal party having no real interest in the claim was collusive). Compare Williamson v. Osenton, 232 U.S. 619, 625 (1914) (finding that a wife separated from her husband could establish her own domicile in a new state and maintain an action based on diversity jurisdiction) with Morris v. Gilmer, 129 U.S. 315, 328–29 (1889) (finding that the plaintiff did not change his domicile because he moved there only to create diversity and did not intend to remain in the new state indefinitely). See, e.g., Gordon v. Steele, 376 F. Supp. 575 (W.D. Pa. 1974). Morris v. Gilmer, 129 U.S. 315, 328–29 (1889) (finding that the plaintiff did not change his domicile because he moved there only to create diversity and did not intend to remain in the new state indefinitely).

rules.source_text:
Substitution Versus Replacement Of Parties
Sometimes there is a need to change parties to a lawsuit. Depending on the circumstances, two processes are in place to do so: i) Substitution – Rule 25 allows for the substitution of a party because of death or incompetence. The newly substituted party takes the place of the original party and does not have to satisfy the diversity requirement. ii) Replacement – When a party must be replaced, such as when the wrong party is named in the complaint, the original party is removed and the new replacement party must satisfy the diversity requirement. Notice the subtle distinction between substitution and replacement. Substitution calls for the substituted party to step into the shoes of the original party, whereas replacement removes one party to the lawsuit for another party. Strawbridge v. Curtiss, 7 U.S. (3 Cranch) 267 (1806). State Farm Fire & Cas. Co. v. Tashire, 386 U.S. 523 (1967). 28 U.S.C. § 1335. City of Dawson v. Columbia Ave. Sav. Fund

rules.rule_statement: Rule A district court with jurisdiction over a claim may exercise “supplemental jurisdiction” over additional claims in certain circumstances even though the court would not independently have subject matter jurisdiction. In these cases, the additional claims must be so related to the original claim that the additional claims form part of the same case or controversy under Article III of the U.S. Constitution. § 1367(a).
rules.short_rule_statement: Rule A district court with jurisdiction over a claim may exercise “supplemental jurisdiction” over additional claims in certain circumstances even though the court would not...
rules.source_text:
Supplemental Jurisdiction
Rule A district court with jurisdiction over a claim may exercise “supplemental jurisdiction” over additional claims in certain circumstances even though the court would not independently have subject matter jurisdiction. In these cases, the additional claims must be so related to the original claim that the additional claims form part of the same case or controversy under Article III of the U.S. Constitution. § 1367(a).

rules.rule_statement: In judging whether the claims are related for § 1367(a), the test is whether they arise out of a common nucleus of operative fact such that all claims should be tried together in a single judicial proceeding.
rules.short_rule_statement: In judging whether the claims are related for § 1367(a), the test is whether they arise out of a common nucleus of operative fact such that all claims should be tried together...
rules.source_text:
In General
In judging whether the claims are related for § 1367(a), the test is whether they arise out of a common nucleus of operative fact such that all claims should be tried together in a single judicial proceeding.

rules.rule_statement: When the district court’s subject matter jurisdiction for a claim is based on the existence of a federal question, the court can exercise supplemental jurisdiction over additional claims against the same party if the common-nucleus-of-operative-fact test is met. Example: Plaintiff sues her corporate employer in federal court under Title VII for sex discrimination and retaliation, matters over which the court has federal question jurisdiction. Plaintiff then seeks to sue her corporate employer for assault and battery under state law. Plaintiff’s state claim arises under the same operative facts as the Title VII claim. The federal court has discretion to exercise supplemental jurisdiction over the assault and battery claim.
rules.short_rule_statement: When the district court’s subject matter jurisdiction for a claim is based on the existence of a federal question, the court can exercise supplemental jurisdiction over...
rules.source_text:
Additional Claims
When the district court’s subject matter jurisdiction for a claim is based on the existence of a federal question, the court can exercise supplemental jurisdiction over additional claims against the same party if the common-nucleus-of-operative-fact test is met. Example: Plaintiff sues her corporate employer in federal court under Title VII for sex discrimination and retaliation, matters over which the court has federal question jurisdiction. Plaintiff then seeks to sue her corporate employer for assault and battery under state law. Plaintiff’s state claim arises under the same operative facts as the Title VII claim. The federal court has discretion to exercise supplemental jurisdiction over the assault and battery claim.

rules.rule_statement: A district court can exercise supplemental jurisdiction over claims that involve the joinder or intervention of additional parties if the claims satisfy the common-nucleus-of-operative-fact test. Such jurisdiction is also referred to as pendent-party jurisdiction. Example: Plaintiff sues her corporate employer (Defendant 1) in federal court under Title VII for sex discrimination and retaliation, matters over which the court has federal question jurisdiction. Plaintiff then seeks to sue her direct supervisor (Defendant 2) for assault and battery. The federal court can exercise supplemental jurisdiction over the assault and battery claim against Defendant 2 if the claim arises under the same operative facts as the Title VII claim against Defendant 1. Dismissal of a federal claim on the merits does not preclude a federal court from exercising pendent-party jurisdiction over the state claim.
rules.short_rule_statement: A district court can exercise supplemental jurisdiction over claims that involve the joinder or intervention of additional parties if the claims satisfy the...
rules.source_text:
Additional Parties
A district court can exercise supplemental jurisdiction over claims that involve the joinder or intervention of additional parties if the claims satisfy the common-nucleus-of-operative-fact test. Such jurisdiction is also referred to as pendent-party jurisdiction. Example: Plaintiff sues her corporate employer (Defendant 1) in federal court under Title VII for sex discrimination and retaliation, matters over which the court has federal question jurisdiction. Plaintiff then seeks to sue her direct supervisor (Defendant 2) for assault and battery. The federal court can exercise supplemental jurisdiction over the assault and battery claim against Defendant 2 if the claim arises under the same operative facts as the Title VII claim against Defendant 1. Dismissal of a federal claim on the merits does not preclude a federal court from exercising pendent-party jurisdiction over the state claim.

rules.rule_statement: When a district court has diversity jurisdiction over a claim, the common-nucleus-of-operative-facts rule also applies to determine whether the court can exercise supplemental jurisdiction over an additional claim.
rules.short_rule_statement: When a district court has diversity jurisdiction over a claim, the common-nucleus-of-operative-facts rule also applies to determine whether the court can exercise supplemental...
rules.source_text:
Diversity Jurisdiction Cases
When a district court has diversity jurisdiction over a claim, the common-nucleus-of-operative-facts rule also applies to determine whether the court can exercise supplemental jurisdiction over an additional claim.

rules.rule_statement: When an additional claim is asserted by a plaintiff seeking to join the action under Rule 20 (permissive joinder), the addition of that party cannot violate the diversity-of-citizenship requirement. Example 1: A citizen of Pennsylvania (Plaintiff 1) brought a negligence action in federal court for $500,000 against a citizen of New York (Defendant 1) based on an automobile accident. A passenger in Plaintiff 1’s car (Plaintiff 2) sought to join the action by filing a negligence claim against Defendant for $30,000. Plaintiff 2 is a citizen of Pennsylvania. Diversity jurisdiction does not exist for Plaintiff 2’s claim because it does not satisfy the $75,000 amount-in-controversy requirement. However, the court can exercise supplemental jurisdiction over Plaintiff’s claim because it meets the common-nucleus-of-operative-facts test. Example 2: Assume the same facts as in Example 1, except that Plaintiff 2 is a citizen of New York. The federal court cannot exercise supplemental jurisdiction over Plaintiff 2’s claim because the presence of Plaintiff 2 defeats the requirement for complete diversity. EXAM NOTE: Remember that no party may be permissively joined to a case based exclusively on diversity jurisdiction if the joined party’s presence would defeat complete diversity.  However, the amount-in-controversy requirement only applies to claims by permissively joined plaintiffs against existing defendants, not claims by existing plaintiffs against permissively joined defendants.
rules.short_rule_statement: When an additional claim is asserted by a plaintiff seeking to join the action under Rule 20 (permissive joinder), the addition of that party cannot violate the...
rules.source_text:
Permissive Joinder
When an additional claim is asserted by a plaintiff seeking to join the action under Rule 20 (permissive joinder), the addition of that party cannot violate the diversity-of-citizenship requirement. Example 1: A citizen of Pennsylvania (Plaintiff 1) brought a negligence action in federal court for $500,000 against a citizen of New York (Defendant 1) based on an automobile accident. A passenger in Plaintiff 1’s car (Plaintiff 2) sought to join the action by filing a negligence claim against Defendant for $30,000. Plaintiff 2 is a citizen of Pennsylvania. Diversity jurisdiction does not exist for Plaintiff 2’s claim because it does not satisfy the $75,000 amount-in-controversy requirement. However, the court can exercise supplemental jurisdiction over Plaintiff’s claim because it meets the common-nucleus-of-operative-facts test. rules.rule_statement: A compulsory counterclaim may be asserted by a defendant against a plaintiff without satisfying the jurisdictional amount. A permissive counterclaim does not qualify for supplemental jurisdiction and therefore must satisfy the jurisdictional amount and the rule of complete diversity.
rules.short_rule_statement: A compulsory counterclaim may be asserted by a defendant against a plaintiff without satisfying the jurisdictional amount. A permissive counterclaim does not qualify for...
rules.source_text:
Counterclaims—Compulsory Versus Permissive
A compulsory counterclaim may be asserted by a defendant against a plaintiff without satisfying the jurisdictional amount. A permissive counterclaim does not qualify for supplemental jurisdiction and therefore must satisfy the jurisdictional amount and the rule of complete diversity.

rules.rule_statement: A crossclaim may be asserted by a defendant against another defendant or by a plaintiff against another plaintiff if the crossclaim arises out of the same transaction or occurrence as the initial claim. This rule applies regardless of the amount in controversy or the citizenship of the parties to the crossclaim so long as the court has subject matter jurisdiction over the initial claim.
rules.short_rule_statement: A crossclaim may be asserted by a defendant against another defendant or by a plaintiff against another plaintiff if the crossclaim arises out of the same transaction or...
rules.source_text:
Crossclaims
A crossclaim may be asserted by a defendant against another defendant or by a plaintiff against another plaintiff if the crossclaim arises out of the same transaction or occurrence as the initial claim. This rule applies regardless of the amount in controversy or the citizenship of the parties to the crossclaim so long as the court has subject matter jurisdiction over the initial claim.

rules.rule_statement: If the court’s original jurisdiction is based solely on diversity jurisdiction, § 1367(b) precludes the exercise of supplemental jurisdiction over certain claims. In these cases, supplemental jurisdiction will not apply to the following claims if they are inconsistent with the requirements for diversity jurisdiction:
rules.short_rule_statement: If the court’s original jurisdiction is based solely on diversity jurisdiction, § 1367(b) precludes the exercise of supplemental jurisdiction over certain claims. In these...
rules.source_text:
Precluded Claims In Diversity Cases
If the court’s original jurisdiction is based solely on diversity jurisdiction, § 1367(b) precludes the exercise of supplemental jurisdiction over certain claims. In these cases, supplemental jurisdiction will not apply to the following claims if they are inconsistent with the requirements for diversity jurisdiction:

rules.rule_statement: ii) Claims by plaintiffs added through compulsory joinder (Rule 19); and iii) Claims by intervening plaintiffs (Rule 24). Example 1: Plaintiff is a citizen of Iowa, and Defendant is a Nebraska corporation. Plaintiff brings suit in federal court under diversity jurisdiction for the wrongful death of Plaintiff’s husband. Under Rule 14, Defendant impleads Contractor, a citizen of Iowa, alleging that Contractor must indemnify Defendant against any liability to Plaintiff. Defendant’s claim against Contractor would fall within the federal court’s supplemental jurisdiction because Defendant’s claim is derived from the same operative facts of the initial claim subject to original jurisdiction. Plaintiff and Contractor are both citizens of the same state (Iowa), so supplemental jurisdiction in federal court will not apply to any claims brought by Plaintiff against Contractor. Plaintiff's claims against Contractor fail to meet the requirements for diversity jurisdiction under § 1367(b). Example 2: Plaintiff, a citizen of Arkansas, sues two Oklahoma citizens (Defendant 1 and Defendant 2) in federal court based on diversity jurisdiction, alleging negligence. Under Rule 13(g), Defendant 1 seeks to assert a crossclaim against Defendant 2 for negligence under the same operative facts as the original claim. Because Defendant 1 and Defendant 2 are citizens of the same state (Oklahoma), the federal court does not have original jurisdiction over the crossclaim. See § I.C.2. Complete Diversity, supra. The federal court could exercise supplemental jurisdiction over the crossclaim, as it is derived from the same operative facts as the initial claim subject to original jurisdiction, and the crossclaim is not excluded under § 1367(b).

rules.rule_statement: A district court has discretion to decline to exercise supplemental jurisdiction over a claim that would otherwise qualify for supplemental jurisdiction in the following circumstances:

Discretionary Rejection Of Supplemental Jurisdiction
A district court has discretion to decline to exercise supplemental jurisdiction over a claim that would otherwise qualify for supplemental jurisdiction in the following circumstances:

rules.canonical_name: The Supplemental Claim Raises A Novel Or Complex Issue Of State Law
rules.rule_statement: ii) The supplemental claim substantially predominates over the claims within original federal jurisdiction; iii) All of the claims within the court’s original jurisdiction have been dismissed; or iv) In exceptional circumstances, if there are other compelling reasons for declining jurisdiction. § 1367(c). United Mine Workers v. Gibbs, 383 U.S. 715 (1966).

rules.rule_statement: Rule Any civil action commenced in a state court that is within the original jurisdiction of a U.S. district court may generally be removed by the defendant to the district court for the district and division in which the state-court action is pending. § 1441(a).
rules.short_rule_statement: Rule Any civil action commenced in a state court that is within the original jurisdiction of a U.S. district court may generally be removed by the defendant to the district...
rules.source_text:
Removal Jurisdiction
Rule Any civil action commenced in a state court that is within the original jurisdiction of a U.S. district court may generally be removed by the defendant to the district court for the district and division in which the state-court action is pending. § 1441(a).

rules.rule_statement: Generally, the right of removal under § 1441(a) is a right of the defendant only and is not available to a plaintiff defending a counterclaim that could have originally been brought in federal court. Removal jurisdiction is not a substitute for either federal question or diversity jurisdiction. Instead, removal jurisdiction is simply a mechanism by which defendants in a state action can get the action into federal court.
rules.short_rule_statement: Generally, the right of removal under § 1441(a) is a right of the defendant only and is not available to a plaintiff defending a counterclaim that could have originally been...
rules.source_text:
Basis
Generally, the right of removal under § 1441(a) is a right of the defendant only and is not available to a plaintiff defending a counterclaim that could have originally been brought in federal court. Removal jurisdiction is not a substitute for either federal question or diversity jurisdiction. Instead, removal jurisdiction is simply a mechanism by which defendants in a state action can get the action into federal court.

rules.canonical_name: Removal To Another District Or Division Within The Same State
rules.rule_statement: The removal statute specifically requires the case to be removed to the district court for the district and division in which the state-court action is pending. Consequently, a case that is removed to a district court that is within the state but outside the district or division of the original state court is subject to a motion to remand. However, because this requirement has been characterized as procedural rather than jurisdictional, some courts have transferred the case to the proper federal court instead of remanding it to the state court.
rules.short_rule_statement: The removal statute specifically requires the case to be removed to the district court for the district and division in which the state-court action is pending. Consequently, a...
rules.source_text:
Removal To Another District Or Division Within The Same State
The removal statute specifically requires the case to be removed to the district court for the district and division in which the state-court action is pending. Consequently, a case that is removed to a district court that is within the state but outside the district or division of the original state court is subject to a motion to remand. However, because this requirement has been characterized as procedural rather than jurisdictional, some courts have transferred the case to the proper federal court instead of remanding it to the state court.

rules.rule_statement: In general, the right of removal is determined by the pleadings filed as of the time of the filing of the notice of removal. However, in diversity cases, diversity of citizenship must exist at:
rules.short_rule_statement: In general, the right of removal is determined by the pleadings filed as of the time of the filing of the notice of removal. However, in diversity cases, diversity of...
rules.source_text:
Determination
In general, the right of removal is determined by the pleadings filed as of the time of the filing of the notice of removal. However, in diversity cases, diversity of citizenship must exist at:

rules.canonical_name: The Time Of Filing The Original Action;
rules.rule_statement: ii) The time of the filing of the notice of removal, unless the plaintiff dismisses a party who would have destroyed diversity jurisdiction. Removal is permitted when a party who prevents diversity jurisdiction is voluntarily dismissed from the action. Moreover, removal is permitted when a party who prevents diversity was fraudulently joined to defeat diversity. However, joinder of a nondiverse party will prevent removal, even if that party was joined for the purpose of preventing removal, when the plaintiff has a colorable basis for recovering from the joined defendant. The federal court to which the action is removed is not precluded from hearing and determining any claim in the action because the state court from which the action was removed did not have jurisdiction over that claim. § 1441(f).
rules.short_rule_statement: ii) The time of the filing of the notice of removal, unless the plaintiff dismisses a party who would have destroyed diversity jurisdiction. Removal is permitted when a party...

The Time Of Filing The Original Action;
ii) The time of the filing of the notice of removal, unless the plaintiff dismisses a party who would have destroyed diversity jurisdiction. Removal is permitted when a party who prevents diversity jurisdiction is voluntarily dismissed from the action. Moreover, removal is permitted when a party who prevents diversity was fraudulently joined to defeat diversity. However, joinder of a nondiverse party will prevent removal, even if that party was joined for the purpose of preventing removal, when the plaintiff has a colorable basis for recovering from the joined defendant. The federal court to which the action is removed is not precluded from hearing and determining any claim in the action because the state court from which the action was removed did not have jurisdiction over that claim. § 1441(f).

rules.rule_statement: If removal is sought solely based on diversity jurisdiction, then the claim may be removed only if no defendant is a citizen of the state in which the action was filed. § 1441(b). Example: If a Texas corporation sues an Illinois defendant in Illinois state court, then the Illinois defendant is not permitted to remove the action to federal court based on diversity jurisdiction.
rules.short_rule_statement: If removal is sought solely based on diversity jurisdiction, then the claim may be removed only if no defendant is a citizen of the state in which the action was filed. §...
rules.source_text:
Home-State Limitation
If removal is sought solely based on diversity jurisdiction, then the claim may be removed only if no defendant is a citizen of the state in which the action was filed. § 1441(b). Example: If a Texas corporation sues an Illinois defendant in Illinois state court, then the Illinois defendant is not permitted to remove the action to federal court based on diversity jurisdiction.

rules.rule_statement: A matter cannot be removed based on diversity of citizenship more than one year after the action is commenced. However, this one-year rule does not apply if the district court finds that the plaintiff acted in bad faith to prevent a defendant from removing the action. § 1446(c). Example: The one-year rule would not apply if a plaintiff deliberately failed to disclose the actual amount in controversy to prevent the defendant from seeking removal in a timely manner.
rules.short_rule_statement: A matter cannot be removed based on diversity of citizenship more than one year after the action is commenced. However, this one-year rule does not apply if the district court...
rules.source_text:
One-Year Rule
A matter cannot be removed based on diversity of citizenship more than one year after the action is commenced. However, this one-year rule does not apply if the district court finds that the plaintiff acted in bad faith to prevent a defendant from removing the action. § 1446(c). Example: The one-year rule would not apply if a plaintiff deliberately failed to disclose the actual amount in controversy to prevent the defendant from seeking removal in a timely manner.

rules.rule_statement: Notice of removal based on diversity may assert that the amount in controversy exceeds $75,000 if the plaintiff:

Amount In Controversy
Notice of removal based on diversity may assert that the amount in controversy exceeds $75,000 if the plaintiff:

rules.rule_statement: ii) Is not required under state law to demand a specific sum; or iii) Is permitted by state law to recover more than the amount demanded. The district court will have jurisdiction if it finds by a preponderance of the evidence that the amount does exceed $75,000. A defendant’s notice of removal must make only a plausible allegation that the amount in controversy exceeds $75,000. The notice does not need to contain evidentiary submissions.

rules.canonical_name: Removal Based On Federal Question Jurisdiction
rules.rule_statement: If removal of separate and independent claims is sought based on federal question jurisdiction, and the federal question claims in the state action are joined with claims that are not independently removable, then the entire case may be removed. The district court must then sever and remand to the state court any nonremovable claims or claims not within the original or supplemental jurisdiction of the district court. § 1441(c). If the plaintiff amends the complaint after removal to delete the federal question claims, then the district court must remand the case to state court because the district court no longer has supplemental jurisdiction over the state law claims.

The district court must then sever and remand to the state court any nonremovable claims or claims not within the original or supplemental jurisdiction of the district court. § 1441(c). If the plaintiff amends the complaint after removal to delete the federal question claims, then the district court must remand the case to state court because the district court no longer has supplemental jurisdiction over the state law claims.

rules.rule_statement: Under § 1446, a defendant who wants to remove a state-court action to federal district court generally must file a notice of removal. Generally, the notice must be filed with the district court within 30 days after receipt by or service on that defendant of the initial pleading or summons. § 1446(b). If the case stated by the initial pleading is not removable, a notice of removal may be filed within 30 days after receipt by the defendant of a copy of an amended pleading, motion, order or other paper from which it may first be ascertained that the case is removable. § 1446(b). Notice must be: ii) Filed in the district court for the district and division in which the state action is pending; and iii) Accompanied by copies of all process, pleadings, and orders served on the defendants seeking removal. § 1446(a). Promptly after the notice of removal is filed with the district court, the defendants must:

Notice Of Removal
Under § 1446, a defendant who wants to remove a state-court action to federal district court generally must file a notice of removal. Generally, the notice must be filed with the district court within 30 days after receipt by or service on that defendant of the initial pleading or summons. § 1446(b). If the case stated by the initial pleading is not removable, a notice of removal may be filed within 30 days after receipt by the defendant of a copy of an amended pleading, motion, order or other paper from which it may first be ascertained that the case is removable. § 1446(b). Notice must be: ii) Filed in the district court for the district and division in which the state action is pending; and iii) Accompanied by copies of all process, pleadings, and orders served on the defendants seeking removal. § 1446(a). Promptly after the notice of removal is filed with the district court, the defendants must:

rules.rule_statement: ii) File a copy of the notice of removal with the clerk of the state court from which the action is sought to be removed. § 1446(d). Once a copy of the removal notice is filed with the state court, the removal acts as a stay of the state-court proceedings. The state court is not allowed to take any further action in the case and can be enjoined by the federal court if it does take any action.

rules.canonical_name: Requirement For Defendants To Join In Or Consent To The Removal
rules.rule_statement: In general, all defendants who have been properly joined and served are required to join in or consent to the removal. If the defendants are served at separate times and a later-served defendant files a notice of removal, then any earlier-served defendant may join in the removal even though that defendant did not previously initiate or consent to removal. § 1446(b). In cases of removal based on federal question jurisdiction, only those defendants against whom the federal claim is asserted must join in or consent to the removal. § 1441(c)(2). Additionally, a class action based on the Class Action Fairness Act of 2005 (CAFA) may be removed by any defendant without the consent of all defendants. § 1453(b).

Requirement For Defendants To Join In Or Consent To The Removal
In general, all defendants who have been properly joined and served are required to join in or consent to the removal. If the defendants are served at separate times and a later-served defendant files a notice of removal, then any earlier-served defendant may join in the removal even though that defendant did not previously initiate or consent to removal. § 1446(b). In cases of removal based on federal question jurisdiction, only those defendants against whom the federal claim is asserted must join in or consent to the removal. § 1441(c)(2). Additionally, a class action based on the Class Action Fairness Act of 2005 (CAFA) may be removed by any defendant without the consent of all defendants. § 1453(b).

The district court may issue all necessary orders and processes to bring before it all proper parties, regardless of whether those parties were served by process issued by the state court. § 1447(a). If the action was removed before each defendant had been served with process, or if service of process was defective, then service of process can be completed or new process issued in the same manner as in cases originally filed in district court. § 1448. State-court orders issued before removal to district court remain in effect but are subject to district court modification when necessary. § 1450.

rules.canonical_name: Procedure For A Demand For A Jury Trial
rules.rule_statement: If an action is removed to federal court before a party has exercised a right to a jury trial, the parties will face specific time limitations on the exercise of the right in federal court. If the removing party seeks a jury trial, then the removing party must file a demand for a jury trial within 14 days of filing the notice of removal. The nonremoving party also has 14 days to file a jury trial demand, but the countdown begins after service of notice of removal. If a party fails to timely file a jury trial demand, then the party’s right to a jury trial is waived. If a party demanded a jury trial before removal, the party need not make a jury trial demand after removal to preserve that right.

Procedure For A Demand For A Jury Trial
If an action is removed to federal court before a party has exercised a right to a jury trial, the parties will face specific time limitations on the exercise of the right in federal court. If the removing party seeks a jury trial, then the removing party must file a demand for a jury trial within 14 days of filing the notice of removal. The nonremoving party also has 14 days to file a jury trial demand, but the countdown begins after service of notice of removal. If a party fails to timely file a jury trial demand, then the party’s right to a jury trial is waived. If a party demanded a jury trial before removal, the party need not make a jury trial demand after removal to preserve that right.
