rules.canonical_name: For Lack Of Subject Matter Jurisdiction
rules.rule_statement: If at any time before final judgment it appears that the district court lacks subject matter jurisdiction, the case must be remanded to the state court from which it came. § 1447(c). However, for the purposes of § 1447(c) and (d), when a case is remanded to a state court because the federal district court declines to exercise supplemental jurisdiction, the remand is not based on a lack of subject matter jurisdiction.

If the propriety of the removal is challenged, then the burden of establishing proper removal is on the party who removed the case.

rules.rule_statement: The district court’s order remanding the case may require payment of costs, including attorney’s fees, incurred because of the removal. § 1447(c). The Supreme Court has held that, absent unusual circumstances, federal courts may award attorney’s fees under § 1447(c) only when the removing party lacked an objectively reasonable basis for seeking removal.

rules.rule_statement: In addition to the types of jurisdiction listed previously, there are federal courts created by Congress with jurisdiction over specific types of cases. i) The United States Claims Court (formerly “the Court of Claims”) – original jurisdiction over actions brought against the United States (or its officers) or concerning federal property; ii) The United States Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit – appellate jurisdiction over claims against the federal government (or its officers); iii) The United States Court of International Trade – nationwide jurisdiction over civil actions arising out of United States customs and international trade laws; iv) The United States Tax Court – jurisdiction over taxpayer challenges to Internal Revenue Service (IRS) deficiency determinations; and No amount-in-controversy requirement exists for the above types of cases unless an action is brought under the Consumer Product Safety Act against a party other than the United States.

rules.rule_statement: A court may not exercise personal jurisdiction over a defendant unless: ii) The exercise of jurisdiction does not offend traditional notions of fair play and substantial justice. Due process also requires that deprivation of property by adjudication be preceded by notice and an opportunity for a hearing appropriate to the case.

rules.rule_statement: Unlike subject matter jurisdiction, personal jurisdiction can be established by a party’s consent. Plaintiffs consent to personal jurisdiction by filing the lawsuit.
rules.short_rule_statement: Unlike subject matter jurisdiction, personal jurisdiction can be established by a party’s consent. Plaintiffs consent to personal jurisdiction by filing the lawsuit.
rules.source_text:
Consent
Unlike subject matter jurisdiction, personal jurisdiction can be established by a party’s consent. Plaintiffs consent to personal jurisdiction by filing the lawsuit.

rules.rule_statement: A defendant may agree in advance by contract to submit to the jurisdiction of the court if a lawsuit is brought by the plaintiff. Such contractual consent is not effective if the court determines that the contract was a contract of adhesion. A defendant may also stipulate to personal jurisdiction once an action is brought. Some states require a party to consent to personal jurisdiction to register to do business in the state. In such states, registering to do business in the state constitutes express consent to any suits in that state.

Express Consent
A defendant may agree in advance by contract to submit to the jurisdiction of the court if a lawsuit is brought by the plaintiff. Such contractual consent is not effective if the court determines that the contract was a contract of adhesion. A defendant may also stipulate to personal jurisdiction once an action is brought. Some states require a party to consent to personal jurisdiction to register to do business in the state. In such states, registering to do business in the state constitutes express consent to any suits in that state.

rules.rule_statement: A defendant may be deemed to have consented through conduct, such as filing a counterclaim or driving a vehicle within a state.
rules.short_rule_statement: A defendant may be deemed to have consented through conduct, such as filing a counterclaim or driving a vehicle within a state.
rules.source_text:
Implied Consent
A defendant may be deemed to have consented through conduct, such as filing a counterclaim or driving a vehicle within a state.

rules.rule_statement: Authorizing an agent to accept service of process in a state also constitutes consent to personal jurisdiction in that state. Usually, a state requires nonresidents doing business in a heavily regulated industry to appoint an agent. When an agent is appointed for process, either by contract or by statute, the defendant is not subject to personal jurisdiction if the agent does not advise the defendant of service of process. This rule does not apply when defendants select their own agents.

This rule does not apply when defendants select their own agents.

rules.rule_statement: Rule In personam jurisdiction is the power that a court has over an individual party. It is required when a judgment is sought that would impose an obligation on a defendant personally. A court with personam jurisdiction over a party may issue a judgment against the party personally, and the judgment can be satisfied by seizure of the party’s assets.

In Personam Jurisdiction

A court with personam jurisdiction over a party may issue a judgment against the party personally, and the judgment can be satisfied by seizure of the party’s assets.

Bases For In Personam Jurisdiction
A court’s exercise of personal jurisdiction over a defendant satisfies due process requirements if one of the three traditional bases for in personam jurisdiction exist:

rules.rule_statement: ii) Domicile; or iii) Consent. If personal jurisdiction is not based on one of the three traditional bases, then the court must find that exercising jurisdiction over a nonresident defendant comports with due process.
rules.short_rule_statement: ii) Domicile; or iii) Consent. If personal jurisdiction is not based on one of the three traditional bases, then the court must find that exercising jurisdiction over a...
rules.source_text:
Voluntary Presence
ii) Domicile; or iii) Consent. If personal jurisdiction is not based on one of the three traditional bases, then the court must find that exercising jurisdiction over a nonresident defendant comports with due process.

rules.rule_statement: If a defendant is voluntarily present in the forum state and is served with process while there, then the court has personal jurisdiction over the defendant. However, most courts today apply two exceptions to the rule regarding voluntary presence: i) Fraud – If a plaintiff fraudulently brings a defendant into the state for the purpose of serving process on him, then the service will most likely be invalid. ii) Passing through the forum state – A defendant is also immune if the defendant is merely passing through the state to attend other judiciary proceedings. California Distinction: Transient Jurisdiction California does not have an exception to the voluntary presence rule for a defendant who is merely in the state to attend other judiciary proceedings. If a nonresident defendant is personally served while in the state, even if only for an unrelated judicial proceeding, California courts will have personal jurisdiction over him. Silverman v. Sup. Ct., 203 Cal. App. 3d 145, 149 (1988).

If a nonresident defendant is personally served while in the state, even if only for an unrelated judicial proceeding, California courts will have personal jurisdiction over him. Silverman v. Sup. Ct., 203 Cal. App.

rules.rule_statement: If authorized by statute, a court can have jurisdiction over a person who is domiciled in the forum state (i.e., state in which the court is located) even if the person is temporarily absent from that state. This statutory authorization can be enacted retroactively to apply to a cause of action arising before the enactment of the statute. The same rules of domicile discussed in § I.C.3. Citizenship of Parties, supra, are applicable here.
rules.short_rule_statement: If authorized by statute, a court can have jurisdiction over a person who is domiciled in the forum state (i.e., state in which the court is located) even if the person is...
rules.source_text:
Domicile
If authorized by statute, a court can have jurisdiction over a person who is domiciled in the forum state (i.e., state in which the court is located) even if the person is temporarily absent from that state. This statutory authorization can be enacted retroactively to apply to a cause of action arising before the enactment of the statute. The same rules of domicile discussed in § I.C.3. Citizenship of Parties, supra, are applicable here.

rules.rule_statement: A long-arm statute is a state statute that gives courts within the state personal jurisdiction over out-of-state defendants. Most states have enacted statutes that authorize personal jurisdiction over nonresidents who engage in some activity in the state or cause some action to occur within the state. In many states, the long-arm statute either directly authorizes or has been interpreted as authorizing jurisdiction to the extent permissible under the Due Process Clause. Therefore, a federal court in those states need only determine whether the exercise of personal jurisdiction comports with due process. In some states, the long-arm statute confers jurisdiction only over specific activities undertaken in the state (e.g., owning property, committing a tort, or entering into a contract to supply goods or services in the state). In these states, before conducting a due process analysis, a federal court must first determine whether the statutory requirements have been met. California Distinction: Long-Arm Statute California’s long-arm statute gives courts the broad power to exercise jurisdiction on any basis not inconsistent with all due process requirements under the California and U.S. Constitutions. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 410.10.

rules.source_text:
Long-Arm Statutes
A long-arm statute is a state statute that gives courts within the state personal jurisdiction over out-of-state defendants. Most states have enacted statutes that authorize personal jurisdiction over nonresidents who engage in some activity in the state or cause some action to occur within the state. In many states, the long-arm statute either directly authorizes or has been interpreted as authorizing jurisdiction to the extent permissible under the Due Process Clause. Therefore, a federal court in those states need only determine whether the exercise of personal jurisdiction comports with due process. In some states, the long-arm statute confers jurisdiction only over specific activities undertaken in the state (e.g., owning property, committing a tort, or entering into a contract to supply goods or services in the state). In these states, before conducting a due process analysis, a federal court must first determine whether the statutory requirements have been met.

rules.rule_statement: Attachment jurisdiction is a special type of quasi-in-rem jurisdiction. Historically, a plaintiff asserting a personal claim against a defendant could use an attachment of property owned by the defendant and located in the forum state as a device to obtain jurisdiction. But under the modern rule, if the claim is not related to the ownership of the attached property, mere ownership of the attached property is generally not sufficient to satisfy the minimum contacts test. If there are such contacts, then the court generally has in personam jurisdiction over the defendant, and attachment jurisdiction is unnecessary. See § II.C.2. Quasi-In-Rem Jurisdiction, infra.
rules.short_rule_statement: Attachment jurisdiction is a special type of quasi-in-rem jurisdiction. Historically, a plaintiff asserting a personal claim against a defendant could use an attachment of...
rules.source_text:
Attachment
Attachment jurisdiction is a special type of quasi-in-rem jurisdiction. Historically, a plaintiff asserting a personal claim against a defendant could use an attachment of property owned by the defendant and located in the forum state as a device to obtain jurisdiction. But under the modern rule, if the claim is not related to the ownership of the attached property, mere ownership of the attached property is generally not sufficient to satisfy the minimum contacts test. If there are such contacts, then the court generally has in personam jurisdiction over the defendant, and attachment jurisdiction is unnecessary. See § II.C.2. Quasi-In-Rem Jurisdiction, infra.

rules.rule_statement: In general, due process requirements are satisfied if:
rules.short_rule_statement: In general, due process requirements are satisfied if:
rules.source_text:
Due Process Requirements
In general, due process requirements are satisfied if:

rules.canonical_name: The Nonresident Defendant Has Sufficient Minimum Contacts With The Forum State;
rules.rule_statement: ii) The maintenance of the action does not offend traditional notions of fair play and substantial justice. EXAM NOTE: When analyzing a personal jurisdiction question, focus on (1) the contacts that the defendant has or had with the forum state and (2) whether the assertion of jurisdiction by the court would comport with fair play and substantial justice.  Personal jurisdiction will depend on the facts of each case. Note that Shaffer expressly excluded actions to enforce previous judgments by such remedies as attachment, garnishment, and sequestration from the requirement of minimum contacts with the defendant.

Note that Shaffer expressly excluded actions to enforce previous judgments by such remedies as attachment, garnishment, and sequestration from the requirement of minimum contacts with the defendant.

rules.rule_statement: To warrant the assertion of in personam jurisdiction, a defendant’s contacts with the forum state must be purposeful and substantial, such that the defendant should reasonably anticipate (foresee) being taken to court there. Foreseeability depends on whether defendants recognize or anticipate that by running their business, they risk being a party to a suit in a particular state. This test is known as the purposeful availment requirement. Specific personal jurisdiction refers to a court’s authority over a defendant that arises from the defendant’s case-specific activities. Specific personal jurisdiction may be warranted over a cause of action that arises out of or closely relates to a defendant’s contact with the forum state. A strict causal relationship between a plaintiff’s cause of action and a defendant’s contacts with the forum state is not required, so long as “there is a strong relationship among the defendant, the forum, and the litigation.” Some courts have permitted specific personal jurisdiction over insurers when a car accident occurs within the territorial scope of the policy’s coverage. This applies even if the insurance company has no other contacts with the state. General personal jurisdiction refers to a court’s authority over a defendant even when the cause of action has no relationship with the defendant’s contacts with the forum state. General personal jurisdiction requires that a defendant:

rules.canonical_name: Be Domiciled In The Forum State;

components.content: Challenges with the purposeful availment requirement
components.source_text: 3)      Challenges with the purposeful availment requirement

rules.rule_statement: ii) Have continuous and systematic contacts with the forum state. There are situations when it is difficult to discern whether purposeful availment is present. For example, consider a product that is put into the stream of commerce by a person who purchased the product from the manufacturer and incorporated it in the person’s own product, which is then put into the stream of commerce. Did the manufacturer avail itself of the benefits and laws of the jurisdiction in which the product containing the manufacturer’s product is used? The Court has not been able to reach a cohesive answer. In Asahi, while four justices found sufficiency with the mere knowledge that the product being sold would end up in the forum state, four other justices believed that the manufacturer needed to take an additional step to have availed itself of the forum. A majority of the justices concluded that, even assuming the manufacturer’s conduct constituted purposeful availment, it violated the notions of fair play and substantial justice for the manufacturer (a foreign corporation) to be subject to the jurisdiction of the forum court. Asahi Metal Industry Co. v. Superior Court of California, 480 U.S. 102 (1987). See § b. Fair Play and Substantial Justice, below. Under some circumstances, the contacts of one defendant with the forum state may be imputed to another defendant for the purposes of determining jurisdiction.

rules.rule_statement: Contacts by a nonresident employer’s agents or employees are generally imputed to the employer when the agent or employee is acting within the scope of the agency or employment. An out-of-state corporation is generally not subject to personal jurisdiction solely because of contacts in the state by an independent contractor.
rules.short_rule_statement: Contacts by a nonresident employer’s agents or employees are generally imputed to the employer when the agent or employee is acting within the scope of the agency or...
rules.source_text:
To Employer
Contacts by a nonresident employer’s agents or employees are generally imputed to the employer when the agent or employee is acting within the scope of the agency or employment. An out-of-state corporation is generally not subject to personal jurisdiction solely because of contacts in the state by an independent contractor.

rules.rule_statement: Each partner is generally an agent of the partnership for the purpose of its business. Therefore, a partner’s activities on behalf of the partnership can confer personal jurisdiction over the partnership entity. However, the contacts may not necessarily establish personal jurisdiction over that partner or the other partners of the partnership in their individual capacities.
rules.short_rule_statement: Each partner is generally an agent of the partnership for the purpose of its business. Therefore, a partner’s activities on behalf of the partnership can confer personal...
rules.source_text:
To Partnership
Each partner is generally an agent of the partnership for the purpose of its business. Therefore, a partner’s activities on behalf of the partnership can confer personal jurisdiction over the partnership entity. However, the contacts may not necessarily establish personal jurisdiction over that partner or the other partners of the partnership in their individual capacities.

rules.rule_statement: An out-of-state corporation’s contacts with the forum state will not automatically establish jurisdiction over a wholly owned subsidiary of the corporation, and contacts by a wholly owned subsidiary will not automatically confer jurisdiction over a corporate parent. However, if the subsidiary is the corporate parent’s alter ego or is specifically acting as the corporate parent’s agent, then its contacts may be imputed to the corporate parent.

To Parent Corporation
An out-of-state corporation’s contacts with the forum state will not automatically establish jurisdiction over a wholly owned subsidiary of the corporation, and contacts by a wholly owned subsidiary will not automatically confer jurisdiction over a corporate parent. However, if the subsidiary is the corporate parent’s alter ego or is specifically acting as the corporate parent’s agent, then its contacts may be imputed to the corporate parent.

Fair Play And Substantial Justice
Once minimum contacts are established, a court must still examine the facts to determine whether maintenance of the action would “offend traditional notions of fair play and substantial justice.” Courts consider a variety of factors when making this determination, including:

rules.canonical_name: The Interest Of The Forum State In Adjudicating The Matter
rules.rule_statement: ii) The burden on the defendant of appearing in the case; iii) The interest of the judicial system in the efficient resolution of controversies; and iv) The shared interests of the states in promoting common social policies.

rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 4(k)(1)(A), the service of a summons in a federal action establishes personal jurisdiction over a defendant “who is subject to the jurisdiction of a court of general jurisdiction in the state where the district court is located.” A federal court must generally determine personal jurisdiction as if it were a court of the state in which it is situated. Therefore, a federal court will look to state jurisdictional statutes to determine whether it has personal jurisdiction over the parties before it. See § II.B.2.d. Long-Arm Statutes, infra.
rules.short_rule_statement: Under Rule 4(k)(1)(A), the service of a summons in a federal action establishes personal jurisdiction over a defendant “who is subject to the jurisdiction of a court of general...
rules.source_text:
General Rule
Under Rule 4(k)(1)(A), the service of a summons in a federal action establishes personal jurisdiction over a defendant “who is subject to the jurisdiction of a court of general jurisdiction in the state where the district court is located.” A federal court must generally determine personal jurisdiction as if it were a court of the state in which it is situated. Therefore, a federal court will look to state jurisdictional statutes to determine whether it has personal jurisdiction over the parties before it. See § II.B.2.d. Long-Arm Statutes, infra.

rules.rule_statement: While a federal court generally is not vested with nationwide personal jurisdiction, a federal court does have national personal jurisdiction when authorized by federal statute (e.g., for federal statutory interpleader actions). Rule 4(k)(1)(C). See § VI.C.2.b. In Personam Jurisdiction, infra.

rules.rule_statement: Rule 4(k)(1)(B) is often referred to as the “bulge provision.” Under this Rule, a federal court has personal jurisdiction over a party who is served within a U.S. judicial district and not more than 100 miles from where the summons is issued, even if state law would otherwise not permit such service. The bulge provision applies to two types of parties: ii) A required party who is joined under Rule 19 (see § VI.A.2.c. In Personam Jurisdiction, infra).

This provision is typically used for non-U.S. residents who have general contacts with the United States but not with any one state in particular.

rules.rule_statement: For the purposes of in personam jurisdiction, a corporation is a resident corporation only if it is incorporated in the forum state. Any action may be brought against a corporation that is incorporated in the forum state. If the corporation is not incorporated in the state, then, for the purposes of in personam jurisdiction, it will constitute a foreign corporation.

rules.source_text:
Resident Corporations
For the purposes of in personam jurisdiction, a corporation is a resident corporation only if it is incorporated in the forum state. Any action may be brought against a corporation that is incorporated in the forum state. If the corporation is not incorporated in the state, then, for the purposes of in personam jurisdiction, it will constitute a foreign corporation.

rules.rule_statement: The rules of minimum contacts and substantial fairness apply to foreign corporations. To determine whether the corporation is subject to a state’s general jurisdiction, the proper inquiry is whether a corporation’s affiliations with the forum state are so “continuous and systematic” as to render the corporation essentially “at home” in the forum state. A corporate defendant is always at home in:

Foreign Corporations
The rules of minimum contacts and substantial fairness apply to foreign corporations. To determine whether the corporation is subject to a state’s general jurisdiction, the proper inquiry is whether a corporation’s affiliations with the forum state are so “continuous and systematic” as to render the corporation essentially “at home” in the forum state. A corporate defendant is always at home in:

rules.canonical_name: The State Of The Corporation’S Place Of Incorporation;
rules.rule_statement: ii) The state of its principal place of business. In exceptional cases, a corporate defendant’s operations in another forum state may be so substantial and of such a nature as to render the corporation at home in that state as well.

rules.rule_statement: It is generally accepted that merely having a website does not subject a defendant to process everywhere that the site can be viewed. Most courts base jurisdiction over a nonresident’s website on the degree of interactivity between the website and the forum state. Merely making a website accessible to the public and posting information is generally considered passive on a sliding scale and should not result in personal jurisdiction. However, websites that are integral to a defendant’s business are on the other end of the sliding scale. When a website’s purpose is to assist in conducting direct business transactions, courts are more likely to find minimum contacts with a state and assert personal jurisdiction. Example: In the Zippo case, the defendant was a nonresident corporation that operated an integral website with commercial contacts to more than 3,000 Pennsylvania residents and internet service providers. The court found a high level of interactivity with Pennsylvania and determined that there was personal jurisdiction over the defendant in Pennsylvania. The law in this area continues to evolve.

rules.canonical_name: Contract As Signiﬁcant Factor In Establishing Minimum Contacts
rules.rule_statement: The fact that one party to a contract is a resident of the forum state will not by itself confer personal jurisdiction over the nonresident party to the contract. Nonetheless, the existence of the contract can be a significant factor in determining whether minimum contacts exist and support a finding that the exercise of personal jurisdiction over the nonresident is appropriate.

Contract As Signiﬁcant Factor In Establishing Minimum Contacts
The fact that one party to a contract is a resident of the forum state will not by itself confer personal jurisdiction over the nonresident party to the contract. Nonetheless, the existence of the contract can be a significant factor in determining whether minimum contacts exist and support a finding that the exercise of personal jurisdiction over the nonresident is appropriate.

rules.rule_statement: If the contract contains a choice-of-law provision indicating that the forum state’s law is to be used in any action regarding the contract, then this will be a significant factor in finding jurisdiction. Such a choice-of-law provision establishes that the nonresident purposefully availed itself of the benefits of the forum state’s laws.

Choice-Of-Law Provision
If the contract contains a choice-of-law provision indicating that the forum state’s law is to be used in any action regarding the contract, then this will be a significant factor in finding jurisdiction. Such a choice-of-law provision establishes that the nonresident purposefully availed itself of the benefits of the forum state’s laws.

rules.canonical_name: Contracts Of Adhesion Or Procured By Fraud
rules.rule_statement: If the contract is adhesive or was procured through fraud, then personal jurisdiction based on the contract is not appropriate.

rules.rule_statement: Personal jurisdiction may also be proper when a defendant commits an intentional tort in his own state, but the conduct was aimed at the plaintiff in the forum state. When a defendant intentionally harms a forum state resident, a court may apply the “Calder Effects Test,” which allows personal jurisdiction over a party whose conduct was expressly aimed at the forum state, knowing that the brunt of the harmful effects would be felt there. Burnham v. Superior Court of California, 495 U.S. 604 (1990). McGee v. Int’l Life Ins. Co., 355 U.S. 220 (1957). International Shoe Co. v. State of Washington, 326 U.S. 310 (1945). See Shaffer v. Heitner, 433 U.S. at 210. World-Wide Volkswagen Corp. v. Woodson, 444 U.S. 286 (1980). Ford Motor Co. v. Montana Eighth Jud. Dist. Ct. 529 U.S. ___, 141 S. Ct. 1017 (2021). Rossman v. State Farm Mut. Auto. Ins. Co., 832 F.2d 282 (4th Cir. 1987); Farmers Ins. Exchange v. Portage La Prairie Mut. Ins. Co., 907 F.2d 911 (9th Cir. 1990) (litigation in a foreign forum is foreseeable under a contract to indemnify and defend the insured nationwide). Helicopteros Nacionales de Colombia, S.A. v. Hall, 466 U.S. 408 (1984). Cannon Mfg. Co. v. Cudahy Packing Co., 267 U.S. 333 (1925). International Shoe Co. v. State of Washington, 326 U.S. 310 (1945). Omni Capital Int’l, Ltd. v. Rudolf Wolff & Co., 484 U.S. 97 (1987). See Mississippi Publ’g Corp. v. Murphree, 326 U.S. 438 (1946); Robertson v. R.R. Labor Bd., 268 U.S. 619 (1925). Rossman v. State Farm Mut. Auto. Ins. Co., 832 F.2d 282 (4th Cir. 1987); Farmers Ins. Exchange v. Portage La Prairie Mut. Ins. Co., 907 F.2d 911 (9th Cir. 1990) (litigation in a foreign forum is foreseeable under a contract to indemnify and defend the insured nationwide). Daimler AG v. Bauman, 571 U.S. 117 (2014) (emphasis added). Compare BNSF Ry. v. Tyrrell, 137 S. Ct. 1549 (2017) (foreign railroad company’s ownership of over 2,000 miles of track and employment of over 2,000 persons in a state not sufficient to render corporation at home when about 94% of the track it owned and more than 95% of its workforce were out-of-state) with Perkins v. Benguet Consol. Mining Co., 342 U.S. 437 (1952) (a foreign mining company that relocated its management activities (e.g., directors’ meetings, business correspondence, banking, stock transfers, payment of salaries, purchasing of machinery) during World War II to a state was at home in that state). See Zippo Mfg. Co. v. Zippo Dot Com, Inc., 952 F. Supp. 1119 (W.D. Pa. 1997). Burger King v. Rudzewicz, 471 U.S. 462 (1985). Calder v. Jones, 465 U.S. 783 (1984).
rules.short_rule_statement: Personal jurisdiction may also be proper when a defendant commits an intentional tort in his own state, but the conduct was aimed at the plaintiff in the forum state. When a...
rules.source_text:
Intentional Torts
Personal jurisdiction may also be proper when a defendant commits an intentional tort in his own state, but the conduct was aimed at the plaintiff in the forum state. When a defendant intentionally harms a forum state resident, a court may apply the “Calder Effects Test,” which allows personal jurisdiction over a party whose conduct was expressly aimed at the forum state, knowing that the brunt of the harmful effects would be felt there. Burnham v. Superior Court of California, 495 U.S. 604 (1990). McGee v. Int’l Life Ins. Co., 355 U.S. 220 (1957). International Shoe Co. v. State of Washington, 326 U.S. 310 (1945). See Shaffer v. Heitner, 433 U.S. at 210. World-Wide Volkswagen Corp. v. Woodson, 444 U.S. 286 (1980). Ford Motor Co. v. Montana Eighth Jud. Dist. Ct. 529 U.S. ___, 141 S. Ct. 1017 (2021). Rossman v. State Farm Mut. Auto. Ins. Co., 832 F.2d 282 (4th Cir. 1987); Farmers Ins. Exchange v. Portage La Prairie Mut. Ins. Co., 907 F.2d 911 (9th Cir. 1990) (litigatio

rules.rule_statement: Historically, jurisdiction over property has been divided into in rem jurisdiction and quasi-in-rem jurisdiction.
rules.short_rule_statement: Historically, jurisdiction over property has been divided into in rem jurisdiction and quasi-in-rem jurisdiction.
rules.source_text:
Jurisdiction Over Things
Historically, jurisdiction over property has been divided into in rem jurisdiction and quasi-in-rem jurisdiction.

rules.rule_statement: In rem jurisdiction is the authority of a court to determine issues concerning rights in property, either real or personal. Example: Examples of in-rem proceedings include (1) forfeiture and eminent domain actions initiated by the government, and (2) quiet-title actions.
rules.short_rule_statement: In rem jurisdiction is the authority of a court to determine issues concerning rights in property, either real or personal. Example: Examples of in-rem proceedings include (1)...
rules.source_text:
In Rem Jurisdiction
In rem jurisdiction is the authority of a court to determine issues concerning rights in property, either real or personal. Example: Examples of in-rem proceedings include (1) forfeiture and eminent domain actions initiated by the government, and (2) quiet-title actions.

rules.rule_statement: In rem jurisdiction allows the court to make determinations concerning property rights that are conclusive against all potential claimants, even persons who are outside of the forum state or who are unknown. Often, no parties are named, and the case is known by the name of the property at issue.
rules.short_rule_statement: In rem jurisdiction allows the court to make determinations concerning property rights that are conclusive against all potential claimants, even persons who are outside of the...
rules.source_text:
General Rule
In rem jurisdiction allows the court to make determinations concerning property rights that are conclusive against all potential claimants, even persons who are outside of the forum state or who are unknown. Often, no parties are named, and the case is known by the name of the property at issue.

rules.rule_statement: While in-rem proceedings are commenced against property, they must satisfy due process requirements for personal jurisdiction because they affect the rights of individuals in the property. In general, for in rem jurisdiction to exist, the property at issue must be in the forum state. In rem jurisdiction can be challenged if the property is fraudulently brought into the state. It is not sufficient to simply post the notice on the property or publish the notice in a newspaper regarding persons who are known to have an interest in the property and whose whereabouts are reasonably ascertainable. Additionally, any absent defendant who does not personally receive notice may set aside the judgment at any time within one year of the final judgment.
rules.short_rule_statement: While in-rem proceedings are commenced against property, they must satisfy due process requirements for personal jurisdiction because they affect the rights of individuals in...
rules.source_text:
Due Process
While in-rem proceedings are commenced against property, they must satisfy due process requirements for personal jurisdiction because they affect the rights of individuals in the property. In general, for in rem jurisdiction to exist, the property at issue must be in the forum state. In rem jurisdiction can be challenged if the property is fraudulently brought into the state. It is not sufficient to simply post the notice on the property or publish the notice in a newspaper regarding persons who are known to have an interest in the property and whose whereabouts are reasonably ascertainable. Additionally, any absent defendant who does not personally receive notice may set aside the judgment at any time within one year of the final judgment.

rules.rule_statement: Quasi-in-rem jurisdiction determines only the interests of the parties to the action regarding property located in the forum state. Traditionally, a judgment from a quasi-in-rem action was not personally binding on the defendant, could not be sued upon in any other court, and could not be enforced by seizing any of the defendant’s property other than the property at issue in the quasi-in-rem action.
rules.short_rule_statement: Quasi-in-rem jurisdiction determines only the interests of the parties to the action regarding property located in the forum state. Traditionally, a judgment from a...
rules.source_text:
Quasi-In-Rem Jurisdiction
Quasi-in-rem jurisdiction determines only the interests of the parties to the action regarding property located in the forum state. Traditionally, a judgment from a quasi-in-rem action was not personally binding on the defendant, could not be sued upon in any other court, and could not be enforced by seizing any of the defendant’s property other than the property at issue in the quasi-in-rem action.

rules.rule_statement: In a quasi-in-rem action, as with in rem and in personam actions, the defendant whose property is subject to the judgment generally must have sufficient minimum contacts with the forum state to justify the exercise of personal jurisdiction over the matter. When the action directly relates to property rights, such as the foreclosure of a mortgage or other lien, the minimum contacts requirement is satisfied because of the relationship between the claim and the property. Provided the owner of the property is given proper notice and an opportunity to be heard, the judgment is personally binding on the defendant. If the defendant chooses not to appear, the judgment of a federal district court is confined to the property that is the subject of the action. When the underlying action does not relate to property rights, but the property instead serves only as the relief sought, the minimum contacts requirement likely is not satisfied, and the court lacks personal jurisdiction to adjudicate the matter. An example is a breach-of-contract action in which the contract does not involve the in-state property.
rules.short_rule_statement: In a quasi-in-rem action, as with in rem and in personam actions, the defendant whose property is subject to the judgment generally must have sufficient minimum contacts with...

rules.rule_statement: A federal district court has quasi-in-rem personal jurisdiction over the claimants to the property in an action: ii) To enforce a lien against real or personal property when the property is located within the district. Rule 4(n)(1). If, despite the existence of sufficient minimum contacts, a federal district cannot acquire personal jurisdiction over the defendant by service of process under Rule 4, then the federal district court can only obtain jurisdiction under the circumstances and in the manner provided by the law of the state where the court is located. Consequently, quasi-in-rem jurisdiction is generally limited to exigent circumstances, such as when the defendant is a fugitive or the property is in imminent danger of disappearing. Rule 4(n)(2).
rules.short_rule_statement: A federal district court has quasi-in-rem personal jurisdiction over the claimants to the property in an action: ii) To enforce a lien against real or personal property when...
rules.source_text:
Federal Court’S Jurisdiction
A federal district court has quasi-in-rem personal jurisdiction over the claimants to the property in an action: ii) To enforce a lien against real or personal property when the property is located within the district. Rule 4(n)(1). If, despite the existence of sufficient minimum contacts, a federal district cannot acquire personal jurisdiction over the defendant by service of process under Rule 4, then the federal district court can only obtain jurisdiction under the circumstances and in the manner provided by the law of the state where the court is located. Consequently, quasi-in-rem jurisdiction is generally limited to exigent circumstances, such as when the defendant is a fugitive or the property is in imminent danger of disappearing. Rule 4(n)(2).

rules.rule_statement: Attachment jurisdiction is a special type of quasi-in-rem jurisdiction. Historically, a plaintiff asserting a personal claim against a defendant could use an attachment of property owned by the defendant and located in the forum state as a device to obtain jurisdiction. But under the modern approach, there must be minimum contacts between the defendant and the forum state to establish jurisdiction. Under the modern rule, if the claim is not related to the ownership of the attached property, mere ownership of the attached property is generally not sufficient to satisfy the minimum contacts test. If there are such contacts, then the court generally has in personam jurisdiction over the defendant, and attachment jurisdiction is unnecessary. Shaffer v. Heitner, 433 U.S. 186 (1977). Walker v. City of Hutchison, 352 U.S. 112 (1956). § 1655. Shaffer v. Heitner, 433 U.S. 186 (1977). § 1655.

Attachment Jurisdiction
Attachment jurisdiction is a special type of quasi-in-rem jurisdiction. Historically, a plaintiff asserting a personal claim against a defendant could use an attachment of property owned by the defendant and located in the forum state as a device to obtain jurisdiction. But under the modern approach, there must be minimum contacts between the defendant and the forum state to establish jurisdiction. Under the modern rule, if the claim is not related to the ownership of the attached property, mere ownership of the attached property is generally not sufficient to satisfy the minimum contacts test. If there are such contacts, then the court generally has in personam jurisdiction over the defendant, and attachment jurisdiction is unnecessary. Shaffer v. Heitner, 433 U.S. 186 (1977). Walker v. City of Hutchison, 352 U.S. 112 (1956). § 1655. Shaffer v. Heitner, 433 U.S. 186 (1977). § 1655.

rules.canonical_name: Notice And Opportunity To Be Heard
rules.rule_statement: Rule Due process requires that deprivation of property by adjudication be preceded by notice and an opportunity for a hearing appropriate to the case.
rules.short_rule_statement: Rule Due process requires that deprivation of property by adjudication be preceded by notice and an opportunity for a hearing appropriate to the case.

rules.rule_statement: Due process is met if the notice is “reasonably calculated, under all the circumstances, to apprise interested parties of the pendency of the action and afford them an opportunity to present their objections.”
rules.short_rule_statement: Due process is met if the notice is “reasonably calculated, under all the circumstances, to apprise interested parties of the pendency of the action and afford them an...

Due Process
Due process is met if the notice is “reasonably calculated, under all the circumstances, to apprise interested parties of the pendency of the action and afford them an opportunity to present their objections.”
topic_path: Notice And Opportunity To Be Heard

rules.rule_statement: If the identity and address of an interested party are known or obtainable through reasonable efforts, then notice through in-person delivery, registered mail, return receipt requested, or some other means likely to notify the particular individual is required. If the plaintiff knows that the defendant did not receive notice, then the plaintiff cannot proceed unless there are no other reasonable methods to notify the defendant. If the identity or address of an interested party is not obtainable through reasonable efforts, then other means may be satisfactory (e.g., publication of notice in newspapers). The general constitutional test is what is reasonable under the circumstances. The standards are less strict for in rem and quasi-in-rem cases than for in personam cases. When there are multiple defendants, each defendant must be served, but the manner of service will depend on whether their identities and addresses are known or unknown. When an agent has been appointed for process, either by contract or by statute, the defendant will not be subject to personal jurisdiction if the agent does not advise the defendant of service of process. This rule does not apply when defendants select their own agents.

rules.rule_statement: Satisfying due process is not the only standard for the sufficiency of notice. Both state and federal courts have procedural rules that dictate the form and service of process. While a particular form of notice may meet due process standards, it must also meet the specific procedural requirements that govern in the court where the action will be heard. For example, certified mail meets due process standards, but not every court system permits it to be used.

Court Rules
Satisfying due process is not the only standard for the sufficiency of notice. Both state and federal courts have procedural rules that dictate the form and service of process. While a particular form of notice may meet due process standards, it must also meet the specific procedural requirements that govern in the court where the action will be heard. For example, certified mail meets due process standards, but not every court system permits it to be used.

rules.rule_statement: In addition to requiring notice of the claim, due process requires that a defendant be given an opportunity to be heard when there is a state-sponsored interference with a defendant’s property interest. The state must actively participate in the interference for due process to apply. Mullane v. Central Hanover Bank & Trust Co., 339 U.S. 306 (1950). Mullane v. Central Hanover Bank & Trust Co., 339 U.S. 306 (1950). Mennonite Bd. of Missions v. Adams, 462 U.S. 791 (1983). Jones v. Flowers, 547 U.S. 220 (2006).

rules.rule_statement: A general appearance occurs when a defendant in some manner recognizes the authority of the court to litigate the merits of the case. A special appearance is a procedure by which a defendant appears before a court to challenge personal jurisdiction rather than to litigate the merits of the case. General appearance operates as a consent to personal jurisdiction. However, a defendant making a special appearance is not considered to have consented to jurisdiction as long as the defendant contests personal jurisdiction in the initial pleadings. Although many states still permit special appearances, under the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, special appearances have been abolished. Instead, the Rules allow a party entering a general appearance to make a motion to dismiss the claim for lack of personal jurisdiction under Rule 12(b) California Distinction: Special Appearances Unlike in federal practice, a defendant in California who wants to challenge the personal jurisdiction of the court may raise such challenges in a special appearance without submitting to the jurisdiction of the court. A defendant who objects to personal jurisdiction for the first time in an answer is deemed to have made a general appearance and may not then object to personal jurisdiction. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 418.10.

rules.rule_statement: ii) The parties received proper notice of the action and a reasonable opportunity to be heard. For example, if a default judgment has been entered against a defendant in an action in one state, and the plaintiff sues to enforce the judgment in another state, then the defendant may collaterally attack the judgment based on lack of personal jurisdiction. However, if the defendant had both notice and an opportunity to be heard, then a collateral attack on the judgment is not permitted under the doctrine of res judicata. See § IX.D. Claim Preclusion (Res Judicata), infra.

rules.rule_statement: Most states provide immunity from service of process to nonresidents who enter the state for the purposes of attending a trial or deposition as a witness, party, or attorney. The federal courts also generally provide immunity from federal suit under the same circumstances. California Distinction: Immunity California provides no immunity from service for defendants who are temporarily present in the state to participate in another judicial proceeding. Silverman v. Superior Court, 249 Cal. Rptr. 724 (Ct. App. 1988). Baldwin v. Iowa State Traveling Men’s Ass’n, 283 U.S. 522 (1931).

rules.rule_statement: Venue refers to the proper place for an action to be brought. Venue addresses which court among the various courts having personal and subject matter jurisdiction would be the proper forum for hearing a matter.
rules.short_rule_statement: Venue refers to the proper place for an action to be brought. Venue addresses which court among the various courts having personal and subject matter jurisdiction would be the...

Venue
Venue refers to the proper place for an action to be brought. Venue addresses which court among the various courts having personal and subject matter jurisdiction would be the proper forum for hearing a matter.

rules.rule_statement: Venue requirements are statutory and are intended to ensure the parties a fair and convenient forum for litigating their dispute. For cases in federal court, the venue rules determine the proper geographic district (e.g., Central District of California) where a trial should occur. Each state has at least one federal judicial district, and a few states have as many as four.
rules.short_rule_statement: Venue requirements are statutory and are intended to ensure the parties a fair and convenient forum for litigating their dispute. For cases in federal court, the venue rules...
rules.source_text:
In General
Venue requirements are statutory and are intended to ensure the parties a fair and convenient forum for litigating their dispute. For cases in federal court, the venue rules determine the proper geographic district (e.g., Central District of California) where a trial should occur. Each state has at least one federal judicial district, and a few states have as many as four.

rules.rule_statement: The common law created a distinction between local and transitory actions for venue. In general, local actions involve title to property and must be brought in a court where the property is located. Transitory actions involve a cause of action based on events that could have taken place anywhere. Many state venue statutes continue to apply this distinction. However, in federal court, venue is determined without regard to whether the action is local or transitory. § 1391(a)(2).
rules.short_rule_statement: The common law created a distinction between local and transitory actions for venue. In general, local actions involve title to property and must be brought in a court where...
rules.source_text:
Local And Transitory Actions
The common law created a distinction between local and transitory actions for venue. In general, local actions involve title to property and must be brought in a court where the property is located. Transitory actions involve a cause of action based on events that could have taken place anywhere. Many state venue statutes continue to apply this distinction. However, in federal court, venue is determined without regard to whether the action is local or transitory. § 1391(a)(2).

rules.rule_statement: Rule In general, venue in a federal civil action is proper in one of the following judicial districts: same state in which the district is located; ii) A judicial district where a “substantial part of the events or omissions” on which the claim is based occurred; or iii) A judicial district where a “substantial part of the property” that is the subject of the action is located.

rules.rule_statement: For venue, a natural person is deemed to reside in the judicial district where that person is domiciled. § 1391(c)(1). A natural person includes a noncitizen lawfully admitted for permanent residence into the United States.

rules.rule_statement: A defendant that is an entity with the capacity to sue and be sued, regardless of whether incorporated, is deemed to reside in any judicial district where the entity is subject to personal jurisdiction regarding the civil action in question. § 1391(c)(2). In a state that contains multiple judicial districts and in which a defendant corporation is subject to personal jurisdiction when the action is commenced, the corporation “shall be deemed to reside in any district in that State within which its contacts would be sufficient to subject it to personal jurisdiction if that district were a separate State.” If there is no such district, then the corporation is deemed to reside in the district with which it has the most significant contacts. § 1391(d). Example: A corporation is incorporated in Delaware and has its principal place of business in Colorado. The corporation maintains a regional sales office in Orlando, Florida (U.S. Middle District Court in Florida) but does not conduct activities anywhere else in Florida. The corporation is sued for breach of a contract executed in the Orlando office and performed in Orlando. For federal venue purposes, the corporation resides in the District of Delaware, the District of Colorado, and the Middle District of Florida. Other than the Middle District, the corporation is not a resident of any other judicial district in Florida. If the entity is a plaintiff, then it is deemed to reside only in the judicial district in which it maintains its principal place of business. § 1391(c)(2). EXAM NOTE: Keep in mind that, regarding partnerships and unincorporated associations, the “entity” approach is taken only for venue.  It does not apply for determining diversity jurisdiction, when a partnership or unincorporated association is considered a citizen of each state in which each of its partners or members is domiciled.

rules.canonical_name: Contract Actions – Venue Also Lies In Either The County Where The Contract Was
rules.rule_statement: If no defendant resides in California, venue is proper in any county. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 395(a). Mixed Actions: In cases involving several causes of action, each subject to different venue rules, venue generally lies in the county where any defendant resides, absent a compelling reason to conduct the trial in another county. Brown v. Superior Court, 37 Cal. 3d 477, 483 (1984). Cases against Corporations, Associations, and Partnerships: As to all three entities, venue lies in (1) the county where the contract was executed or is to be performed, (2) where the obligation or liability arose, or (3) in which the breach occurred. For corporations only, it also lies in the county where the corporation has its principal place of business. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 395.5. Local Actions: Venue lies in the county in which the land, or some part of it, is located. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 394. Transfer of Venue: California permits a motion for transfer of venue when the wrong court is designated in the complaint. Even if venue is proper, the court may, on motion, transfer venue if:

Change Of Venue
Rule District court may transfer the case for the convenience of the parties or witnesses and in the interest of justice to any other venue: From proper venue Where it might have been brought; or To which all parties have consented. § 1404. From improper venue When venue is improper, the district court must either: Dismiss the case; or Transfer the case to a proper venue (if required by interest of justice). § 1406.

rules.canonical_name: Change Of Venue When The Original Venue Is Proper
rules.rule_statement: Change of venue when the original venue is proper may be ordered upon motion of a party, including the plaintiff, or by the court on its own initiative. The right to transfer is available only when the jurisdiction and venue of the court considering the issue are proper. The burden of proof regarding the motion rests on the party seeking the transfer. § 1404(a). There is no specific time limit imposed on filing a change of venue motion. However, a court can deny a motion when a delay in filing the motion would prejudice an opposing party or was a dilatory tactic. EXAM NOTE: Section 1404(a) is essentially a codiﬁcation of the common-law doctrine of forum non conveniens for a subset of cases, allowing transfer from one proper federal forum to a more appropriate federal forum instead of requiring dismissal. When transfer from an otherwise proper venue is sought based on a forum-selection clause in a contract, the clause is accorded respect. Even though the clause itself does not render other forums legally improper, most circuit courts treat forum-selection clauses as prima facie valid, to be set aside only upon a strong showing that:

The Supreme Court has held that a forum-selection clause should be given “controlling weight in all but the most exceptional cases.” However, a few circuit courts continue to treat the clause as a significant, but not determinative, factor. EXAM NOTE:  Remember that the enforcement of a forum-selection clause may require a different remedy depending on the forum speciﬁed by the clause.  If the clause speciﬁes a federal forum, it may be enforced by a motion to transfer under §1404(a).  If the clause speciﬁes arbitration or a state or foreign forum, then §1404(a) will not apply, and the common-law doctrine of forum non conveniens may instead require the action to be dismissed.

rules.canonical_name: Change Of Venue When The Original Venue Is Improper
rules.rule_statement: If no timely objection is made to improper venue, then nothing prevents the district court from maintaining jurisdiction over the case. § 1406. Even if the court lacks personal jurisdiction over the defendant, the court is not barred from transferring a case to a different venue.

If no timely objection is made to improper venue, then nothing prevents the district court from maintaining jurisdiction over the case. § 1406. Even if the court lacks personal jurisdiction over the defendant, the court is not barred from transferring a case to a different venue.

rules.rule_statement: Rule The doctrine of forum non conveniens is a common-law rule that traditionally allows a court to dismiss an action, even when personal jurisdiction and venue are otherwise proper, if the court finds that: The forum would be too inconvenient for the parties and witnesses, and Another more convenient venue is available.

Forum Non Conveniens
Rule The doctrine of forum non conveniens is a common-law rule that traditionally allows a court to dismiss an action, even when personal jurisdiction and venue are otherwise proper, if the court finds that: The forum would be too inconvenient for the parties and witnesses, and Another more convenient venue is available.

rules.rule_statement: The doctrine of forum non conveniens has been codified and relaxed regarding transfers between federal district courts, now allowing the remedy of transfer as opposed to outright dismissal. See § III.E.1. Change of Venue When the Original Venue Is Proper, supra. Consequently, as a common-law rule, the doctrine is now used by a federal district court primarily when the forum that is deemed most appropriate for the action is a state or foreign court, because the federal statute does not apply in that circumstance.
rules.short_rule_statement: The doctrine of forum non conveniens has been codified and relaxed regarding transfers between federal district courts, now allowing the remedy of transfer as opposed to...
rules.source_text:
In General
The doctrine of forum non conveniens has been codified and relaxed regarding transfers between federal district courts, now allowing the remedy of transfer as opposed to outright dismissal. See § III.E.1. Change of Venue When the Original Venue Is Proper, supra. Consequently, as a common-law rule, the doctrine is now used by a federal district court primarily when the forum that is deemed most appropriate for the action is a state or foreign court, because the federal statute does not apply in that circumstance.

rules.rule_statement: To convince a court to invoke the doctrine of forum non conveniens, the defendant ordinarily bears a heavy burden to displace the plaintiff’s chosen forum. Further, a forum-selection clause designating a state or foreign forum modifies the conventional rule for forum non conveniens by shifting the burden to the plaintiff to show why the court should disregard the forum-selection clause. This deference for the plaintiff’s chosen forum does not apply when the plaintiff is a foreign citizen. California Distinction: Motion for Forum Non Conveniens Under California’s codification of the doctrine of forum non conveniens, a court may stay or dismiss an action if it finds that, in the interest of substantial justice, the action should be heard in a different state or country. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 418.10. Atlantic Marine Construction Co. v. United States District Court, 571 U.S. 49 (2013). Piper Aircraft Co. v. Reyno, 454 U.S. 235 (1981).

Burden Of Proof
To convince a court to invoke the doctrine of forum non conveniens, the defendant ordinarily bears a heavy burden to displace the plaintiff’s chosen forum. Further, a forum-selection clause designating a state or foreign forum modifies the conventional rule for forum non conveniens by shifting the burden to the plaintiff to show why the court should disregard the forum-selection clause. This deference for the plaintiff’s chosen forum does not apply when the plaintiff is a foreign citizen. California Distinction: Motion for Forum Non Conveniens Under California’s codification of the doctrine of forum non conveniens, a court may stay or dismiss an action if it finds that, in the interest of substantial justice, the action should be heard in a different state or country. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 418.10. Atlantic Marine Construction Co. v. United States District Court, 571 U.S. 49 (2013). Piper Aircraft Co. v. Reyno, 454 U.S. 235 (1981).

rules.rule_statement: In a diversity action, the district court is required to apply state substantive law. To determine which state’s substantive law applies, the district court must apply the conflict-of-law rules of the state in which it is located (i.e., the forum state).

rules.source_text:
Substantive Law
In a diversity action, the district court is required to apply state substantive law. To determine which state’s substantive law applies, the district court must apply the conflict-of-law rules of the state in which it is located (i.e., the forum state).
topic_path: Choice Of Law: The Erie Doctrine

rules.rule_statement: Concerning procedure in a diversity action, when a procedural issue is addressed by a valid federal law (e.g., a statute or Federal Rule of Evidence), then federal law is applied, even if a state rule or statute is in conflict. If no federal law applies, then the general rule is that the district court must follow state law regarding substance. Under certain circumstances, the district court can choose to ignore state law concerning procedure.

Procedural Law

If no federal law applies, then the general rule is that the district court must follow state law regarding substance. Under certain circumstances, the district court can choose to ignore state law concerning procedure.

rules.rule_statement: When applying the Erie doctrine, it is sometimes difficult to determine whether an issue involves substance or procedure. The following general approach is often used: i) The district court starts by determining whether there is a conflict between state and federal law regarding the issue before the court. If no conflict exists, then the analysis does not need to proceed any further because the court can apply state and federal law harmoniously to the issue. ii) However, if the applicable state and federal laws conflict, then the district court must ask whether a valid federal statute or Federal Rule covers the disputed issue. a) If there is a valid federal statute or rule of procedure on point, then the district court must apply federal law rather than state law. b) Before applying a Federal Rule (rather than a federal statute) that is on point, the court must determine whether the Federal Rule is valid under the Rules Enabling Act. Specifically, the court must ask whether the Federal Rule abridges, enlarges, or modifies any substantive right. 1) If the Federal Rule does not abridge, enlarge, or modify any substantive right, then the Federal Rule must be applied. 2) If the Federal Rule can be considered to abridge, enlarge, or modify a substantive right, then the court must determine whether the Federal Rule only incidentally affects a litigant’s substantive rights. A Federal Rule that only incidentally affects substantive rights is valid under the Rules Enabling Act and will be applied by the court. c) If no federal statute or rule is on point, then the court must determine whether federal common law, rather than state law, should be applied. In making this determination regarding federal common law, the district court will ask whether the failure to apply state law will lead to different outcomes in state and federal court. 2) If the answer is yes, then the court will apply state law, unless affirmative countervailing federal interests are at stake that warrant application of federal law. See § IV.C. Federal Common Law, infra. d) The court may also choose to examine the issue by weighing the interests of the state and federal judiciaries and applying the law whose policy is of greater importance. e) Finally, to prevent forum-shopping (i.e., selecting a court based on where the plaintiff believes there will be a favorable outcome), the court will follow state law if selecting state law would prevent overcrowding of the federal court system. Examples of procedural and substantive issues under Erie doctrine Filing deadlines Court rules and procedures Discovery practices Procedural Rules of evidence (processes and Whether an issue is legal or equitable procedures) Allocation of functions between judge and jury Assessment of attorney’s fees Elements of claim or defense Substantive Burdens of proof (legal rights and duties) Statutes of limitations and tolling provisions Hanna v. Plumer, 380 U.S. 460 (1965). 28 U.S.C. § 2072. Burlington N.R. Co. v. Woods, 480 U.S. 1 (1987). Guar. Trust Co. v. York, 326 U.S. 99 (1945). Byrd v. Blue Ridge Rural Elec. Co-op, Inc., 356 U.S. 525 (1958). Hanna v. Plumer, 380 U.S. 460 (1965). Simler v. Conner, 372 U.S. 221 (1963). Byrd v. Blue Ridge Rural Elec. Co-op., Inc., 356 U.S. 525 (1958) (holding that if there is a jury in a diversity case on a state-law claim, then the jury, rather than a judge, will decide all factual issues in the case, regardless of whether state law would provide otherwise). Chambers v. NASCO, Inc., 501 U.S. 32 (1991) (holding that federal courts sitting in diversity can use their inherent power to assess attorney fees as sanction for bad-faith conduct, regardless of applicable state law). Bank of Am. Nat’l Trust & Sav. Ass’n v. Parnell, 352 U.S. 29 (1956). Guar. Trust Co. v. York, 326 U.S. 99 (1945).

rules.rule_statement: When it is appropriate under the Erie doctrine to apply federal law (e.g., a federal question claim or a procedural issue in diversity case), the court may look to federal statutes, constitutional provisions, and federal common law as a source of the law. Federal common law is created when no applicable federal statute or constitutional provision exists. Therefore, the court must resort to source materials of the common law. No general federal common law exists, and the general areas to which federal common law is applicable will be limited.
rules.short_rule_statement: When it is appropriate under the Erie doctrine to apply federal law (e.g., a federal question claim or a procedural issue in diversity case), the court may look to federal...
rules.source_text:
Federal Common Law
When it is appropriate under the Erie doctrine to apply federal law (e.g., a federal question claim or a procedural issue in diversity case), the court may look to federal statutes, constitutional provisions, and federal common law as a source of the law. Federal common law is created when no applicable federal statute or constitutional provision exists. Therefore, the court must resort to source materials of the common law. No general federal common law exists, and the general areas to which federal common law is applicable will be limited.

rules.rule_statement: The Supreme Court has also recognized the application of federal common law when a uniquely federal interest is at stake and a significant conflict exists between that interest and the operation of state law.
rules.short_rule_statement: The Supreme Court has also recognized the application of federal common law when a uniquely federal interest is at stake and a significant conflict exists between that interest...

Diversity Cases
The Supreme Court has also recognized the application of federal common law when a uniquely federal interest is at stake and a significant conflict exists between that interest and the operation of state law.

rules.rule_statement: Federal common law may also apply in state court. If state jurisdiction is concurrent with federal question jurisdiction and federal common law would have applied in federal court, then it will also apply in state court. See § I.B.2. Concurrent Versus Exclusive Jurisdiction, supra. See, e.g., Boyle v. United Technologies Corp., 487 U.S. 500 (1988) (a federal defense contractor could assert a defense recognized under federal common law to a state products liability action brought under diversity jurisdiction because the unique interests of the U.S. government in contracting for the procurement of military equipment were directly affected and a significant conflict existed with state law); Clearfield Trust Co. v. United States, 318 U.S. 363 (1943) (federal common law governed the federal government’s liability for a check issued by the United States that was later stolen and cashed because it was important to have a uniform standard for determining liability on such commercial paper). See Semtek Int’l Inc. v. Lockheed Martin Corp., 531 U.S. 497 (2001).

United States, 318 U.S. 363 (1943) (federal common law governed the federal government’s liability for a check issued by the United States that was later stolen and cashed because it was important to have a uniform standard for determining liability on such com

rules.rule_statement: After determining that state substantive law should apply, a U.S. district court with diversity jurisdiction generally must apply the conflict-of-law rules of the forum state to determine which state’s substantive law applies. Example: A U.S. District Court located in Delaware that is sitting in diversity must apply Delaware’s conflict-of-law rules to determine which state’s substantive law applies to the action before it. However, if a district court transfers a diversity case, then the district court to which the case is transferred must apply the law that would have been applied in the district court that transferred the case. Example: A claim is initially brought in a U.S. District Court located in Delaware that is sitting in diversity. Although venue was proper, the claim is transferred to a U.S. District Court located in California for the convenience of the parties. The U.S. District Court located in California still must apply Delaware’s conflict-of-law rules to determine which state’s substantive law applies to the action before it.

rules.canonical_name: Highest State Court’S Rulings On Substantive Law Control
rules.rule_statement: In determining a state’s substantive law, the U.S. district court will be bound by the rulings of the state’s highest court.
rules.short_rule_statement: In determining a state’s substantive law, the U.S. district court will be bound by the rulings of the state’s highest court.

rules.canonical_name: Highest State Court Not Yet Ruled
rules.rule_statement: If the state’s highest court has not spoken on an issue, the federal court must try to determine how the state’s highest court would rule on the issue if it were to consider it. To make this determination, a federal court will generally look to any lower state-court decisions that have considered the issue. The federal court will follow a lower court’s view unless it believes that the highest state court would not follow it. If no state court has considered the issue, then the federal court will have to determine how it believes the highest court in the state would rule if it looked at the issue today. Some states have procedures that allow the federal district court to certify a question of substantive law to the state supreme court for clarification.

The federal court will follow a lower court’s view unless it believes that the highest state court would not follow it. If no state court has considered the issue, then the federal court will have to determine how it believes the highest court in the state would rule if it looked at the issue today. rules.rule_statement: In diversity actions, a U.S. district court is bound by the conflict-of-laws rules of the state where the district court is located, but only to the extent that the state’s rules are valid under the Full Faith and Credit and Due Process Clauses of the U.S. Constitution. State conflict-of-laws rules frequently determine whether to apply the law of the forum state or the law of a foreign jurisdiction by considering whether the law to be applied is substantive or procedural. States apply their own procedural laws and sometimes apply the substantive law of a foreign jurisdiction. EXAM NOTE: Remember that although the substance-procedure distinction also arises in federal-state conﬂicts under Erie, it is not the same substance-procedure distinction that arises in state-state conﬂicts under Klaxon.  Some issues (e.g., burden of proof) are treated differently depending on whether they arise in a federal-state or state-state conﬂict. Questions about the following issues are generally considered procedural and controlled by the law of the forum state:

rules.rule_statement: xi) The burden of proof; xii) Trial procedure; and xiii) The methods of enforcing a judgment. California Distinction: California Conflict-of-Law Rules A federal court sitting in California must apply California’s conflict-of-laws rules. Real Property Disputes: Real property within California is governed by California law, except where the title is owned by the United States government. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 755. Tort Claims (Government Interest Approach): The court first determines whether the laws of two or more states are identical. If not, the court evaluates whether each state has an interest in applying its own law. If each state has an interest, an actual conflict exists. The court then analyzes the comparative impairment of each state’s interest if the other state’s law is applied. Kearney v. Salomon Smith Barney, Inc., 39 Cal. 4th 95 (2006). Contract Claims (Choice-of-Law Clauses): Even if a contract’s choice-of-law clause covers all claims, the court must nevertheless determine whether the chosen state law has a substantial relationship to the parties, their transaction, or any other reasonable basis. If the court determines that the clause is enforceable, it then inquires whether the chosen law conflicts with California public policy. If it does, the court must decide whether California or the chosen state has a materially greater interest in the determination of the particular issue. If the contract contains no choice-of-law clause, or the clause is unenforceable, the government interest approach applies. Washington Mut. Bank, FA v. Superior Court, 24 Cal. 4th 906, 916–17 (2001).

rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 3, a civil action is commenced by filing a complaint with the court clerk. In a federal diversity action, state law will apply to decide when the action commenced for the purposes of the statute of limitations. Therefore, if state law provides that an action is commenced by service of process on a defendant, then the state rule will control for the purposes of diversity jurisdiction.
rules.short_rule_statement: Under Rule 3, a civil action is commenced by filing a complaint with the court clerk. In a federal diversity action, state law will apply to decide when the action commenced...
rules.source_text:
Manner
Under Rule 3, a civil action is commenced by filing a complaint with the court clerk. In a federal diversity action, state law will apply to decide when the action commenced for the purposes of the statute of limitations. Therefore, if state law provides that an action is commenced by service of process on a defendant, then the state rule will control for the purposes of diversity jurisdiction.

rules.rule_statement: Rule 6 provides guidelines for computing time limits that apply throughout the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, unless otherwise provided.
rules.short_rule_statement: Rule 6 provides guidelines for computing time limits that apply throughout the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, unless otherwise provided.

Time Computation
Rule 6 provides guidelines for computing time limits that apply throughout the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, unless otherwise provided.

rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 6(a)(1), when a time period is stated in days, the period excludes the day of the event that triggers the period, but it includes every day following, including intervening Saturdays, Sundays, and legal holidays. If the last day of the period is a Saturday, Sunday, or legal holiday, then the period is extended to include the next nonweekend or nonholiday day. Rule 6(a)(1).

If the last day of the period is a Saturday, Sunday, or legal holiday, then the period is extended to include the next nonweekend or nonholiday day. Rule 6(a)(1).
