rules.rule_statement: Service of process refers to the delivery of the summons and complaint upon the defendant. Rule 4 sets forth the procedure for service of process in a federal action, detailing the form, content, method of issuance, methods of service, and time of service of a summons and complaint. In the absence of service of process (or waiver of service by the defendant), a court ordinarily may not exercise power over a party named as a defendant in a complaint. Methods of service on individuals Serve on the defendant personally Deliver to the defendant’s usual place of abode with a Inside the U.S. person of suitable age and discretion residing there or Deliver to an authorized agent In any internationally agreed upon manner reasonably calculated to give notice, or: Any method permitted by the foreign country; Personal service on the individual, unless the foreign country’s law prohibits it: Outside the U.S. Any form of mail that (1) the clerk addresses and sends to the individual and (2) requires a signed receipt, unless prohibited by foreign country; or Any other means not prohibited by international agreement, as ordered by the court Methods of service on business organizations Delivering the summons to: An officer Inside the U.S. A managing agent or general agent An agent appointed or authorized by law to receive process Any method available for service on an individual outside the U.S., Outside the U.S. except personal delivery California Distinction: Service of Process Substituted service: When substituted service is made in California (i.e., service made by leaving the summons and complaint at the defendant's home or office with a person over the age of 18), the state requires a follow up mailing to the defendant, which is not required in federal court. Substituted service on a defendant entity may be used at the option of the plaintiff and does not require failed attempts to personally serve. Substituted service on an individual defendant, however, may only be used after the plaintiff has attempted to effect personal service with reasonable diligence. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 415.20. Service by publication: California also authorizes service by publication if the defendant cannot be served by reasonable diligence. This method requires the plaintiff to publish the summons in a newspaper once a week for four successive weeks, unless the court orders publication for a longer period. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 415.50. Notice and Acknowledgment: Additionally, California authorizes service by Notice and Acknowledgment of Receipt. This method allows the plaintiff to mail the summons and complaint to the defendant along with a form titled "Notice and Acknowledgment of Receipt." The defendant then signs, dates, and mails the form back to the plaintiff. The summons and complaint are deemed "served" on the date the defendant puts on the Acknowledgment. If a defendant refuses to sign and return the Acknowledgment within 20 days of receipt, the plaintiff can affect service via another method and the defendant will be responsible for the cost. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 415.30. Service on out-of-state defendants: In addition to the methods of service authorized on in-state defendants, service of process on a person outside of California may be made by first class mail requiring a return receipt so long as a proper statutory and constitutional basis for personal jurisdiction exists. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 415.40. Methods of service on government agents and entities Deliver to the U.S. Attorney (or a designee) for the district where the action is brought, or send a copy by registered or certified mail to the civil-process clerk at the U.S. Attorney’s Office U.S. government Send a copy by registered or certified mail to (1) the U.S. Attorney General at Washington, D.C. and (2) the agency or officer, if the action challenges an order of a nonparty agency or officer of the United States Serve the United States and Federal agency or officer or employee sued in Deliver by registered or certified mail to the federal official capacity agency, officer, or employee Serve the U.S. government and Officer or employee sued individually Serve the officer or employee as an individual Deliver to the chief executive officer or State or local government Follow the state’s law for serving a summons or like process on a defendant Follow any arrangement for service between the plaintiff and foreign government or Foreign government If no arrangement exists, deliver by an applicable international convention on the service of judicial documents Exam Note In an action brought in a court of general jurisdiction in the state where the district court is located or where service is made, service may be made on any individual or business organization within the United States by following the state’s law for serving a summons in an action. Rule 4(h)(1)(A). If a Civil Rules emergency is declared pursuant to Rule 87 (e.g., public health emergency), a federal court may authorize service of process on (1) an individual located in the United States, (2) a corporation or association located in the United States, (3) the United States government, its agencies, corporations, officers, or employees; a state or local government, or (4) a minor or incompetent person located in the United States by a method that is reasonably calculated to give notice. Rule 87(c)(1).

rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 4(d), a competent individual, corporation, or association that is subject to service has a duty to avoid unnecessary expenses of serving the summons by waiving service. The plaintiff may notify such a defendant that an action has been commenced and request that the defendant waive service of the summons. Waiver of service may not be requested if the defendant is a government entity, a minor, or incompetent.

rules.source_text:
Waiver Of Service
Under Rule 4(d), a competent individual, corporation, or association that is subject to service has a duty to avoid unnecessary expenses of serving the summons by waiving service. The plaintiff may notify such a defendant that an action has been commenced and request that the defendant waive service of the summons. Waiver of service may not be requested if the defendant is a government entity, a minor, or incompetent.

rules.rule_statement: A plaintiff’s notice and request for waiver of service must be in writing and be addressed to the individual defendant, or, for a corporation, to an officer, managing or general agent, or any other agent authorized by appointment or by law to receive service of process. The notice must be accompanied by a copy of the complaint, two copies of a waiver form, and a prepaid means for returning the form. Additionally, the notice must give the defendant a reasonable time of at least 30 days after the request was sent (or at least 60 days if sent to a foreign defendant) to return the waiver. Rule 4(d)(1).
rules.short_rule_statement: A plaintiff’s notice and request for waiver of service must be in writing and be addressed to the individual defendant, or, for a corporation, to an officer, managing or...
rules.source_text:
Request For Waiver
A plaintiff’s notice and request for waiver of service must be in writing and be addressed to the individual defendant, or, for a corporation, to an officer, managing or general agent, or any other agent authorized by appointment or by law to receive service of process. The notice must be accompanied by a copy of the complaint, two copies of a waiver form, and a prepaid means for returning the form. Additionally, the notice must give the defendant a reasonable time of at least 30 days after the request was sent (or at least 60 days if sent to a foreign defendant) to return the waiver. Rule 4(d)(1).

rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 4(d)(3), if a defendant timely returns a waiver of service before being served with process, then the defendant’s deadline to serve an answer to the complaint extends from 21 days to 60 days after the notice was sent. If the defendant is outside the United States and waives service, the time period for serving an answer is extended to 90 days after the notice was sent. If the defendant agrees to waive service, then the date on which the plaintiff files the waiver form with the court is deemed the date of service. Rule 4(d)(4). California Distinction: Effect of Waiver Waiving service in California does not extend a defendant’s time to respond to a complaint (unlike in the federal system).
rules.short_rule_statement: Under Rule 4(d)(3), if a defendant timely returns a waiver of service before being served with process, then the defendant’s deadline to serve an answer to the complaint...
rules.source_text:
The Effect Of A Waiver
Under Rule 4(d)(3), if a defendant timely returns a waiver of service before being served with process, then the defendant’s deadline to serve an answer to the complaint extends from 21 days to 60 days after the notice was sent. If the defendant is outside the United States and waives service, the time period for serving an answer is extended to 90 days after the notice was sent. If the defendant agrees to waive service, then the date on which the plaintiff files the waiver form with the court is deemed the date of service. Rule 4(d)(4). California Distinction: Effect of Waiver Waiving service in California does not extend a defendant’s time to respond to a complaint (unlike in the federal system).

rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 4(l)(1), if formal service is not waived, then the process server must submit proof of service to the court. Generally, this proof will be by an affidavit of the process server. However, a failure to make proof of service does not affect the validity of the service.
rules.short_rule_statement: Under Rule 4(l)(1), if formal service is not waived, then the process server must submit proof of service to the court. Generally, this proof will be by an affidavit of the...
rules.source_text:
Proof Of Service
Under Rule 4(l)(1), if formal service is not waived, then the process server must submit proof of service to the court. Generally, this proof will be by an affidavit of the process server. However, a failure to make proof of service does not affect the validity of the service.

rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 12(b), the defenses of insufficiency of process and insufficiency of service of process must be asserted in a responsive pleading or by motion before a responsive pleading is submitted. A party’s failure to object waives these objections. Rule 12(h). Rule 4(d)(5) specifically provides that waiver of service does not waive any objection to personal jurisdiction or to venue. Murphy Bros., Inc. v. Michetti Pipe Stringing, Inc., 526 U.S. 344 (1999).
rules.short_rule_statement: Under Rule 12(b), the defenses of insufficiency of process and insufficiency of service of process must be asserted in a responsive pleading or by motion before a responsive...
rules.source_text:
Waiver Of Objections
Under Rule 12(b), the defenses of insufficiency of process and insufficiency of service of process must be asserted in a responsive pleading or by motion before a responsive pleading is submitted. A party’s failure to object waives these objections. Rule 12(h). Rule 4(d)(5) specifically provides that waiver of service does not waive any objection to personal jurisdiction or to venue. Murphy Bros., Inc. v. Michetti Pipe Stringing, Inc., 526 U.S. 344 (1999).

rules.rule_statement: Rule An injunction is a form of equitable relief mandating that a defendant perform a specified act or prohibiting a defendant from performing a specified act. Rule 65 governs the issuance of an injunction. Under Rule 65(d), every order granting injunctive relief must: State the reasons for its issuance Specifically state its terms and Specify the acts that the defendant is restrained from performing or required to perform. Additionally, to protect the adverse party against harm from an erroneous grant of preliminary relief, the Rule expresses a preference for the moving party to post an injunction bond. Rule 65(c). An injunction is considered an extraordinary remedy, only to be granted in limited circumstances.
rules.short_rule_statement: Rule An injunction is a form of equitable relief mandating that a defendant perform a specified act or prohibiting a defendant from performing a specified act. Rule 65 governs...
rules.source_text:
Injunctions
Rule An injunction is a form of equitable relief mandating that a defendant perform a specified act or prohibiting a defendant from performing a specified act. Rule 65 governs the issuance of an injunction. Under Rule 65(d), every order granting injunctive relief must: State the reasons for its issuance Specifically state its terms and Specify the acts that the defendant is restrained from performing or required to perform. Additionally, to protect the adverse party against harm from an erroneous grant of preliminary relief, the Rule expresses a preference for the moving party to post an injunction bond. Rule 65(c). An injunction is considered an extraordinary remedy, only to be granted in limited circumstances.

rules.rule_statement: There are two types of injunctions: mandatory and prohibitory. The determining factor is the type of conduct that is affected by the injunction, if granted. A mandatory injunction, which is a specific relief, requires a person to engage in an affirmative act that typically changes the status quo. When the injunction is directed at providing mandatory relief, as opposed to preserving the status quo, the moving party’s burden is particularly heavy. A prohibitory injunction, which is a form of preventive relief, restrains or prohibits a party from engaging in a specified behavior.
rules.short_rule_statement: There are two types of injunctions: mandatory and prohibitory. The determining factor is the type of conduct that is affected by the injunction, if granted. A mandatory...
rules.source_text:
Types Of Injunctions
There are two types of injunctions: mandatory and prohibitory. The determining factor is the type of conduct that is affected by the injunction, if granted. A mandatory injunction, which is a specific relief, requires a person to engage in an affirmative act that typically changes the status quo. When the injunction is directed at providing mandatory relief, as opposed to preserving the status quo, the moving party’s burden is particularly heavy. A prohibitory injunction, which is a form of preventive relief, restrains or prohibits a party from engaging in a specified behavior.

rules.rule_statement: A temporary restraining order (TRO) preserves the status quo of the parties until there is an opportunity to hold a full hearing on the application for a preliminary injunction. This interlocutory injunction may remain in effect only a limited number of days, to be set by the court, and no longer than 14 days unless good cause exists or the adversary consents. Rule 65(b)(2). TROs are not generally immediately appealable, unless they have the effect of an injunction, as when the court extends the length of the TRO beyond the time limit specified in the Rules. California Distinction: TRO In California, a TRO issued without notice to the defendant generally cannot exceed 15 days. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 527.
rules.short_rule_statement: A temporary restraining order (TRO) preserves the status quo of the parties until there is an opportunity to hold a full hearing on the application for a preliminary...
rules.source_text:
Temporary Restraining Order
A temporary restraining order (TRO) preserves the status quo of the parties until there is an opportunity to hold a full hearing on the application for a preliminary injunction. This interlocutory injunction may remain in effect only a limited number of days, to be set by the court, and no longer than 14 days unless good cause exists or the adversary consents. Rule 65(b)(2). TROs are not generally immediately appealable, unless they have the effect of an injunction, as when the court extends the length of the TRO beyond the time limit specified in the Rules. California Distinction: TRO In California, a TRO issued without notice to the defendant generally cannot exceed 15 days. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 527.

rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 65(b)(1), a TRO may issue without notice to the adverse party if: i) The moving party can establish in an affidavit or a verified complaint that immediate and irreparable injury will result before hearing the adverse party’s opposition; and ii) The movant’s attorney certifies in writing any efforts made to give notice and the reason notice should not be required.

When Notice Is Not Required

rules.rule_statement: If a TRO is issued without notice, the adverse party may appear and move to dissolve or modify the TRO, but the adverse party must give the party who obtained the TRO two days’ notice unless a shorter time is set by the court. Rule 65(b)(4).

Motion To Dissolve Or Modify
If a TRO is issued without notice, the adverse party may appear and move to dissolve or modify the TRO, but the adverse party must give the party who obtained the TRO two days’ notice unless a shorter time is set by the court. Rule 65(b)(4).

rules.rule_statement: A preliminary injunction is a form of relief issued before a full hearing on the merits, but only upon notice to the defendant and a hearing on whether the injunction should issue. Should an expedited decision on the merits be appropriate, a court may order a trial on the merits to be consolidated with the preliminary injunction hearing. Rule 65(a). A plaintiff seeking a preliminary injunction must establish that:

Preliminary Injunction
A preliminary injunction is a form of relief issued before a full hearing on the merits, but only upon notice to the defendant and a hearing on whether the injunction should issue. Should an expedited decision on the merits be appropriate, a court may order a trial on the merits to be consolidated with the preliminary injunction hearing. Rule 65(a). A plaintiff seeking a preliminary injunction must establish that:

rules.canonical_name: The Plaintiff Is Likely To Succeed On The Merits

rules.canonical_name: The Likelihood That The Plaintiff Will Prevail On The Merits;
rules.rule_statement: ii) The relative balance of harms that is likely to result from the granting or denial of interim injunctive relief. The court's evaluation of the relative balance of harms typically compares the interim harm the plaintiff is likely to sustain if the injunction is denied to the harm the defendant is likely to suffer if the preliminary injunction is issued. These two factors are described as "interrelated" because the greater the plaintiff's showing on one, the less must be shown on the other. White v. Davis, 30 Cal.4th 528 (2003); Butt v. State of California, 4 Cal.4th 668 (1992).

rules.rule_statement: A permanent injunction is a judicial determination on the merits of an application for relief. Once issued, it continues until dissolved by the court, but any affected person may move for modification or dissolution. The standard for a permanent injunction is essentially the same as for a preliminary injunction except that the plaintiff must show actual success on the merits.
rules.short_rule_statement: A permanent injunction is a judicial determination on the merits of an application for relief. Once issued, it continues until dissolved by the court, but any affected person...
rules.source_text:
Permanent Injunction
A permanent injunction is a judicial determination on the merits of an application for relief. Once issued, it continues until dissolved by the court, but any affected person may move for modification or dissolution. The standard for a permanent injunction is essentially the same as for a preliminary injunction except that the plaintiff must show actual success on the merits.

rules.rule_statement: Rule The complaint is the initial pleading in an action filed by the plaintiff and serves as notice to the opposing party. Under Rule 8(a), a complaint (or any pleading in which a claim is made) is generally required to include: A short and plain statement of the grounds that establish the court’s subject matter jurisdiction; A short and plain statement of the claim establishing entitlement to relief; and A demand for judgment for the relief sought by the pleader. California Distinction: Complaints in California State Courts California state courts require fact pleading—unlike the Federal Rules, which allow notice pleading. Fact pleading requires the plaintiff to allege all material, operative facts for the cause of action. Davaloo v. State Farm Ins. Co., 37 Cal. Rptr. 3d 528 (Ct. App. 2005); Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 425.10. All pleadings must contain (1) a statement of facts constituting each cause of action and (2) a demand for relief. The amount of damages must be stated if damages are sought, except in personal injury or wrongful death cases seeking actual or punitive damages. In these cases, the defendant may file a request for specificity as to the nature and amount of damages. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 425.10-11.

rules.rule_statement: A complaint in federal court must contain an allegation of the subject matter jurisdiction of the court unless the court already has jurisdiction and the claim needs no new jurisdictional support.
rules.short_rule_statement: A complaint in federal court must contain an allegation of the subject matter jurisdiction of the court unless the court already has jurisdiction and the claim needs no new...
rules.source_text:
Subject Matter Jurisdiction
A complaint in federal court must contain an allegation of the subject matter jurisdiction of the court unless the court already has jurisdiction and the claim needs no new jurisdictional support.

rules.rule_statement: Rule 8(a)(2) generally requires only “a short and plain statement of the claim showing that the pleader is entitled to relief” to “give the defendant fair notice of what [the] plaintiff’s claim is and the grounds upon which it rests.” Detailed factual allegations are not required, but a party must plead facts sufficient to show that the claim has substantive plausibility. A claimant may not merely recite the elements of a cause of action with broad, conclusory statements. A complaint should not be dismissed for an imperfect statement of the legal theory. See § V.E.2. Motion to Dismiss for Failure to State a Claim Upon Which Relief Can Be Granted, infra. Rule 9 provides certain special rules regarding pleading, which apply not just in the context of a complaint, but to any pleading. In certain circumstances (described as follows), a party is required to plead with greater detail than under the general rules. Claim Special pleading requirement Need not allege a party’s capacity to sue or be sued unless required to establish jurisdiction Capacity Must challenge capacity by specific denial stating any supporting facts that are peculiarly within the party’s knowledge Malice, intent, knowledge, or other mental condition may be alleged generally Fraud, mistake, mental condition Circumstances giving rise to fraud/mistake must be stated with particularity Assertion that conditions precedent occurred may be alleged generally Conditions precedent Denial that conditions precedent occurred must be stated with particularity Sufficient to allege that the document was legally Official document or act issued or the act was legally done Judgment Sufficient to plead the judgment without showing any jurisdiction to render it General damages may be alleged generally Damages Special damages must be specifically stated Time and place Relevant and material facts regarding time and place must be specified in detail Federal courts have no power to require more specific pleading beyond following the rules provided in the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure or by statute.

rules.rule_statement: A plaintiff’s complaint may include relief in the alternative and seek different types of relief (e.g., monetary damages, equitable relief, or a declaratory judgment). As a general matter, a demand for judgment in a contested case does not limit the nature or scope of relief that the trial court may grant. The plaintiff is entitled to whatever relief is appropriate for the claims alleged in the complaint and proved at trial. The exception to this general rule concerns default judgments, as a default judgment cannot “differ in kind from, or exceed in amount, what is demanded in the pleadings.” Rule 54(c). Conley v. Gibson, 355 U.S. 41, 47 (1957). See Swierkiewicz v. Sorema N.A., 534 U.S. 506 (2002).

Demand For Relief
A plaintiff’s complaint may include relief in the alternative and seek different types of relief (e.g., monetary damages, equitable relief, or a declaratory judgment). As a general matter, a demand for judgment in a contested case does not limit the nature or scope of relief that the trial court may grant. The plaintiff is entitled to whatever relief is appropriate for the claims alleged in the complaint and proved at trial. The exception to this general rule concerns default judgments, as a default judgment cannot “differ in kind from, or exceed in amount, what is demanded in the pleadings.” Rule 54(c). Conley v. Gibson, 355 U.S. 41, 47 (1957). See Swierkiewicz v. Sorema N.A., 534 U.S. 506 (2002).

rules.rule_statement: Rule Under Rule 12, within 21 days of service of process, a defendant must respond to a complaint either by an answer or by a pre-answer motion, or the defendant must seek additional time to answer. The defendant risks a default for failing to take one of these steps.

Before filing an answer, a defendant may file a motion under Rule 12(b), raising any or all of the following defenses:

Lack Of Subject Matter Jurisdiction
ii) Lack of personal jurisdiction; iii) Improper venue; iv) Insufficient process;

rules.rule_statement: vi) Failure to state a claim upon which relief can be granted; and vii) Failure to join a necessary or indispensable party under Rule 19. Such motions generally seek dismissal of the claim. For the defenses of insufficient process and service of process, it is common to make a motion to quash the service of process or the process itself. A motion to quash is a request that a court nullify or void a legal action, e.g., find that service of process was invalid or a subpoena was improperly issued.

A motion to quash is a request that a court nullify or void a legal action, e.g., find that service of process was invalid or a subpoena was improperly issued.

rules.rule_statement: Rule 12 defense Timing Lack of subject matter jurisdiction May be raised at any time Lack of personal jurisdiction Must be raised: Improper venue In a pre-answer motion or Insufficient service or process If no pre-answer motion is made, within the time for amending the answer as of right Failure to state a claim upon which May be raised: relief can be granted In any pleading Failure to join a necessary or In a motion for judgment on the indispensable party under Rule 19 pleading; or At trial Except for lack of subject matter jurisdiction, the consequence of not asserting these defenses in time provided is that the defense will be waived. Rule 12(h). Although Rule 12(b) states that a motion asserting one of these defenses must be made before pleading if a responsive pleading is allowed, courts have concluded, based on the waiver provision in Rule 12(h)(1), that a motion to dismiss based on one of these defenses may be filed after an answer is filed if the answer pleads one of these defenses. Under the omnibus motion rule, when a party makes a pre-answer motion raising one of these defenses but omitting the others, the party may not make another pre-answer motion raising one of the omitted defenses that was available to the party when the earlier motion was filed. The party is deemed to have waived the excluded defenses. Rule 12(g)(2), (h)(1)(A). California Distinction: California Challenges to the Complaint A defendant may object to the allegations in a complaint by filing (1) an answer, or (2) either a general or special demurrer to the entire complaint or any of its causes of action. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 430.10. This is similar to Federal Rule 12(b). General demurrer: The grounds for a general demurrer are:

rules.canonical_name: Failure To Plead Whether A Contract Is Written Or Oral;
rules.rule_statement: vi) Failure to file certain certificates. Before filing a demurrer to a pleading, the demurring party must meet and confer with the pleading party to determine whether an agreement can be reached that would resolve the anticipated objections. If an amended pleading is filed, the responding party must meet and confer again with the pleading party before filing a demurrer to the amended pleading. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 430.41. A court ruling on a demurrer may consider only the material allegations of the complaint and matters subject to judicial notice. A plaintiff may file a demurrer to a defendant’s answer. Motion to Quash Service of Summons: A defendant who objects to the court’s exercise of personal jurisdiction must do so at the earliest opportunity, usually by a motion to quash service of summons. When this occurs before filing an answer, demurrer, or motion to strike, the defendant is not deemed to have made a general appearance. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 418.10.

rules.rule_statement: The facts alleged in the complaint must “raise a right to relief above the speculative level... on the assumption that all the allegations in the complaint are true (even if doubtful in fact).” The complaint must state enough facts to raise a reasonable expectation that discovery will reveal evidence of the necessary element.

rules.source_text:
More Than Speculation
The facts alleged in the complaint must “raise a right to relief above the speculative level... on the assumption that all the allegations in the complaint are true (even if doubtful in fact).” The complaint must state enough facts to raise a reasonable expectation that discovery will reveal evidence of the necessary element.

rules.rule_statement: In ruling on a motion to dismiss under Rule 12(b)(6), the court may consider only:
rules.short_rule_statement: In ruling on a motion to dismiss under Rule 12(b)(6), the court may consider only:
rules.source_text:
What The Court May Consider
In ruling on a motion to dismiss under Rule 12(b)(6), the court may consider only:

rules.rule_statement: ii) Any exhibits attached to the complaint; and iii) Any matters subject to judicial notice. If additional evidence presented to the court (e.g., affidavits) is not excluded from the court’s review, then the motion must be treated as a motion for summary judgment. In a summary judgment proceeding, all parties must be given an opportunity to present all material information for the court’s consideration. Rule 12(d). Note that a court also can consider other matters regarding a motion to dismiss under other Rule 12(b) defenses.

rules.source_text:
The Allegations In The Complaint
ii) Any exhibits attached to the complaint; and iii) Any matters subject to judicial notice. If additional evidence presented to the court (e.g., affidavits) is not excluded from the court’s review, then the motion must be treated as a motion for summary judgment. In a summary judgment proceeding, all parties must be given an opportunity to present all material information for the court’s consideration. Rule 12(d). Note that a court also can consider other matters regarding a motion to dismiss under other Rule 12(b) defenses.

rules.rule_statement: The U.S. Supreme Court has established a two-step analysis for adjudicating a motion to dismiss under Rule 12(b)(6). First, the court must identify and reject legal conclusions unsupported by factual allegations. This includes mere conclusory statements and assertions devoid of facts. For example, a complaint that alleges that a defendant caused an injury, without explanation as to how it occurred, does not meet the requirements of Rule 8(a) and, as a result, cannot survive a Rule 12(b)(6) motion. Second, the court should assume the truth or veracity of well-pleaded factual allegations and should include a “context specific” analysis that “draw[s] on [the Court’s] judicial experience and common sense” to determine whether the allegations “plausibly give rise to an entitlement of relief.”
rules.short_rule_statement: The U.S. Supreme Court has established a two-step analysis for adjudicating a motion to dismiss under Rule 12(b)(6). First, the court must identify and reject legal conclusions...
rules.source_text:
Two-Step Analysis
The U.S. Supreme Court has established a two-step analysis for adjudicating a motion to dismiss under Rule 12(b)(6). First, the court must identify and reject legal conclusions unsupported by factual allegations. This includes mere conclusory statements and assertions devoid of facts. For example, a complaint that alleges that a defendant caused an injury, without explanation as to how it occurred, does not meet the requirements of Rule 8(a) and, as a result, cannot survive a Rule 12(b)(6) motion. Second, the court should assume the truth or veracity of well-pleaded factual allegations and should include a “context specific” analysis that “draw[s] on [the Court’s] judicial experience and common sense” to determine whether the allegations “plausibly give rise to an entitlement of relief.”

rules.rule_statement: If a claim is dismissed, then the plaintiff may generally amend the pleading and continue the action. If the plaintiff does not wish to do so, then a judgment will be entered, and the plaintiff can appeal. If a defendant’s motion to dismiss is denied, then the defendant may either answer the claim or allow a default judgment to be entered and then appeal.
rules.short_rule_statement: If a claim is dismissed, then the plaintiff may generally amend the pleading and continue the action. If the plaintiff does not wish to do so, then a judgment will be entered,...
rules.source_text:
Outcome
If a claim is dismissed, then the plaintiff may generally amend the pleading and continue the action. If the plaintiff does not wish to do so, then a judgment will be entered, and the plaintiff can appeal. If a defendant’s motion to dismiss is denied, then the defendant may either answer the claim or allow a default judgment to be entered and then appeal.

rules.rule_statement: After the pleadings are closed, a party may move for judgment on the pleadings under Rule 12(c). A motion for judgment on the pleadings allows a court to dispose of a case when the material facts are not in dispute and a judgment on the merits can be achieved based on the content of the pleadings.
rules.short_rule_statement: After the pleadings are closed, a party may move for judgment on the pleadings under Rule 12(c). A motion for judgment on the pleadings allows a court to dispose of a case when...
rules.source_text:
In General
After the pleadings are closed, a party may move for judgment on the pleadings under Rule 12(c). A motion for judgment on the pleadings allows a court to dispose of a case when the material facts are not in dispute and a judgment on the merits can be achieved based on the content of the pleadings.

rules.rule_statement: A motion under Rule 12(c) must be made after an answer is filed. California Distinction: Meet and Confer Before Filing Motion Before filing a motion for judgment on the pleadings, the moving party must meet and confer with the pleading party to determine whether an agreement can be reached that resolves the anticipated claims. If an amended pleading is filed, the responding party must meet and confer again with the pleading party before filing a motion for judgment on the pleadings against the amended pleading. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 439.

Timing
A motion under Rule 12(c) must be made after an answer is filed. California Distinction: Meet and Confer Before Filing Motion Before filing a motion for judgment on the pleadings, the moving party must meet and confer with the pleading party to determine whether an agreement can be reached that resolves the anticipated claims. If an amended pleading is filed, the responding party must meet and confer again with the pleading party before filing a motion for judgment on the pleadings against the amended pleading. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 439.

rules.rule_statement: The standard for a motion under Rule 12(c) is generally the same as that for a motion under Rule 12(b)(6). Likewise, if matters outside the pleadings are not excluded from the court’s review, then the motion must be treated as a motion for summary judgment.

Standard
The standard for a motion under Rule 12(c) is generally the same as that for a motion under Rule 12(b)(6). Likewise, if matters outside the pleadings are not excluded from the court’s review, then the motion must be treated as a motion for summary judgment.

rules.rule_statement: If a pleading is so vague or ambiguous that a party cannot reasonably draft a required responsive pleading, then the responding party may move for a more definite statement. The motion must specify the defects in the pleading as well as the details sought by the party making the motion. Rule 12(e).
rules.short_rule_statement: If a pleading is so vague or ambiguous that a party cannot reasonably draft a required responsive pleading, then the responding party may move for a more definite statement....
rules.source_text:
Vague And Ambiguous
If a pleading is so vague or ambiguous that a party cannot reasonably draft a required responsive pleading, then the responding party may move for a more definite statement. The motion must specify the defects in the pleading as well as the details sought by the party making the motion. Rule 12(e).

rules.rule_statement: Courts are generally reluctant to grant a motion for a more definite statement because discovery is available to get more information about an issue. The standard for granting such a motion is whether the pleading provides enough information for the responding party to draft a responsive pleading and commence discovery. A motion for a more definite statement may be appropriate when the pleader fails to allege facts required to be specifically pleaded, such as allegations of fraud or mistake under Rule 9(b).
rules.short_rule_statement: Courts are generally reluctant to grant a motion for a more definite statement because discovery is available to get more information about an issue. The standard for granting...
rules.source_text:
Standard
Courts are generally reluctant to grant a motion for a more definite statement because discovery is available to get more information about an issue. The standard for granting such a motion is whether the pleading provides enough information for the responding party to draft a responsive pleading and commence discovery. A motion for a more definite statement may be appropriate when the pleader fails to allege facts required to be specifically pleaded, such as allegations of fraud or mistake under Rule 9(b).

rules.rule_statement: A party must make a motion for a more definite statement before filing a responsive pleading. The court may strike a party’s pleading if the party fails to respond to a court order granting this motion within 14 days of the notice of the order. Rule 12(e).
rules.short_rule_statement: A party must make a motion for a more definite statement before filing a responsive pleading. The court may strike a party’s pleading if the party fails to respond to a court...
rules.source_text:
Timing
A party must make a motion for a more definite statement before filing a responsive pleading. The court may strike a party’s pleading if the party fails to respond to a court order granting this motion within 14 days of the notice of the order. Rule 12(e).

rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 12(f), if a pleading contains any insufficient defense or redundant, immaterial, impertinent, or scandalous material, then the court may order that such defense or material be stricken. The court can take such action upon motion or upon the court’s initiative. Defenses that tend to complicate litigation significantly or that are insufficient at law may be stricken to avoid unnecessary time and money in litigating invalid and spurious issues.
rules.short_rule_statement: Under Rule 12(f), if a pleading contains any insufficient defense or redundant, immaterial, impertinent, or scandalous material, then the court may order that such defense or...
rules.source_text:
Rule 12(F)
Under Rule 12(f), if a pleading contains any insufficient defense or redundant, immaterial, impertinent, or scandalous material, then the court may order that such defense or material be stricken. The court can take such action upon motion or upon the court’s initiative. Defenses that tend to complicate litigation significantly or that are insufficient at law may be stricken to avoid unnecessary time and money in litigating invalid and spurious issues.

rules.rule_statement: When a responsive pleading is permitted, the responding party must move to strike before responding to such a pleading. When a responsive pleading is not permitted, the party must make a motion to strike within 21 days after service of the pleading.
rules.short_rule_statement: When a responsive pleading is permitted, the responding party must move to strike before responding to such a pleading. When a responsive pleading is not permitted, the party...
rules.source_text:
Timing
When a responsive pleading is permitted, the responding party must move to strike before responding to such a pleading. When a responsive pleading is not permitted, the party must make a motion to strike within 21 days after service of the pleading.

rules.rule_statement: Although not specifically provided for in the Rules, courts have generally allowed a party to amend a motion to dismiss to raise an omitted ground if the party acts promptly and before the court rules on the original motion. The courts reason that “an amendment made just a few days following the motion is consistent with the spirit of Rule 12(h) and neither prejudices the plaintiff nor burdens the court.” California Distinction: Two Types of Motions to Strike Motion to Strike: A party may move to strike any irrelevant, false, or improper matter included in a pleading, or any part of a pleading not filed in conformity with statute, rule, or court order. The motion must be filed in the time allowed to respond to a pleading (30 days). Cal. Civ. Proc. Code §§ 435–436. Before filing a motion to strike a pleading, the moving party must meet and confer with the pleading party to determine whether an agreement can be reached that resolves the anticipated objections. If an amended pleading is filed, the responding party must meet and confer again with the pleading party before filing a motion to strike the amended pleading. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 435.5. Anti-SLAPP Motion to Strike: Within 60 days of service of the complaint, a defendant may file an anti-SLAPP motion to strike a claim when the plaintiff has filed a “strategic lawsuit against public participation.” Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 425.16. The defendant must show that a cause of action arises from the right to free speech or to petition for the redress of grievances. The burden then shifts to the plaintiff to show a probability of prevailing on the claim. Anti-SLAPP motions are restricted to certain public interest lawsuits, class actions, and actions that arise from commercial statements or conduct. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 425.17. Royal Globe Ins. Co. v. Logicon, Inc., 487 F. Supp. 1245 (N.D. Ill. 1980) (lack of personal jurisdiction defense). Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544, 545 (2007). See, e.g., Thompson v. Chrysler Motors Corp., 755 F.2d 1162 (5th Cir. 1985) (in determining motion to dismiss for lack of personal jurisdiction, court can consider not only affidavits but also evidence obtained by discovery methods, such as depositions and interrogatories). Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009). See Anchor Hocking Corp. v. Jacksonville Elec. Auth., 419 F. Supp. 992. (M.D. Fla. 1976); SEC v. Gulf & Western Indus., Inc., 502 F. Supp. 343 (D.D.C. 1980). Friedman v. World Transportation, Inc., 636 F. Supp. 685 (N.D. Ill. 1986).

rules.rule_statement: An answer is a pleading by the defendant that responds to a plaintiff’s complaint. A plaintiff would also file an answer if responding to a defendant’s counterclaim.
rules.short_rule_statement: An answer is a pleading by the defendant that responds to a plaintiff’s complaint. A plaintiff would also file an answer if responding to a defendant’s counterclaim.
rules.source_text:
Answer
An answer is a pleading by the defendant that responds to a plaintiff’s complaint. A plaintiff would also file an answer if responding to a defendant’s counterclaim.

rules.rule_statement: The answer must admit or deny the allegations of the plaintiff’s complaint. Rule 8(b). If the defendant lacks knowledge or information sufficient to form a belief concerning the truth or falsity of an allegation, then the defendant must say so in the answer. This response has the effect of a denial under Rule 8(b). Before pleading lack of sufficient knowledge, the defendant must make a reasonable investigation into whether the information exists and how difficult it would be to ascertain.

rules.source_text:
Admissions Or Denials
The answer must admit or deny the allegations of the plaintiff’s complaint. Rule 8(b). If the defendant lacks knowledge or information sufficient to form a belief concerning the truth or falsity of an allegation, then the defendant must say so in the answer. This response has the effect of a denial under Rule 8(b). Before pleading lack of sufficient knowledge, the defendant must make a reasonable investigation into whether the information exists and how difficult it would be to ascertain.

rules.rule_statement: A specific denial is a denial of a particular paragraph or allegation in the complaint or other claim for relief (e.g., counterclaim, crossclaim, etc.). A party can respond to each paragraph of the complaint by:

Speciﬁc Denial
A specific denial is a denial of a particular paragraph or allegation in the complaint or other claim for relief (e.g., counterclaim, crossclaim, etc.). A party can respond to each paragraph of the complaint by:

rules.canonical_name: Denying The Allegation In The Paragraph
rules.rule_statement: ii) Admitting the allegation in the paragraph; iii) Pleading insufficient knowledge to either admit or deny it; or iv) Admitting part of the allegation and either denying or pleading insufficient knowledge concerning the rest.

rules.rule_statement: A general denial is a statement by a party that denies every allegation of the complaint. A general denial can be made only if the party intends in good faith to controvert all the allegations. Alternatively, a party could make a qualified general denial, stating that the party denies every allegation in the complaint, except certain specified allegations.

Alternatively, a party could make a qualified general denial, stating that the party denies every allegation in the complaint, except certain specified allegations.

If there are other affirmative defenses that the party has in addition to those listed (e.g., novation, qualified immunity), then they must be raised in the answer as well. A party is not required to choose among inconsistent defenses. All defenses must be raised in the answer. A failure to raise an affirmative defense constitutes a waiver of that defense. Rule 8(d).

rules.rule_statement: If a defendant has a claim against the plaintiff, then the defendant may state it as a counterclaim in the answer to the complaint. Under certain circumstances, a counterclaim will be compulsory (see § VI.D.2. Counterclaims, infra) under Rule 13. The counterclaim must be pleaded or it will be precluded in any future litigation. A party may state separate claims as well as defenses, regardless of consistency. Rule 8(d).

Counterclaims
If a defendant has a claim against the plaintiff, then the defendant may state it as a counterclaim in the answer to the complaint. Under certain circumstances, a counterclaim will be compulsory (see § VI.D.2. Counterclaims, infra) under Rule 13. The counterclaim must be pleaded or it will be precluded in any future litigation. A party may state separate claims as well as defenses, regardless of consistency. Rule 8(d).

rules.rule_statement: Rule 5 governs the service of a pleading other than the original complaint, including answers, unless there are numerous defendants and the court orders otherwise. Rule 5(a). If a party is represented by an attorney, service under this Rule must be made on the attorney unless the court orders service on the party. Rule 5(b)(1). rules.source_text:
Service Under Rule 5
Rule 5 governs the service of a pleading other than the original complaint, including answers, unless there are numerous defendants and the court orders otherwise. Rule 5(a). If a party is represented by an attorney, service under this Rule must be made on the attorney unless the court orders service on the party. Rule 5(b)(1). Service under Rule 5 may generally be accomplished by:

rules.rule_statement: ii) Leaving the pleading: a) At the person’s office with a clerk or someone in charge or, if no one is in charge, in a conspicuous place in the office; or b) If the person has no office or the office is closed, at the person’s dwelling or usual place of abode with someone of suitable age and discretion who resides there; iii) Mailing it to the person’s last known address; iv) Leaving it with the court clerk if the person has no known address; v) Sending it to a registered user by filing it with the court’s electronic-filing system or sending it by other electronic means that the person consented to in writing; or vi) Delivering it by any other means that the person consented to in writing. California Distinction: Service by Electronic Means A California court may order electronic service on a person represented by counsel who has appeared in an action or proceeding, regardless of whether that person has consented to that service. An unrepresented party cannot be served electronically unless the party has expressly consented. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 1010.6.

rules.rule_statement: If a motion is not made under Rule 12, then a defendant must serve an answer within 21 days after being served with the summons and complaint. Rule 12(a)(1)(A)(i). If the defendant has timely waived service under Rule 4(d), then the defendant must serve the answer within 60 days after the request for a waiver was sent, or within 90 days after it was sent to the defendant outside any judicial district of the United States. Rule 12(a)(1)(A)(ii).
rules.short_rule_statement: If a motion is not made under Rule 12, then a defendant must serve an answer within 21 days after being served with the summons and complaint. Rule 12(a)(1)(A)(i). If the...
rules.source_text:
No Motion Made Under Rule 12
If a motion is not made under Rule 12, then a defendant must serve an answer within 21 days after being served with the summons and complaint. Rule 12(a)(1)(A)(i). If the defendant has timely waived service under Rule 4(d), then the defendant must serve the answer within 60 days after the request for a waiver was sent, or within 90 days after it was sent to the defendant outside any judicial district of the United States. Rule 12(a)(1)(A)(ii).

rules.rule_statement: When a motion is made under Rule 12, a defendant will not have to serve an answer while the motion is pending. If the court denies or postpones disposition of the motion until a trial on the merits, then the answer must be served within 14 days after notice of the court’s action. Rule 12(a)(4)(A). If the court grants a motion for a more definite statement under Rule 12(e), then the answer must be served within 14 days after service of the more definite statement. California Distinction: Answer and Cross-Complaint in California State Court California law allows a defendant to file:

Motion Made Under Rule 12
When a motion is made under Rule 12, a defendant will not have to serve an answer while the motion is pending. If the court denies or postpones disposition of the motion until a trial on the merits, then the answer must be served within 14 days after notice of the court’s action. Rule 12(a)(4)(A). If the court grants a motion for a more definite statement under Rule 12(e), then the answer must be served within 14 days after service of the more definite statement. California Distinction: Answer and Cross-Complaint in California State Court California law allows a defendant to file:

rules.canonical_name: A General Denial, Which Denies Every Allegation Of The Complaint;
rules.rule_statement: ii) Specific denials, which deny specific portions of the complaint and can be made on information and belief. If the plaintiff files a verified complaint, the defendant must respond with a verified answer containing specific denials. Any material allegations not denied are deemed admitted. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code §§ 430.20–430.80. Timing: The answer must be filed within 30 days from the date of service of process. If the defendant’s demurrer or motion to strike is denied, an answer must be filed within 10 days of service of notice of the denial. Cross-complaint: Affirmative relief against the plaintiff must be sought in a separate cross-complaint, not in the answer. A cross-complaint in California includes what the federal system calls counterclaims, crossclaims, and third-party complaints. A defendant’s related cause of action against the plaintiff must be brought as a compulsory cross- complaint or it will be barred. Other unrelated causes of action may be brought by permissive cross-complaint.

rules.rule_statement: A reply is a response by the plaintiff to a defendant’s answer that is required only if ordered by the court. It can also be a response by a defendant to a plaintiff’s counterclaim answer, a third-party answer, or a crossclaim answer. A reply is made only when the court orders it. Rule 7(a)(7). In general, a party must serve a reply to an answer within 21 days after being served with an order to reply unless the order specifies a different time. Rule 12(a)(1)(C). California Distinction: Reply A California defendant may file a cross-complaint that alleges a related or unrelated cause of action against the plaintiff. In response, the plaintiff files a separate answer to the cross-complaint (instead of a reply).

Reply
A reply is a response by the plaintiff to a defendant’s answer that is required only if ordered by the court. It can also be a response by a defendant to a plaintiff’s counterclaim answer, a third-party answer, or a crossclaim answer. A reply is made only when the court orders it. Rule 7(a)(7). In general, a party must serve a reply to an answer within 21 days after being served with an order to reply unless the order specifies a different time. Rule 12(a)(1)(C). California Distinction: Reply A California defendant may file a cross-complaint that alleges a related or unrelated cause of action against the plaintiff. In response, the plaintiff files a separate answer to the cross-complaint (instead of a reply).

rules.rule_statement: Rule 15 provides the rules about when and how pleadings can be amended or supplemented.
rules.short_rule_statement: Rule 15 provides the rules about when and how pleadings can be amended or supplemented.
rules.source_text:
Amendments And Supplemental Pleadings
Rule 15 provides the rules about when and how pleadings can be amended or supplemented.

rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 15(a), a party may amend a pleading once as of right no later than 21 days after serving it if no responsive pleading is required. If a responsive pleading is required, a party may amend within 21 days of (1) service of the responsive pleading or (2) being served with a motion under Rule 12(b), whichever is earlier. In practical terms, Rule 15 means that a plaintiff may amend the complaint even after being served with an answer (up to 21 days), but the plaintiff is also limited to 21 days to amend after being served with a Rule 12(b) motion. A party may amend a pleading during and after a trial if:

rules.source_text:
By Right
Under Rule 15(a), a party may amend a pleading once as of right no later than 21 days after serving it if no responsive pleading is required. If a responsive pleading is required, a party may amend within 21 days of (1) service of the responsive pleading or (2) being served with a motion under Rule 12(b), whichever is earlier. In practical terms, Rule 15 means that a plaintiff may amend the complaint even after being served with an answer (up to 21 days), but the plaintiff is also limited to 21 days to amend after being served with a Rule 12(b) motion. A party may amend a pleading during and after a trial if:

rules.canonical_name: Amending The Pleading Will Conform To The Evidence;
rules.rule_statement: ii) The opposing party has an opportunity to prepare. Due process is required for the amended pleading.
rules.short_rule_statement: ii) The opposing party has an opportunity to prepare. Due process is required for the amended pleading.

rules.rule_statement: The court should freely give leave to amend a pleading when justice so requires. Rule 15(a)(2). As a general matter, the court will first determine if the proposed amendment to the pleading would be futile because it would immediately be subject to dismissal under Rule 12(b)(6). If it would not, the amendment will generally be permitted unless it would result in undue prejudice to the opposing party. However, when the court has issued an order regarding the trial plan after a final pretrial conference, which may include the issues for trial, the court may modify that order only to prevent manifest injustice. Rule 16(e).

By Leave Of The Court
The court should freely give leave to amend a pleading when justice so requires. Rule 15(a)(2). As a general matter, the court will first determine if the proposed amendment to the pleading would be futile because it would immediately be subject to dismissal under Rule 12(b)(6). If it would not, the amendment will generally be permitted unless it would result in undue prejudice to the opposing party. However, when the court has issued an order regarding the trial plan after a final pretrial conference, which may include the issues for trial, the court may modify that order only to prevent manifest injustice. Rule 16(e).

rules.canonical_name: At Any Time Before An Answer, Demurrer, Or Motion To Strike Is Filed;
rules.rule_statement: ii) After a demurrer or motion to strike is filed but before it is heard if the amended pleading is filed and served no later than the date for filing an opposition to the demurrer or motion to strike. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 472. The parties may stipulate to a party amending the complaint, cross-complaint, or answer after the date for filing an opposition. A court may also grant leave to amend after sustaining a demurrer or granting an order to strike. Otherwise, a party may amend only with the written consent of the adverse party or by leave of court upon a motion. Leave to amend is liberally granted. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 472.

rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 15(c)(1), an amendment to a pleading will relate back to the date of the original pleading when: i) The amendment asserts a claim or defense that arose out of the conduct, transaction, or occurrence set out, or attempted to be set out, in the original pleading; or ii) The law that provides the applicable statute of limitations allows relation back. California Distinction: Relation Back Two additional elements are required for an amendment to relate back to the date of the original pleading in California state court. First, the amended pleading must involve the same accident and injuries. Second, the amended pleading must refer to the same offending instrumentality. San Diego Gas & Elec. Co. v. Superior Court, 53 Cal. Rptr. 3d 722 (Ct. App. 2007). Under Rule 15(c)(1)(C), if the amendment changes the party or the naming of the party against whom a claim is asserted, then it will relate back to the date of the original pleading if: i) The amendment asserts a claim or defense that arose out of the conduct, transaction, or occurrence set out, or attempted to be set out, in the original pleading; ii) Within 90 days after the filing of the original complaint, the party to be brought in by amendment receives notice of the action such that the party will not be prejudiced in defending on the merits; and iii) The party to be brought in by amendment knew or should have known that the action would have been brought against him, but for a mistake concerning the proper party’s identity. The proper focus when applying the third element is on the defendant’s knowledge, rather than on information in the plaintiff’s possession. Although the plaintiff’s possession of information may be relevant, it is not dispositive. California Distinction: Cases of “Misnomer” California does not have a provision parallel to Fed. R. Civ. P. 15(c). However, California allows a plaintiff to correct a “misnomer” in naming a defendant, even when the statute of limitations has run. California also allows substituting a defendant’s true name for a fictitious “Doe” label on the complaint when: against all defendants; ii) The plaintiff was genuinely ignorant of the defendant’s identity, of facts giving rise to a cause of action, or that the law allows a cause of action; and iii) The plaintiff’s ignorance was pleaded in the complaint.

rules.rule_statement: A court has discretion under Rule 15(d) to permit supplemental pleadings that describe events occurring after the filing of an earlier pleading. The court may permit supplemental pleadings even though the original pleading is defective in stating a claim or defense.

Judicial Discretion
A court has discretion under Rule 15(d) to permit supplemental pleadings that describe events occurring after the filing of an earlier pleading. The court may permit supplemental pleadings even though the original pleading is defective in stating a claim or defense.

rules.rule_statement: Rule Rule 11 establishes the standards that attorneys and individual parties must meet when filing: Pleadings Motions Other papers filed with the court The Rule does not apply to mandatory disclosures and discovery requests, responses, and objections. Rule 11 provides for sanctions against parties, attorneys, and law firms for violations of the Rule. Mandatory disclosures and discovery requests, responses, and objections are subject to their own signature requirement (see § VII.B.6. Signature Requirement and Certification, infra) and enforcement provisions (see § VII.F. Enforcement, infra). Rule 11(d).

The court must strike an unsigned paper unless the omission is promptly corrected after it is brought to the attention of the attorney or party. The signature need not be verified (i.e., sworn to). Rule 11(a).

rules.rule_statement: By presenting to the court a pleading, written motion, or other paper, an attorney or unrepresented party certifies that to the best of their knowledge, information, and belief, formed after an inquiry reasonable under the circumstances: i) The paper is not being presented for any improper purpose, such as to harass, cause unnecessary delay, or needlessly increase the cost of litigation; ii) The claims, defenses, and other legal contentions are warranted by existing law or by a nonfrivolous argument for extending, modifying, or reversing existing law or for establishing new law; iii) The factual contentions have evidentiary support or, if specifically so identified, will likely have evidentiary support after a reasonable opportunity for further investigation or discovery; and iv) The denials of factual contentions are warranted on the evidence or, if specifically so identified, are reasonably based on belief or a lack of information. “Presenting” a pleading under Rule 11 includes “signing, filing, submitting, or later advocating” a position presented in the pleading. Rule 11(b).

rules.rule_statement: The sanctions process can be initiated by a party’s motion or by the court on its own initiative. A motion for sanctions must be made separately from any other motion and must describe the specific conduct alleged to violate Rule 11. The motion must first be served on the opposing party under Rule 5. The opposing party must then be given 21 days to withdraw or correct the challenged pleading. If this 21-day “safe harbor” period passes, and the opposing party fails to withdraw or correct the pleading, the party seeking sanctions may file the motion with the court. Rule 11(c)(2). On its own initiative, the court may order an attorney, a law firm, or a party to show cause why conduct specifically described in the order has not violated Rule 11. Rule 11(c)(3).

How Initiated
The sanctions process can be initiated by a party’s motion or by the court on its own initiative. A motion for sanctions must be made separately from any other motion and must describe the specific conduct alleged to violate Rule 11. The motion must first be served on the opposing party under Rule 5. The opposing party must then be given 21 days to withdraw or correct the challenged pleading. If this 21-day “safe harbor” period passes, and the opposing party fails to withdraw or correct the pleading, the party seeking sanctions may file the motion with the court. Rule 11(c)(2). On its own initiative, the court may order an attorney, a law firm, or a party to show cause why conduct specifically described in the order has not violated Rule 11. Rule 11(c)(3).

rules.rule_statement: After notice and a reasonable opportunity to respond, the court may, in its discretion, impose sanctions on attorneys, law firms, and parties for violations of Rule 11. Absent exceptional circumstances, a law firm must be held jointly responsible for a violation committed by its partner, associate, or employee. Rule 11(c)(1).

rules.rule_statement: Sanctions “must be limited to what suffices to deter repetition of the conduct or comparable conduct by others similarly situated.” Rule 11(c)(4). The court cannot impose a monetary sanction on its own unless it has issued an order to show cause. The order to show cause must be issued before the claims were voluntarily dismissed or settled by or against the party who is, or whose attorneys are, to be sanctioned. Rule 11(c)(5)(B). An order to show cause is a court order that requires a person to appear before the court to argue against the court’s intended action. The court also is not permitted to impose a monetary sanction against a represented party for violating the requirement that the claims, defenses, and other legal contentions of the paper be warranted by existing law or by a nonfrivolous argument for extending, modifying, or reversing existing law or for establishing new law. Rule 11(c)(5)(A).

Limitations

The court cannot impose a monetary sanction on its own unless it has issued an order to show cause. The order to show cause must be issued before the claims were voluntarily dismissed or settled by or against the party who is, or whose attorneys are, to be sanctioned. Rule 11(c)(5)(B). An order to show cause is a court order that requires a person to appear before the court to argue against the court’s intended action. The court also is not permitted to impose a monetary sanction against a represented party for violating the requirement that the claims, defenses, and other legal contentions of the paper be warranted by existing law or by a nonfrivolous argument for extending, modifying, or reversing existing law or for establishing new law. Rule 11(c)(5)(A).

rules.rule_statement: Rule 20 provides the circumstances in which a plaintiff may join other plaintiffs in an action or in which defendants may be joined in the same action.
rules.short_rule_statement: Rule 20 provides the circumstances in which a plaintiff may join other plaintiffs in an action or in which defendants may be joined in the same action.
rules.source_text:
Permissive Joinder
Rule 20 provides the circumstances in which a plaintiff may join other plaintiffs in an action or in which defendants may be joined in the same action.

rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 20(a)(1), persons may join in one action as plaintiffs if: i) They assert any right to relief jointly, severally, or in the alternative regarding or arising out of the same transaction, occurrence, or series of transactions or occurrences; and ii) Any question of law or fact common to all plaintiffs will arise in the action.

Plaintiffs
Under Rule 20(a)(1), persons may join in one action as plaintiffs if: i) They assert any right to relief jointly, severally, or in the alternative regarding or arising out of the same transaction, occurrence, or series of transactions or occurrences; and ii) Any question of law or fact common to all plaintiffs will arise in the action.

rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 20(a)(2), persons may be joined in one action as defendants if: i) Any right to relief is asserted against them jointly, severally, or in the alternative regarding or arising out of the same transaction, occurrence, or series of transactions or occurrences; and ii) Any question of law or fact common to all defendants will arise in the action.

rules.source_text:
Defendants
Under Rule 20(a)(2), persons may be joined in one action as defendants if: i) Any right to relief is asserted against them jointly, severally, or in the alternative regarding or arising out of the same transaction, occurrence, or series of transactions or occurrences; and ii) Any question of law or fact common to all defendants will arise in the action.

rules.rule_statement: The same relief need not be demanded among the joined plaintiffs or against the joined defendants. Rule 20(a)(3).
rules.short_rule_statement: The same relief need not be demanded among the joined plaintiffs or against the joined defendants. Rule 20(a)(3).
rules.source_text:
Extent Of Relief
The same relief need not be demanded among the joined plaintiffs or against the joined defendants. Rule 20(a)(3).

rules.rule_statement: To avoid unfairness or hardship to any party, the court may order separate trials on any claims joined or may make any other order to prevent delay or undue expense to any party. Rule 20(b).
rules.short_rule_statement: To avoid unfairness or hardship to any party, the court may order separate trials on any claims joined or may make any other order to prevent delay or undue expense to any...
rules.source_text:
Protective Measures
To avoid unfairness or hardship to any party, the court may order separate trials on any claims joined or may make any other order to prevent delay or undue expense to any party. Rule 20(b).

rules.rule_statement: A plaintiff or defendant sought to be joined must also meet the requirements of federal subject matter jurisdiction.
rules.short_rule_statement: A plaintiff or defendant sought to be joined must also meet the requirements of federal subject matter jurisdiction.
rules.source_text:
Jurisdiction And Venue
A plaintiff or defendant sought to be joined must also meet the requirements of federal subject matter jurisdiction.

rules.rule_statement: Under § 1367(b), supplemental jurisdiction does not apply to defendants sought to be joined under the permissive joinder rule in a case based exclusively on diversity jurisdiction. Example: While driving his car, Plaintiff, a citizen of Arizona, was hit by a truck operated by Defendant 1, a citizen of New Mexico. Plaintiff was thrown from his car and injured by a car driven by Defendant 2, a citizen of Nevada. Plaintiff brings a negligence suit for $100,000 against Defendant 1 in federal court based on diversity jurisdiction. Plaintiff then joins Defendant 2 under Rule 20, suing for $25,000. Under § 1367(b), there is no supplemental jurisdiction against Defendant 2. Nor is there diversity jurisdiction against Defendant 2 because the statutory jurisdictional amount ($75,000) is not met. ii)      Joinder of plaintiffs If multiple plaintiffs join together under Rule 20, then supplemental jurisdiction can exist for a claim that does not meet the statutory jurisdictional amount, provided the parties still meet the requirement of complete diversity. Example: Plaintiff 1 and Plaintiff 2, who are citizens of California, join together under Rule 20 as plaintiffs against Defendant, a citizen of Oregon, in a diversity suit for negligence. The claims arise out of the same occurrence or transaction. Plaintiff 1 claims $200,000 and Plaintiff 2 claims $25,000. Supplemental jurisdiction exists over Plaintiff 2’s claim even though the claim does not meet the statutory jurisdictional amount. But if Plaintiff 2 was a citizen of Oregon, there would not be complete diversity, and Plaintiff 2 would have to be dropped from the federal case. If a defendant is joined under Rule 20, then the court must have in personam jurisdiction over the defendant for joinder to be proper. Joinder under Rule 20 is also subject to any applicable venue requirements.

rules.rule_statement: Rule 19 specifies the rules for joining additional parties. The requirements of jurisdiction (both subject matter and personal) and venue must still be met for compulsory joinder to occur. Under certain circumstances, if compulsory joinder cannot occur because of jurisdictional or venue issues, Rule 19(b) may require the action to be dismissed from federal court.

rules.source_text:
Compulsory Joinder
Rule 19 specifies the rules for joining additional parties. The requirements of jurisdiction (both subject matter and personal) and venue must still be met for compulsory joinder to occur. Under certain circumstances, if compulsory joinder cannot occur because of jurisdictional or venue issues, Rule 19(b) may require the action to be dismissed from federal court.

rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 19(b), if a necessary party cannot be joined because of jurisdictional or venue concerns, then the court must determine whether, in equity and good conscience, the action should proceed among the existing parties or be dismissed. Factors for the court to consider are: ii) The extent to which any prejudice could be reduced or avoided by using protective provisions in the judgment, shaping the relief, or taking other measures; iii) Whether a judgment rendered in the person’s absence would be adequate; and iv) Whether the plaintiff would have an adequate remedy if the action were dismissed for nonjoinder. When the court dismisses an action because of the inability to join a necessary party, the party is said to be indispensable. California Distinction: Joinder of Plaintiffs In California, plaintiffs may be joined if: series of transactions or occurrences, and a common question of law or fact will arise; or ii) They have a claim adverse to the defendant.

Under Rule 19(b), if a necessary party cannot be joined because of jurisdictional or venue concerns, then the court must determine whether, in equity and good conscience, the action should proceed among the existing parties or be dismissed. Factors for the court to consider are: ii) The extent to which any prejudice could be reduced or avoided by using protective provisions in the judgment, shaping the relief, or taking other measures; iii) Whether a judgment rendered in the person’s absence would be adequate; and iv) Whether the plaintiff would have an adequate remedy if the action were dismissed for nonjoinder. When the court dismisses an action because of the inability to join a necessary party, the party is said to be indispensable. rules.rule_statement: Rule Rule 24 governs the circumstances under which a nonparty may join in a lawsuit. In some circumstances, the nonparty may intervene as of right. In other circumstances, the nonparty must have the permission of the court. In either case, a motion to intervene must be timely.

Intervention
Rule Rule 24 governs the circumstances under which a nonparty may join in a lawsuit. In some circumstances, the nonparty may intervene as of right. In other circumstances, the nonparty must have the permission of the court. In either case, a motion to intervene must be timely.

rules.rule_statement: ii) The nonparty timely moves to intervene. Upon a timely motion, a nonparty also has the right to intervene when: ii) The disposition of the action may, as a practical matter, impair the nonparty’s interest; and iii) The nonparty’s interest is not adequately represented by existing parties. Rule 24(a)(2). The criteria for intervention as of right under Rule 24(a)(2) are similar to the criteria for compulsory joinder under Rule 19(a). The burden of proof is on the person seeking to intervene.

In exercising its discretion, the court must consider whether the intervention will unduly delay or prejudice the adjudication of the rights of the original parties. California Distinction: Permissive Intervention In California, a court must find that the potential intervenor’s interest is “direct and immediate” to allow permissive intervention. If the interest is indirect and consequential, intervention will be denied. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 387.

rules.rule_statement: The decision of whether the nonparty timely moved to intervene is in the discretion of the trial court, considering factors such as: i) The length of time the movant knew or reasonably should have known that its interest was threatened before moving to intervene; ii) The prejudice to existing parties if intervention is permitted; and iii) The prejudice to the movant if intervention is denied.

rules.source_text:
Timeliness
The decision of whether the nonparty timely moved to intervene is in the discretion of the trial court, considering factors such as: i) The length of time the movant knew or reasonably should have known that its interest was threatened before moving to intervene; ii) The prejudice to existing parties if intervention is permitted; and iii) The prejudice to the movant if intervention is denied.

rules.rule_statement: A claim of an intervenor must be supported by its own jurisdictional basis. Under § 1367(b), supplemental jurisdiction does not apply to the claims of a person seeking to intervene as a plaintiff under Rule 24 (either as of right or permissively) in a case based exclusively on diversity jurisdiction if the exercise of jurisdiction would be inconsistent with the requirements of diversity jurisdiction. Example: Party X, a citizen of Ohio, sues Party Y, a citizen of Michigan, in federal court based on diversity jurisdiction. Party Z, a citizen of Michigan, seeks to intervene as a plaintiff (as of right or permissively) under Rule 24. Party Z cannot intervene because Z would destroy diversity jurisdiction in the action. If Z were a citizen of any state other than Michigan, then Z could intervene, provided the requirements for intervention under Rule 24 were met.
