rules.source_text:
Subject Matter Jurisdiction
A claim of an intervenor must be supported by its own jurisdictional basis. Under § 1367(b), supplemental jurisdiction does not apply to the claims of a person seeking to intervene as a plaintiff under Rule 24 (either as of right or permissively) in a case based exclusively on diversity jurisdiction if the exercise of jurisdiction would be inconsistent with the requirements of diversity jurisdiction. Example: Party X, a citizen of Ohio, sues Party Y, a citizen of Michigan, in federal court based on diversity jurisdiction. Party Z, a citizen of Michigan, seeks to intervene as a plaintiff (as of right or permissively) under Rule 24. Party Z cannot intervene because Z would destroy diversity jurisdiction in the action. If Z were a citizen of any state other than Michigan, then Z could intervene, provided the requirements for intervention under Rule 24 were met.

rules.rule_statement: Rule Interpleader allows a person holding property (traditionally known as the “stakeholder”) who legitimately fears multiple claims against the property to force all potential claimants to the property into a single lawsuit to determine who has a right to the property. There are two forms of interpleader, each with its own requirements: interpleader under Rule 22 and federal statutory interpleader under § 1335.
rules.short_rule_statement: Rule Interpleader allows a person holding property (traditionally known as the “stakeholder”) who legitimately fears multiple claims against the property to force all potential...
rules.source_text:
Interpleader
Rule Interpleader allows a person holding property (traditionally known as the “stakeholder”) who legitimately fears multiple claims against the property to force all potential claimants to the property into a single lawsuit to determine who has a right to the property. There are two forms of interpleader, each with its own requirements: interpleader under Rule 22 and federal statutory interpleader under § 1335.

rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 22, persons with claims that may expose a plaintiff to double or multiple liability may be joined as defendants and required to interplead. Such joinder is proper even though the claims of the claimants, or the titles on which their claims depend, lack a common origin or are adverse and independent rather than identical. Also, joinder is proper even though the plaintiff denies liability in whole or in part to any or all of the claimants. A defendant who is exposed to similar liability may seek interpleader through a crossclaim or counterclaim. Rule 22(a).
rules.short_rule_statement: Under Rule 22, persons with claims that may expose a plaintiff to double or multiple liability may be joined as defendants and required to interplead. Such joinder is proper...
rules.source_text:
Basis
Under Rule 22, persons with claims that may expose a plaintiff to double or multiple liability may be joined as defendants and required to interplead. Such joinder is proper even though the claims of the claimants, or the titles on which their claims depend, lack a common origin or are adverse and independent rather than identical. Also, joinder is proper even though the plaintiff denies liability in whole or in part to any or all of the claimants. A defendant who is exposed to similar liability may seek interpleader through a crossclaim or counterclaim. Rule 22(a).

rules.rule_statement: Rule 22 does not create subject matter jurisdiction in interpleader actions. Rather, the court must already have jurisdiction over all parties. Therefore, a plaintiff will need federal question jurisdiction or, for diversity jurisdiction, the citizenship of the party bringing the action must be completely diverse from that of the claimants, and the statutory amount in controversy must be met. While the stakeholder needs to be diverse from the claimants, the claimants need not be diverse among themselves.
rules.short_rule_statement: Rule 22 does not create subject matter jurisdiction in interpleader actions. Rather, the court must already have jurisdiction over all parties. Therefore, a plaintiff will need...
rules.source_text:
Subject Matter Jurisdiction
Rule 22 does not create subject matter jurisdiction in interpleader actions. Rather, the court must already have jurisdiction over all parties. Therefore, a plaintiff will need federal question jurisdiction or, for diversity jurisdiction, the citizenship of the party bringing the action must be completely diverse from that of the claimants, and the statutory amount in controversy must be met. While the stakeholder needs to be diverse from the claimants, the claimants need not be diverse among themselves.

rules.rule_statement: An interpleader action is an action against the claimants. Therefore, the general requirements of in personam jurisdiction in federal court must be met.
rules.short_rule_statement: An interpleader action is an action against the claimants. Therefore, the general requirements of in personam jurisdiction in federal court must be met.
rules.source_text:
In Personam Jurisdiction
An interpleader action is an action against the claimants. Therefore, the general requirements of in personam jurisdiction in federal court must be met.

rules.rule_statement: Federal venue requirements must also be satisfied in interpleader actions under Rule 22.
rules.short_rule_statement: Federal venue requirements must also be satisfied in interpleader actions under Rule 22.
rules.source_text:
Venue
Federal venue requirements must also be satisfied in interpleader actions under Rule 22.

rules.rule_statement: There are some key differences in the requirements for federal statutory interpleader under § 1335.
rules.short_rule_statement: There are some key differences in the requirements for federal statutory interpleader under § 1335.
rules.source_text:
Federal Statutory Interpleader
There are some key differences in the requirements for federal statutory interpleader under § 1335.

rules.rule_statement: For statutory interpleader, diversity jurisdiction is met if any two adverse claimants are citizens of different states. However, the amount in controversy does not need to meet the $75,000 threshold required for regular diversity matters. The property at issue must merely be $500 or more in value.
rules.short_rule_statement: For statutory interpleader, diversity jurisdiction is met if any two adverse claimants are citizens of different states. However, the amount in controversy does not need to...
rules.source_text:
Subject Matter Jurisdiction
For statutory interpleader, diversity jurisdiction is met if any two adverse claimants are citizens of different states. However, the amount in controversy does not need to meet the $75,000 threshold required for regular diversity matters. The property at issue must merely be $500 or more in value.

rules.rule_statement: Statutory interpleader provides for nationwide personal jurisdiction and service of process and permits the federal court to enjoin other federal and state proceedings that may affect the property that is subject to dispute. § 2361.
rules.short_rule_statement: Statutory interpleader provides for nationwide personal jurisdiction and service of process and permits the federal court to enjoin other federal and state proceedings that may...
rules.source_text:
In Personam Jurisdiction
Statutory interpleader provides for nationwide personal jurisdiction and service of process and permits the federal court to enjoin other federal and state proceedings that may affect the property that is subject to dispute. § 2361.

rules.rule_statement: Venue is proper in any federal judicial district where one of the claimants resides. § 1397.
rules.short_rule_statement: Venue is proper in any federal judicial district where one of the claimants resides. § 1397.
rules.source_text:
Venue
Venue is proper in any federal judicial district where one of the claimants resides. § 1397.

rules.rule_statement: For statutory interpleader, unlike for interpleader under the Federal Rules, the stakeholder is required to either deposit with the court the property at issue or post a bond in an appropriate amount.
rules.short_rule_statement: For statutory interpleader, unlike for interpleader under the Federal Rules, the stakeholder is required to either deposit with the court the property at issue or post a bond...
rules.source_text:
Deposit
For statutory interpleader, unlike for interpleader under the Federal Rules, the stakeholder is required to either deposit with the court the property at issue or post a bond in an appropriate amount.

rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 18(a), a party who can assert a claim, counterclaim, crossclaim, or third-party claim (i.e., a qualifying claim that clearly can be brought under the Federal Rules) may join with it as many independent or alternative claims the party may have against an opposing party. Rule 18 permits joinder of claims, but it does not compel it. However, res judicata concerns will often require joinder. See § IX.D. Claim Preclusion (Res Judicata), infra.
rules.short_rule_statement: Under Rule 18(a), a party who can assert a claim, counterclaim, crossclaim, or third-party claim (i.e., a qualifying claim that clearly can be brought under the Federal Rules)...
rules.source_text:
In General
Under Rule 18(a), a party who can assert a claim, counterclaim, crossclaim, or third-party claim (i.e., a qualifying claim that clearly can be brought under the Federal Rules) may join with it as many independent or alternative claims the party may have against an opposing party. Rule 18 permits joinder of claims, but it does not compel it. However, res judicata concerns will often require joinder. See § IX.D. Claim Preclusion (Res Judicata), infra.

rules.rule_statement: A party may join two claims even though one of them is contingent on the disposition of the other. Rule 18(b). Example: A plaintiff may assert both a claim for monetary damages and a claim to set aside a conveyance that was fraudulent as a result of the defendant’s transfer of assets to frustrate enforcement of the claim for monetary damages.
rules.short_rule_statement: A party may join two claims even though one of them is contingent on the disposition of the other. Rule 18(b). Example: A plaintiff may assert both a claim for monetary damages...
rules.source_text:
Joinder Of Contingent Claims
A party may join two claims even though one of them is contingent on the disposition of the other. Rule 18(b). Example: A plaintiff may assert both a claim for monetary damages and a claim to set aside a conveyance that was fraudulent as a result of the defendant’s transfer of assets to frustrate enforcement of the claim for monetary damages.

rules.rule_statement: To join a claim under Rule 18, the court must have subject matter jurisdiction over the claim. EXAM NOTE: Remember that if subject matter jurisdiction is based on diversity jurisdiction, then a party may aggregate all claims joined against an opposing party to satisfy the statutory jurisdictional amount-in-controversy requirement.
rules.short_rule_statement: To join a claim under Rule 18, the court must have subject matter jurisdiction over the claim. EXAM NOTE: Remember that if subject matter jurisdiction is based on diversity...
rules.source_text:
Subject Matter Jurisdiction
To join a claim under Rule 18, the court must have subject matter jurisdiction over the claim. EXAM NOTE: Remember that if subject matter jurisdiction is based on diversity jurisdiction, then a party may aggregate all claims joined against an opposing party to satisfy the statutory jurisdictional amount-in-controversy requirement.

rules.rule_statement: If the original claim is based on federal question jurisdiction, then a nonfederal claim may be joined only if diversity jurisdiction exists or if the two claims are part of the same case or controversy as the federal claim such that supplemental jurisdiction applies.
rules.short_rule_statement: If the original claim is based on federal question jurisdiction, then a nonfederal claim may be joined only if diversity jurisdiction exists or if the two claims are part of...
rules.source_text:
Federal Question Jurisdiction
If the original claim is based on federal question jurisdiction, then a nonfederal claim may be joined only if diversity jurisdiction exists or if the two claims are part of the same case or controversy as the federal claim such that supplemental jurisdiction applies.

rules.rule_statement: The venue requirements for federal court must be satisfied to join a claim under Rule 18. California Distinction: Joinder of Claims California’s rule is more limiting than its federal counterpart (Rule 18) in cases in which several defendants are joined—at least one question of law or fact must be common to all defendants. The court may discretionarily sever claims if undue delay or prejudice would otherwise result. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 1048(b).
rules.short_rule_statement: The venue requirements for federal court must be satisfied to join a claim under Rule 18. California Distinction: Joinder of Claims California’s rule is more limiting than its...
rules.source_text:
Venue
The venue requirements for federal court must be satisfied to join a claim under Rule 18. California Distinction: Joinder of Claims California’s rule is more limiting than its federal counterpart (Rule 18) in cases in which several defendants are joined—at least one question of law or fact must be common to all defendants. The court may discretionarily sever claims if undue delay or prejudice would otherwise result. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 1048(b).

rules.rule_statement: A counterclaim is a claim for relief made against an opposing party after an original claim has been made. A counterclaim may be asserted in the answer to the complaint and the reply to a counterclaim. Rule 13 governs the requirements for bringing a counterclaim. A party must serve an answer to a counterclaim (or crossclaim) within 21 days of service. Rule 12(a)(1)(B). EXAM NOTE: A federal court must have subject matter jurisdiction over the counterclaim.  Because a compulsory counterclaim arises out of the same transaction or occurrence as does the original claim before the court, compulsory counterclaims generally fall under the supplemental jurisdiction of the federal court.  However, a permissive counterclaim must meet the requirements for diversity or federal question jurisdiction.
rules.short_rule_statement: A counterclaim is a claim for relief made against an opposing party after an original claim has been made. A counterclaim may be asserted in the answer to the complaint and the...
rules.source_text:
Counterclaims
A counterclaim is a claim for relief made against an opposing party after an original claim has been made. A counterclaim may be asserted in the answer to the complaint and the reply to a counterclaim. Rule 13 governs the requirements for bringing a counterclaim. A party must serve an answer to a counterclaim (or crossclaim) within 21 days of service. Rule 12(a)(1)(B). EXAM NOTE: A federal court must have subject matter jurisdiction over the counterclaim.  Because a compulsory counterclaim arises out of the same transaction or occurrence as does the original claim before the court, compulsory counterclaims generally fall under the supplemental jurisdiction of the federal court.  However, a permissive counterclaim must meet the requirements for diversity or federal question jurisdiction.

Rule 13(a)(1). However, the pleader is not required to make the claim if:

rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 13(b), a pleading may state as a counterclaim against an opposing party any claim that is not compulsory. Therefore, a party has discretion as to whether to raise the counterclaim in the action before the court or in a separate action.

Permissive
Under Rule 13(b), a pleading may state as a counterclaim against an opposing party any claim that is not compulsory. Therefore, a party has discretion as to whether to raise the counterclaim in the action before the court or in a separate action.

rules.rule_statement: A third-party defendant may file a counterclaim against either the original defendant or the original plaintiff. The requirements of Rule 13(a)–(b) govern whether a third-party defendant’s counterclaim is classified as compulsory or permissive. A third-party defendant is a party brought into a lawsuit by an original defendant based on a theory that the party may be liable for damages that the original defendant may owe to the original plaintiff. Under Rule 13(h), new parties to a counterclaim may be joined as long as they meet the requirements for joinder under Rule 20. New parties must be joined if they are indispensable parties under Rule 19.
rules.short_rule_statement: A third-party defendant may file a counterclaim against either the original defendant or the original plaintiff. The requirements of Rule 13(a)–(b) govern whether a third-party...
rules.source_text:
By Third Parties
A third-party defendant may file a counterclaim against either the original defendant or the original plaintiff. The requirements of Rule 13(a)–(b) govern whether a third-party defendant’s counterclaim is classified as compulsory or permissive. A third-party defendant is a party brought into a lawsuit by an original defendant based on a theory that the party may be liable for damages that the original defendant may owe to the original plaintiff. Under Rule 13(h), new parties to a counterclaim may be joined as long as they meet the requirements for joinder under Rule 20. New parties must be joined if they are indispensable parties under Rule 19.

rules.rule_statement: A crossclaim is a claim made against a co-party, such as a claim by a defendant against another defendant. The statement of a defense in a pleading that is labeled a crossclaim (e.g., an allegation that a codefendant is solely liable to the plaintiff) does not constitute a claim against a coparty. Under Rule 13(g), a pleading may state as a crossclaim any claim by one party against a coparty if: i) The claim arises out of the same transaction or occurrence that is the subject matter of the original action or of a counterclaim; or ii) The claim relates to any property that is the subject matter of the original action. The crossclaim may include a claim that the coparty is liable to the crossclaimant for all or part of a claim asserted in the action against the crossclaimant.
rules.short_rule_statement: A crossclaim is a claim made against a co-party, such as a claim by a defendant against another defendant. The statement of a defense in a pleading that is labeled a crossclaim...
rules.source_text:
In General
A crossclaim is a claim made against a co-party, such as a claim by a defendant against another defendant. The statement of a defense in a pleading that is labeled a crossclaim (e.g., an allegation that a codefendant is solely liable to the plaintiff) does not constitute a claim against a coparty. Under Rule 13(g), a pleading may state as a crossclaim any claim by one party against a coparty if: i) The claim arises out of the same transaction or occurrence that is the subject matter of the original action or of a counterclaim; or ii) The claim relates to any property that is the subject matter of the original action. The crossclaim may include a claim that the coparty is liable to the crossclaimant for all or part of a claim asserted in the action against the crossclaimant.

rules.rule_statement: A party is never required to assert a crossclaim against a coparty. Under Rule 13(h), new parties to a crossclaim may be joined if they meet the requirements for joinder under Rule 20. New parties must be joined if they meet the requirements for joinder under Rule 19.

Crossclaim Not Mandatory
A party is never required to assert a crossclaim against a coparty. Under Rule 13(h), new parties to a crossclaim may be joined if they meet the requirements for joinder under Rule 20. New parties must be joined if they meet the requirements for joinder under Rule 19.

rules.rule_statement: A crossclaim must fall within the subject matter jurisdiction of the federal court. This requirement is generally not a problem because, by definition, a crossclaim must arise out of the same transaction or occurrence as the subject matter of the original action or of a counterclaim or it must relate to the property at issue. Therefore, crossclaims tend to fall under the court’s supplemental jurisdiction.

This requirement is generally not a problem because, by definition, a crossclaim must arise out of the same transaction or occurrence as the subject matter of the original action or of a counterclaim or it must relate to the property at issue. Therefore, crossclaims tend to fall under the court’s supplemental jurisdiction.

In Personam Jurisdiction And Venue
Because the parties are already before the court, personal jurisdiction is satisfied. Additionally, if venue was proper over the original claim, then a party cannot object to venue with regarding the crossclaim.

rules.rule_statement: Rule 14 provides the rules governing third-party claims (impleader). The court may sever any third-party claim if justice demands it. Impleaded claims are made by a defending party against a nonparty for all or part of the defending party’s liability on an original claim. The plaintiff may also assert related claims against the impleaded party. In both cases, the impleaded claim must relate to the original claim against the defending party.

rules.source_text:
In General
Rule 14 provides the rules governing third-party claims (impleader). The court may sever any third-party claim if justice demands it. Impleaded claims are made by a defending party against a nonparty for all or part of the defending party’s liability on an original claim. The plaintiff may also assert related claims against the impleaded party. In both cases, the impleaded claim must relate to the original claim against the defending party.

rules.rule_statement: A defending party—including a plaintiff against whom a counterclaim has been asserted—may assert a third-party claim at any time after the complaint is filed. The defending party, referred to for impleader purposes as the “third-party plaintiff,” must serve a summons and third-party complaint on the nonparty (third-party defendant). The third-party plaintiff must obtain the court’s permission if the filing was more than 14 days after service of the original answer. Rule 14(a)(1). The third-party defendant may (and in some cases must) assert defenses, counterclaims, and crossclaims against any of the parties as appropriate under the Rules and may also implead another nonparty who is or may be liable to the third-party defendant for the claim on which the defendant was impleaded. Rule 14(a)(2). The third-party plaintiff must obtain the court’s permission if the filing was more than 14 days after service of the original answer. Rule 14(a)(1). The third-party defendant may (and in some cases must) assert defenses, counterclaims, and crossclaims against any of the parties as appropriate under the Rules and may also implead another nonparty who is or may be liable to the third-party defendant for the claim on which the defendant was impleaded. rules.rule_statement: A third-party claim must fall within the federal court’s subject matter jurisdiction. Because, by definition, a third-party claim will be closely related to the original claim, the court generally will have supplemental jurisdiction over the matter. However, if the original claims are based exclusively on diversity jurisdiction under § 1367(b), supplemental jurisdiction will not apply to claims by the plaintiff against a third-party defendant brought in under Rule 14. These claims need to meet diversity or federal question jurisdiction requirements on their own.

Subject Matter Jurisdiction
A third-party claim must fall within the federal court’s subject matter jurisdiction. Because, by definition, a third-party claim will be closely related to the original claim, the court generally will have supplemental jurisdiction over the matter. However, if the original claims are based exclusively on diversity jurisdiction under § 1367(b), supplemental jurisdiction will not apply to claims by the plaintiff against a third-party defendant brought in under Rule 14. These claims need to meet diversity or federal question jurisdiction requirements on their own.

Rule 4(k)(1)(B).

rules.rule_statement: In a class action, the court authorizes a single person or a small group of people to represent the interests of a larger group. Rule 23 and the Class Action Fairness Act of 2005 govern class actions.

Class Actions
In a class action, the court authorizes a single person or a small group of people to represent the interests of a larger group. Rule 23 and the Class Action Fairness Act of 2005 govern class actions.

rules.rule_statement: Rule 23(a) establishes four requirements for representative members of a class to sue or be sued on behalf of all members of the class:

rules.source_text:
Basic Requirements
Rule 23(a) establishes four requirements for representative members of a class to sue or be sued on behalf of all members of the class:

rules.canonical_name: Numerosity – The Class Must Be So Numerous That Joinder Of All Members Is...
rules.rule_statement: ii) Commonality – There must be questions of law or fact that are common to the class; iii) Typicality – The claims or defenses of the representatives must be typical of the class; and iv) Adequacy – The representatives must fairly and adequately protect the interests of the class. No strict threshold exists for determining whether the requirement of “numerosity” has been met. However, a class of 40 or more members is generally presumed to be so numerous that joinder of all members is impracticable, while a class of fewer than 20 members is unlikely to be certified absent other indications of impracticability of joinder.

rules.rule_statement: In addition to the requirements of Rule 23(a), before a class action can be certified, it must fit within one of the three situations specified in Rule 23(b). Under Rule 23(b)(1), the class is maintainable if the prosecution of separate actions: i) Would create the risk that the class opponent would become subject to incompatible standards of conduct resulting from inconsistent adjudications; or ii) Would impair the interests of the class members as a practical matter. Under Rule 23(b)(2), a class seeking final injunctive or declaratory relief may be certified if the class shares a general claim against the opposing party. An additional claim for monetary damages may not be sought when the monetary relief is not incidental to the injunctive or declaratory relief. Moreover, a claim for individualized monetary relief is not available because a claim under Rule 23(b)(2) is available only when a single, indivisible remedy would provide relief to each class member. Under Rule 23(b)(3), a class can be certified if questions of law or fact that are common to the class members predominate over any questions affecting only individual members. In these circumstances, the class action must be the superior method for bringing about a fair and efficient adjudication of the controversy. In making this determination, the court must consider, among other things: ii) The extent and nature of any litigation concerning the controversy already begun by or against class members; iii) The desirability or undesirability of concentrating the litigation of the claims in the particular forum; and iv) The likely difficulties in managing a class action. The class representatives need not establish that success on a common question of law is likely for the class to be certified.

Three Types

An additional claim for monetary damages may not be sought when the monetary relief is not incidental to the injunctive or declaratory relief. Moreover, a claim for individualized monetary relief is not available because a claim under Rule 23(b)(2) is available only when a single, indivisible remedy would provide relief to each class member. rules.rule_statement: A class action must also satisfy federal subject matter jurisdiction requirements. A class action can invoke federal question jurisdiction or diversity jurisdiction. If diversity jurisdiction is invoked in a class action, then diversity of citizenship will be satisfied if the class representatives are diverse from the party or parties opposing the claim. When at least one representative plaintiff of a putative class action has a claim that meets the statutory jurisdictional amount (generally it must exceed $75,000), other persons with claims that do not meet the jurisdictional amount can be made part of the class under the doctrine of supplemental jurisdiction.
rules.short_rule_statement: A class action must also satisfy federal subject matter jurisdiction requirements. A class action can invoke federal question jurisdiction or diversity jurisdiction. If...
rules.source_text:
Subject Matter Jurisdiction
A class action must also satisfy federal subject matter jurisdiction requirements. A class action can invoke federal question jurisdiction or diversity jurisdiction. If diversity jurisdiction is invoked in a class action, then diversity of citizenship will be satisfied if the class representatives are diverse from the party or parties opposing the claim. When at least one representative plaintiff of a putative class action has a claim that meets the statutory jurisdictional amount (generally it must exceed $75,000), other persons with claims that do not meet the jurisdictional amount can be made part of the class under the doctrine of supplemental jurisdiction.

rules.rule_statement: When the class action involves a defendant class, venue is determined based on the residences of the representative parties, not the residences of all the class members.

Venue
When the class action involves a defendant class, venue is determined based on the residences of the representative parties, not the residences of all the class members.

rules.canonical_name: The Class Action Involves At Least 100 Members
rules.rule_statement: ii) The primary defendants are not states, state officials, or other government entities against whom the district court may be foreclosed from ordering relief; iii) The action does not involve certain securities-related cases or litigation concerning the internal affairs or governance of a corporation; iv) The aggregate amount in controversy exceeds the sum or value of $5,000,000, exclusive of interest and costs; and v) Minimum diversity exists. Minimum diversity is satisfied when any member of a class of plaintiffs is a citizen of a state different from any defendant. Under CAFA, any defendant can remove the case to federal court. If the class action claim is based only on federal securities law or corporate governance, then there is no federal jurisdiction under CAFA. This exclusion also applies to primary defendants who are government entities. The benefit to filing under CAFA is the various protections afforded to the class members. The court will generally protect members against loss in consumer class actions, oversee that the settlement is fair to the members, and ensure that the settlement is equally distributed. The court will also notify federal and state officials of the proposed settlement. If notice is not given, then a class member may choose not to be bound by the agreement. The court is required to repudiate jurisdiction when:

rules.source_text:
Standard For Approval
If the proposal would bind class members, then the court may approve it only after a hearing and on finding that the proposal is fair, reasonable, and adequate. The parties seeking approval must file a statement identifying any collateral agreements made related to the proposal.

rules.rule_statement: Any class member may object to the proposal if it requires court approval. Rule 23(e)(5). Once the named representative class members reach a settlement that is approved over the objections of a class member who was not a named representative, the objecting class member’s interests diverge from those of the class representatives, and the class member who objects may appeal the court’s approval of a settlement.
rules.short_rule_statement: Any class member may object to the proposal if it requires court approval. Rule 23(e)(5). Once the named representative class members reach a settlement that is approved over...
rules.source_text:
Objection By Class Members
Any class member may object to the proposal if it requires court approval. Rule 23(e)(5). Once the named representative class members reach a settlement that is approved over the objections of a class member who was not a named representative, the objecting class member’s interests diverge from those of the class representatives, and the class member who objects may appeal the court’s approval of a settlement.

rules.rule_statement: If a class action was certified under Rule 23(b)(3), the court may refuse to approve of the settlement unless class members are given a second opportunity to opt out of the litigation and proceed on their own. Rule 23(e)(4). A class member who fails to opt out is bound by the settlement.
rules.short_rule_statement: If a class action was certified under Rule 23(b)(3), the court may refuse to approve of the settlement unless class members are given a second opportunity to opt out of the...
rules.source_text:
Second Opt-Out Opportunity
If a class action was certified under Rule 23(b)(3), the court may refuse to approve of the settlement unless class members are given a second opportunity to opt out of the litigation and proceed on their own. Rule 23(e)(4). A class member who fails to opt out is bound by the settlement.

rules.rule_statement: In general, a valid judgment binds all members of the class. In a class action certified under Rule 23(b)(3), a valid judgment does not bind those class members who opted out of the lawsuit. However, a class member who does not opt out is bound by the judgment even when that class member did not have minimum contacts with the forum state. California Distinction: Class Actions A class action may be brought in California when “the question is one of a common or general interest, of many persons, or when the parties are numerous, and it is impracticable to bring them all before the court.” Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 382. Claims of individual class members are aggregated to satisfy the amount in controversy requirement for California subject matter jurisdiction. The requirements for class action certification are:

rules.rule_statement: A shareholder derivative action is an action brought by a shareholder on behalf of a corporation to enforce a right of the corporation that the corporation has failed to enforce. While not generally subject to the Rules for a class action, the shareholder must represent the shareholders of the corporation fairly and adequately. The shareholder is bound to plead with particularity that a demand was made on the directors, and, if state law requires, also made a demand on the shareholders. If a demand was not made, the shareholder must state why. The shareholder must also assert status as a shareholder at the time the transaction occurred or that this status was subsequently gained by operation of law (e.g., by inheritance). Finally, the shareholder must allege that the action is not a collusive effort to impart jurisdiction on the court. Rule 23.1.
rules.short_rule_statement: A shareholder derivative action is an action brought by a shareholder on behalf of a corporation to enforce a right of the corporation that the corporation has failed to...
rules.source_text:
Shareholder Derivative Suits
A shareholder derivative action is an action brought by a shareholder on behalf of a corporation to enforce a right of the corporation that the corporation has failed to enforce. While not generally subject to the Rules for a class action, the shareholder must represent the shareholders of the corporation fairly and adequately. The shareholder is bound to plead with particularity that a demand was made on the directors, and, if state law requires, also made a demand on the shareholders. If a demand was not made, the shareholder must state why. The shareholder must also assert status as a shareholder at the time the transaction occurred or that this status was subsequently gained by operation of law (e.g., by inheritance). Finally, the shareholder must allege that the action is not a collusive effort to impart jurisdiction on the court. Rule 23.1.

rules.rule_statement: Rule Rule 26(a) requires the parties to make certain disclosures: Initial disclosures Disclosures of expert testimony 90 days before trial Pretrial disclosures 30 days before trial The three sets of disclosures are mandatory and must be made even if an opposing party does not ask for such information. Unless otherwise ordered by the court, the disclosures must be in writing, signed, and served.
rules.short_rule_statement: Rule Rule 26(a) requires the parties to make certain disclosures: Initial disclosures Disclosures of expert testimony 90 days before trial Pretrial disclosures 30 days before...
rules.source_text:
Mandatory Disclosures
Rule Rule 26(a) requires the parties to make certain disclosures: Initial disclosures Disclosures of expert testimony 90 days before trial Pretrial disclosures 30 days before trial The three sets of disclosures are mandatory and must be made even if an opposing party does not ask for such information. Unless otherwise ordered by the court, the disclosures must be in writing, signed, and served.

rules.rule_statement: California Distinction: Automatic Disclosures Although there are no required automatic disclosures in California, the parties may agree to provide an automatic initial disclosure. Upon an order of the court following stipulation by all of the parties to the action, a party must provide to the other parties an initial disclosure within 60 days of the court order and without awaiting a discovery request. The initial disclosure must include: likely to have discoverable information that the disclosing party may use to support its claims or defenses, and the subjects of that information, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; ii) A copy or a description by category and location of all documents, electronically stored information, and tangible things that the disclosing party has in its possession, custody, or control and may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; iii) Any agreement under which an insurance company may be wholly or partially liable to satisfy a judgment entered in the action or to indemnify or reimburse for payments made to satisfy the judgment; and iv) The material provisions (e.g., the identities of the parties and the nature and limits of the coverage) of any agreement under which a person may be partially or wholly liable to satisfy a judgment entered in the action or to indemnify or reimburse for payments made to satisfy the judgment.

rules.rule_statement: Unless otherwise agreed by stipulation or ordered by the court, each party must provide to the other parties: i) The name and, if known, address and telephone number of each individual likely to have discoverable information, along with the subjects of that information that the disclosing party may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; ii) A copy, or a description by category and location, of all documents, electronically stored information, and tangible things that the disclosing party has in its possession, custody, or control and may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; iii) A computation of each category of damages claimed by the disclosing party, who must also make available for inspection and copying the documents or other evidentiary material on which each computation is based, including materials bearing on the nature and extent of injury, unless privileged or protected from disclosure; and iv) For inspection and copying, any insurance agreement under which an insurance business may be liable to satisfy all or part of a possible judgment in the action or to indemnify or reimburse for payments made to satisfy the judgment. Rule 26(a)(1).

rules.rule_statement: A party must make these disclosures about experts at the times and in the sequence that the court orders. Absent a stipulation or court order, the disclosures must be made at least 90 days before the date set for trial or for the case to be ready for trial. If the evidence is intended solely to contradict or rebut the opposing party’s expert evidence on the same subject matter, then disclosure must be made within 30 days after the other party’s disclosure. Rule 26(a)(2)(D).

rules.source_text:
Time To Disclose Expert Testimony
A party must make these disclosures about experts at the times and in the sequence that the court orders. Absent a stipulation or court order, the disclosures must be made at least 90 days before the date set for trial or for the case to be ready for trial. If the evidence is intended solely to contradict or rebut the opposing party’s expert evidence on the same subject matter, then disclosure must be made within 30 days after the other party’s disclosure. Rule 26(a)(2)(D).

rules.rule_statement: The parties must supplement these disclosures when required under Rule 26(e). (See § VII.B.8. Supplementation, infra.) For an expert whose report must be disclosed, the party’s duty to supplement extends both to information included in the report and to information given during the expert’s deposition. Any additions or changes to this information must be disclosed by the time that the party’s pretrial disclosures under Rule 26(a)(3) are due.

Supplementing The Disclosure
The parties must supplement these disclosures when required under Rule 26(e). (See § VII.B.8. Supplementation, infra.) For an expert whose report must be disclosed, the party’s duty to supplement extends both to information included in the report and to information given during the expert’s deposition. Any additions or changes to this information must be disclosed by the time that the party’s pretrial disclosures under Rule 26(a)(3) are due.

rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 26(a)(3)(A), in addition to the initial and expert disclosures, the parties must make certain disclosures regarding evidence that they may present at trial other than for impeachment purposes. They must file with the court: i) The name and, if not previously provided, the address and telephone number of each witness, separately identifying those the party expects to present and those it may call if the need arises; ii) The designation of those witnesses whose testimony the party expects to present by deposition and, if not taken stenographically, a transcript of the pertinent parts of the deposition; and iii) An identification of each document or other exhibit, including summaries of other evidence, separately identifying those items the party expects to offer and those it may offer if the need arises.

In General
Under Rule 26(a)(3)(A), in addition to the initial and expert disclosures, the parties must make certain disclosures regarding evidence that they may present at trial other than for impeachment purposes. They must file with the court: i) The name and, if not previously provided, the address and telephone number of each witness, separately identifying those the party expects to present and those it may call if the need arises; ii) The designation of those witnesses whose testimony the party expects to present by deposition and, if not taken stenographically, a transcript of the pertinent parts of the deposition; and iii) An identification of each document or other exhibit, including summaries of other evidence, separately identifying those items the party expects to offer and those it may offer if the need arises.

Objections
Within 14 days after the disclosures are made, unless the court sets a different time, a party may serve and promptly file objections to the use of the depositions at trial and to the admissibility of disclosed documents and exhibits. If an objection is not made at this point, then it will be waived unless:

rules.rule_statement: Rule Rule 26 provides rules establishing the scope and limitations of discovery. In addition to obtaining information under the mandatory disclosure rules, parties may use a variety of discovery methods provided in Rules 27–36. When litigation is reasonably anticipated, potential litigants in possession of potentially relevant evidence have a duty to preserve such evidence. To trigger a duty to preserve evidence, the reasonable anticipated future litigation must be probable—more than a mere possibility. Once a duty to preserve evidence is triggered, the party in possession of the evidence must take reasonable measures to preserve it.
rules.short_rule_statement: Rule Rule 26 provides rules establishing the scope and limitations of discovery. In addition to obtaining information under the mandatory disclosure rules, parties may use a...
rules.source_text:
Discovery Scope And Limits
Rule Rule 26 provides rules establishing the scope and limitations of discovery. In addition to obtaining information under the mandatory disclosure rules, parties may use a variety of discovery methods provided in Rules 27–36. When litigation is reasonably anticipated, potential litigants in possession of potentially relevant evidence have a duty to preserve such evidence. To trigger a duty to preserve evidence, the reasonable anticipated future litigation must be probable—more than a mere possibility. Once a duty to preserve evidence is triggered, the party in possession of the evidence must take reasonable measures to preserve it.

rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 26(b)(1), discovery is generally permitted regarding any nonprivileged matter relevant to any party’s claim or defense in the action and proportional to the needs of the case. Courts consider the following factors in allowing discovery to take place:

In General
Under Rule 26(b)(1), discovery is generally permitted regarding any nonprivileged matter relevant to any party’s claim or defense in the action and proportional to the needs of the case. Courts consider the following factors in allowing discovery to take place:

California Point of Law: Electronically Stored Information When any method of discovery allows the production, inspection, copying, testing, or sampling of documents or tangible things, that method also permits the same for electronically stored information. All procedures available to compel, prevent, or limit the production, inspection, copying, testing, or sampling of documents or tangible things are available to compel, prevent, or limit the same for electronically stored information. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code §§ 2019.040; 2031.010.

In California, all material “relevant to the subject matter in the pending litigation” must be disclosed. Cal. or defense” need be disclosed.

rules.rule_statement: Privileged information is not discoverable. In federal question cases, privileges are determined under federal common law, pursuant to Rule 501 of the Federal Rules of Evidence. In diversity or supplemental claims in which a state’s substantive laws apply, that state’s law determines whether a privilege applies. California Distinction: Privacy Protection The California Constitution’s right to privacy may be exercised in the discovery context. The court balances the need for the discovery against the right to privacy. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 2017.020. If an objection to discovery is based on a claim of privilege or a claim that the information sought is protected work product, the response must provide sufficient factual information for other parties to evaluate the merits of that claim, including a privilege log if necessary. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 2031.240.
rules.short_rule_statement: Privileged information is not discoverable. In federal question cases, privileges are determined under federal common law, pursuant to Rule 501 of the Federal Rules of...
rules.source_text:
Privilege
Privileged information is not discoverable. In federal question cases, privileges are determined under federal common law, pursuant to Rule 501 of the Federal Rules of Evidence. In diversity or supplemental claims in which a state’s substantive laws apply, that state’s law determines whether a privilege applies. California Distinction: Privacy Protection The California Constitution’s right to privacy may be exercised in the discovery context. The court balances the need for the discovery against the right to privacy. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 2017.020. If an objection to discovery is based on a claim of privilege or a claim that the information sought is protected work product, the response must provide sufficient factual information for other parties to evaluate the merits of that claim, including a privilege log if necessary. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 2031.240.

rules.rule_statement: On motion or on its own initiative, the court is required to limit the frequency or extent of discovery otherwise allowed by the Rules if it determines that: i) The discovery sought is unreasonably cumulative or can be obtained from some other source that is more convenient or less expensive; ii) The party seeking discovery has had ample opportunity to obtain the information by discovery in the action; or iii) The proposed discovery is outside the scope permitted by Rule 26(b)(1) (not relevant and proportional). When discovery is challenged, the court must weigh the party’s interests in seeking discovery against the privacy interests of the party resisting discovery.

Required Limitations
On motion or on its own initiative, the court is required to limit the frequency or extent of discovery otherwise allowed by the Rules if it determines that: i) The discovery sought is unreasonably cumulative or can be obtained from some other source that is more convenient or less expensive; ii) The party seeking discovery has had ample opportunity to obtain the information by discovery in the action; or iii) The proposed discovery is outside the scope permitted by Rule 26(b)(1) (not relevant and proportional). When discovery is challenged, the court must weigh the party’s interests in seeking discovery against the privacy interests of the party resisting discovery.

Discretion To Alter Limits
The court may alter the limits in the Rules as to the number of depositions, interrogatories, and requests for admission. Additionally, the court may limit the length of oral depositions.

rules.rule_statement: A party is not required to provide discovery of electronically stored information from sources that the party identifies as not reasonably accessible because of undue burden or cost. If challenged, the party will have the burden to show the undue burden and cost. Even if undue burden and cost are shown, the court may still order such discovery if the requesting party shows good cause. Rule 26(b)(2)(B). Moreover, the requesting party may specify the form in which the documents should be produced.
rules.short_rule_statement: A party is not required to provide discovery of electronically stored information from sources that the party identifies as not reasonably accessible because of undue burden or...
rules.source_text:
Limitations On Electronically Stored Information
A party is not required to provide discovery of electronically stored information from sources that the party identifies as not reasonably accessible because of undue burden or cost. If challenged, the party will have the burden to show the undue burden and cost. Even if undue burden and cost are shown, the court may still order such discovery if the requesting party shows good cause. Rule 26(b)(2)(B). Moreover, the requesting party may specify the form in which the documents should be produced.

rules.rule_statement: Spoliation of evidence is the negligent or intentional destruction or significant alteration of evidence required for discovery. Spoliation of evidence may result in sanctions under Rule 37, as well as liability under an independent tort cause of action. Sanctions are authorized under Rule 37 only if the information cannot be restored or replaced by additional discovery. When retrieval of the information is possible, a court may order retrieval and assign the costs to the party who destroyed the evidence, and no further sanctions may be imposed. Rules 26(b)(2)(B) and Committee Notes on Rules—2015 Amendments to Rule 37(e). If a party has a policy in place that results in routine operations that may destroy evidence in the regular course of business, that party has a duty to affirmatively act to prevent the destruction or alteration of such evidence. Failure to do so may result in the negligent destruction of evidence. Sanctions for the negligent loss of electronically stored information may be no greater than necessary to cure the prejudice. Rule 37(e). If the court finds that the sanctioned party acted with the purpose of depriving the other party of the evidence’s use in litigation, then the available sanctions include:

In general, a party may not discover documents and tangible things that are prepared in anticipation of litigation or for trial by or for another party or its representative. rules.short_rule_statement: Rule 26(b)(3) provides limited protection for otherwise discoverable trial-preparation materials or an attorney’s work product. In general, a party may not discover documents...
rules.source_text:
Trial-Preparation Materials
Rule 26(b)(3) provides limited protection for otherwise discoverable trial-preparation materials or an attorney’s work product. In general, a party may not discover documents and tangible things that are prepared in anticipation of litigation or for trial by or for another party or its representative. However, the materials will be subject to discovery if the party shows that:

rules.canonical_name: The Party Has Substantial Need For The Materials To Prepare Its Case;
rules.rule_statement: ii) The party cannot without undue hardship obtain a substantial equivalent by other means. If the court orders discovery of trial-preparation materials, Rule 26(b)(3)(B) requires the court to protect against disclosure of the mental impressions, conclusions, opinions, or legal theories of a party’s attorney or other representative concerning the litigation. California Distinction: Attorney Work Product In California, writings containing an attorney’s impressions, conclusions, opinions, or legal research are absolutely privileged and cannot be discovered. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 2018.030. Witness statements recorded by investigators hired by an attorney are, at minimum, subject to qualified work product protection. They are discoverable if the party seeking discovery can show unfair prejudice or injustice. However, they are absolutely protected if the recordings show the attorney’s tactics, impressions, or evaluation of the case. Coito v. Superior Court, 54 Cal. 4th 480 (2012).

rules.source_text:
Signature Requirement And Certiﬁcation
Each initial or pretrial disclosure, as well as each discovery request, response, or objection, must be signed by at least one attorney of record, or by the party personally if unrepresented. By signing, the attorney or party certifies that to the best of the person’s knowledge, information, and belief formed after a reasonable inquiry:

rules.canonical_name: Regarding A Disclosure, It Is Complete And Correct As Of The Time It Is Made;
rules.rule_statement: ii) Regarding a discovery request, response, or objection, it is: a) Consistent with the Rules and warranted by existing law or by a nonfrivolous argument for extending, modifying, or reversing existing law, or for establishing new law; b) Not interposed for any improper purpose, such as to harass, cause unnecessary delay, or needlessly increase the cost of litigation; and c) Neither unreasonable nor unduly burdensome or expensive, considering the needs of the case, prior discovery in the case, the amount in controversy, and the importance of the issues at stake in the action. If a certification violates this Rule without substantial justification, the court, on motion or on its own, must impose an appropriate sanction on the signer, the party on whose behalf the signer was acting, or both. The sanction may include an order to pay the reasonable expenses, including attorney’s fees, caused by the violation. Rule 26(g).

rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 26(c), for good cause, the court may enter protective orders to protect parties and other persons from annoyance, embarrassment, oppression, or undue burden or expense resulting from discovery. For example, a party may seek a protective order limiting the time and manner of conducting a physical examination.
rules.short_rule_statement: Under Rule 26(c), for good cause, the court may enter protective orders to protect parties and other persons from annoyance, embarrassment, oppression, or undue burden or...
rules.source_text:
Protective Orders
Under Rule 26(c), for good cause, the court may enter protective orders to protect parties and other persons from annoyance, embarrassment, oppression, or undue burden or expense resulting from discovery. For example, a party may seek a protective order limiting the time and manner of conducting a physical examination.

rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 26(e)(1)(A), if a party who made a required disclosure or responded to an interrogatory, a request for production, or a request for admission learns that their disclosure or response is materially incomplete or incorrect, then the party is required to supplement or correct the disclosure or response in a timely manner. There is no duty to supplement or correct if the information has otherwise been made known to the other parties in discovery or in writing. Regarding an expert whose report must be disclosed under Rule 26(a)(2)(B), the party’s duty to supplement extends both to information included in the expert’s report and to information given during the expert’s deposition. Rule 26(e)(2). California Distinction: Continuing Duty to Disclose Unlike the Federal Rules, no California statute or rule mandates a party to supplement any previous discovery of its own accord. However, California does permit a party to demand that another party supplement its discovery disclosures twice before the initial trial date is set and once after. In addition, the court may order an additional supplemental demand on a showing of good cause. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 2030.060(a) (3). See Silvestri v. Gen’l Motors Corp., 271 F.3d 583 (4th Cir. 2001). Eckstein Marine Serv., Inc. v. M/V Basin Pride, 168 F.R.D. 38 (W.D. La. 1996). Gehring v. Case Corp., 43 F.3d 340 (7th Cir. 1994). Doe v. Norwalk Community College, 248 F.R.D. 372 (D. Conn. 2007).

However, California does permit a party to demand that another party supplement its discovery

rules.canonical_name: Failure To Participate In The Framing Of The Discovery Plan
rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 37(f), if a party or her attorney fails to participate in good faith in the development and submission of a proposed discovery plan as required by Rule 26(f), then the court may require the party or attorney to pay the reasonable expenses, including attorney’s fees, incurred because of such failure. The court must give the other party an opportunity to be heard. California Distinction: Discovery Conference Case Management Conference: The court must hold an initial case management conference within 180 days of the filing of a complaint. Under California’s “fast track” system, most unlimited cases are assigned a time goal for disposition (e.g., 75% of cases to be disposed of in 12 months, 85% in 18 months, 100% in 24 months). This system obligates the courts to dispose of cases as speedily as possible. Additional sanctions may be applied to parties who violate “fast track” rules or court orders. Cal. R. of Ct. § 3.722. Discovery Plan: Parties in California are not specifically required to “meet and confer” to plan for discovery or to provide a proposed discovery plan.

rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 30, a party may take the deposition of any party or nonparty witness at any time after the discovery conference pursuant to Rule 26(f). A party who seeks an oral deposition of a person must give reasonable written notice to every other party, stating the time and place of the deposition and, if known, the deponent’s name and address. Notice of deposition is all that is needed to compel attendance by any party, even a party who is beyond the reach of the court’s subpoena power. To compel attendance by a nonparty, a subpoena must be served. Rule 30(b). When deposing a corporation, partnership, association, or government entity, Rule 30(b)(6) states that the notice must describe with reasonable particularity the areas of inquiry. Additionally, the notice must state that the named entity has the duty to designate a representative regarding the designated areas of inquiry. The named entity must then designate one or more officers, directors, managing agents, or other persons who consent to testify on the entity’s behalf concerning those areas. Before or promptly after the notice or subpoena is served, the serving party and the organization must confer in good faith about the matters for examination. A subpoena must advise a nonparty organization of its duty to confer with the serving party. Without leave of the court, the plaintiffs, the defendants, and the third-party defendants, each as a group, are limited to 10 depositions by oral or written examination. Unless the parties agree to the deposition, leave of the court must be obtained to:

rules.source_text:
Oral Depositions
Under Rule 30, a party may take the deposition of any party or nonparty witness at any time after the discovery conference pursuant to Rule 26(f). A party who seeks an oral deposition of a person must give reasonable written notice to every other party, stating the time and place of the deposition and, if known, the deponent’s name and address. Notice of deposition is all that is needed to compel attendance by any party, even a party who is beyond the reach of the court’s subpoena power. To compel attendance by a nonparty, a subpoena must be served. Rule 30(b). When deposing a corporation, partnership, association, or government entity, Rule 30(b)(6) states that the notice must describe with reasonable particularity the areas of inquiry. Additionally, the notice must state that the named entity has the duty to designate a representative regarding the designated areas of inquiry. The named entity must then designate one or more officers, directors, managing agents, or o

rules.rule_statement: ii) Depose a witness a second time; or iii) Depose a person before the discovery conference under Rule 26(f). A deposition must be conducted before an officer who is appointed or designated under Rule 28 to administer oaths and take testimony, unless the parties agree otherwise. By stipulation of the parties or by court order, a deposition may be taken by telephonic or other remote electronic means. An oral deposition is subject to the following requirements: i) Examination of the deponent may proceed as permitted at trial under the Federal Rules of Evidence, except that cross-examination is not limited to matters raised on direct examination. Rule 30(c); ii) The deponent will be placed under oath, and the testimony will be recorded either by stenographic or electronic means. Rule 30(b); iii) Any objections are to be made on the record, but the examination still proceeds. The testimony is taken subject to any objection. Rule 30(c)(2); and iv) A person may instruct the deponent not to answer only when necessary to preserve a privilege, to enforce a limitation ordered by the court, or to present a motion to terminate or limit the examination. Rule 30(c)(2). Under Rule 30(d)(1), a deposition is limited to one day of seven hours unless the parties agree otherwise or the court orders otherwise. The court must allow additional time if needed to fairly examine the deponent, or if the deposition is impeded or delayed by a deponent, another person, or any other circumstance. At any time during an oral deposition, the deponent or a party may move to terminate or limit the deposition on the grounds that it is being conducted:

rules.rule_statement: Rule 31 allows depositions to be taken in the form of providing written questions to the deponent. Like an oral deposition, a written deposition is subject to the rules regarding leave of the court (e.g., exception to 10-deposition rule) and deposing a corporation or an organization. Written depositions must also comply with these requirements: i) To take a written deposition, the deposing party must serve the questions and a notice, identifying the deponent and the officer before whom the deposition will be taken, to all other parties. Rule 31(a)(3); ii) The other parties may then serve cross-questions, redirect questions, and re-cross questions on all other parties. Rule 31(a)(5); iii) The deposing party serves the notice and all questions on the officer designated in the notice. Rule 31(b); and iv) The officer asks the written questions of the deponent and records the deponent’s oral responses stenographically or by electronic means. Id.

Written Depositions
Rule 31 allows depositions to be taken in the form of providing written questions to the deponent. Like an oral deposition, a written deposition is subject to the rules regarding leave of the court (e.g., exception to 10-deposition rule) and deposing a corporation or an organization. Written depositions must also comply with these requirements: i) To take a written deposition, the deposing party must serve the questions and a notice, identifying the deponent and the officer before whom the deposition will be taken, to all other parties. Rule 31(a)(3); ii) The other parties may then serve cross-questions, redirect questions, and re-cross questions on all other parties. Rule 31(a)(5); iii) The deposing party serves the notice and all questions on the officer designated in the notice. Rule 31(b); and iv) The officer asks the written questions of the deponent and records the deponent’s oral responses stenographically or by electronic means. Id.

rules.rule_statement: Certain errors and irregularities in depositions are waived if not objected to in a timely manner. There must be a written objection promptly served on the party giving notice when there are irregularities with the notice and its service. In noting errors regarding the form of the written questions, the party submitting the questions should receive notice within five days after service of the last questions authorized. A seasonable objection must be made if there are errors in the manner of taking the deposition. A motion to suppress is required when there are errors related to the signing, certification, and transmittal of depositions.
rules.short_rule_statement: Certain errors and irregularities in depositions are waived if not objected to in a timely manner. There must be a written objection promptly served on the party giving notice...
rules.source_text:
Irregularities In Depositions
Certain errors and irregularities in depositions are waived if not objected to in a timely manner. There must be a written objection promptly served on the party giving notice when there are irregularities with the notice and its service. In noting errors regarding the form of the written questions, the party submitting the questions should receive notice within five days after service of the last questions authorized. A seasonable objection must be made if there are errors in the manner of taking the deposition. A motion to suppress is required when there are errors related to the signing, certification, and transmittal of depositions.

rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 33(a), any party may serve no more than 25 written interrogatories on any other party. Interrogatories may not be used on nonparty witnesses. California Distinction: Interrogatories A party may serve an unlimited number of Judicial Council form interrogatories. A party may also serve up to 35 specially drafted interrogatories, or more upon a showing of good cause. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code §§ 2030.010 et seq.
rules.short_rule_statement: Under Rule 33(a), any party may serve no more than 25 written interrogatories on any other party. Interrogatories may not be used on nonparty witnesses. California Distinction:...
rules.source_text:
Availability
Under Rule 33(a), any party may serve no more than 25 written interrogatories on any other party. Interrogatories may not be used on nonparty witnesses. California Distinction: Interrogatories A party may serve an unlimited number of Judicial Council form interrogatories. A party may also serve up to 35 specially drafted interrogatories, or more upon a showing of good cause. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code §§ 2030.010 et seq.

rules.rule_statement: Interrogatories may relate to any matters permitted under Rule 26(b)(1)—nonprivileged matters relevant to any party’s claim or defense and proportional to the needs of the case. Rule 33(a)(2). An interrogatory is not objectionable merely because it asks for an opinion or contention that relates to fact or the application of law to fact. However, the court may order that the interrogatory need not be answered until designated discovery is complete or until a pretrial conference or some other time.
rules.short_rule_statement: Interrogatories may relate to any matters permitted under Rule 26(b)(1)—nonprivileged matters relevant to any party’s claim or defense and proportional to the needs of the...
rules.source_text:
Scope
Interrogatories may relate to any matters permitted under Rule 26(b)(1)—nonprivileged matters relevant to any party’s claim or defense and proportional to the needs of the case. Rule 33(a)(2). An interrogatory is not objectionable merely because it asks for an opinion or contention that relates to fact or the application of law to fact. However, the court may order that the interrogatory need not be answered until designated discovery is complete or until a pretrial conference or some other time.

rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 33(b)(1), the interrogatories must be answered by the party to whom they are directed. If that party is a corporation, partnership, association, or government entity, the interrogatories must be answered by any officer or agent who must furnish the information available to the party. The person who makes the answers must sign them. Rule 33(b)(5). Each interrogatory must be answered fully and separately under oath unless the responding party objects to the interrogatory. A responding party must serve its answers (and any objections) within 30 days after being served with the interrogatories. The court may order or the parties may stipulate to a shorter or longer time. Rule 33(b)(4).
rules.short_rule_statement: Under Rule 33(b)(1), the interrogatories must be answered by the party to whom they are directed. If that party is a corporation, partnership, association, or government...
rules.source_text:
Answers
Under Rule 33(b)(1), the interrogatories must be answered by the party to whom they are directed. If that party is a corporation, partnership, association, or government entity, the interrogatories must be answered by any officer or agent who must furnish the information available to the party. The person who makes the answers must sign them. Rule 33(b)(5). Each interrogatory must be answered fully and separately under oath unless the responding party objects to the interrogatory. A responding party must serve its answers (and any objections) within 30 days after being served with the interrogatories. The court may order or the parties may stipulate to a shorter or longer time. Rule 33(b)(4).

rules.rule_statement: The grounds for objecting to an interrogatory must be stated with specificity. Any ground not stated in a timely objection is deemed waived unless the court excuses the failure for good cause. Rule 33(b)(2), (3). The attorney who objects must sign any objections. Rule 33(b)(5). Like an answer, any objections must be served by the responding party within 30 days after being served with the interrogatories. The court may order or the parties may stipulate to a shorter or longer time. Rule 33(b)(4).
rules.short_rule_statement: The grounds for objecting to an interrogatory must be stated with specificity. Any ground not stated in a timely objection is deemed waived unless the court excuses the failure...
rules.source_text:
Objections
The grounds for objecting to an interrogatory must be stated with specificity. Any ground not stated in a timely objection is deemed waived unless the court excuses the failure for good cause. Rule 33(b)(2), (3). The attorney who objects must sign any objections. Rule 33(b)(5). Like an answer, any objections must be served by the responding party within 30 days after being served with the interrogatories. The court may order or the parties may stipulate to a shorter or longer time. Rule 33(b)(4).

rules.rule_statement: Rule 33(d) provides a mechanism for a responding party to answer an interrogatory by producing business records. This mechanism can be used when an answer to an interrogatory may be ascertained from (1) the responding party’s business records (including electronically stored information) or (2) a summation of the responding party’s business records. To exercise this option, the burden of deriving answers must be substantially the same for the party serving the interrogatories as for the responding party. Under this option, the responding party may answer the interrogatory by:

Option To Produce Business Records

To exercise this option, the burden of deriving answers must be substantially the same for the party serving the interrogatories as for the responding party. Under this option, the responding party may answer the interrogatory by:

rules.rule_statement: Rule 34 provides a party with a right to issue discovery to another party for the inspection of documents, tangible things, and land. Although these discovery requests may be directed only to other parties, nonparties may be compelled to produce documents and other things or submit to an inspection pursuant to a subpoena served under Rule 45. Rule 34(c).
rules.short_rule_statement: Rule 34 provides a party with a right to issue discovery to another party for the inspection of documents, tangible things, and land. Although these discovery requests may be...
rules.source_text:
In General
Rule 34 provides a party with a right to issue discovery to another party for the inspection of documents, tangible things, and land. Although these discovery requests may be directed only to other parties, nonparties may be compelled to produce documents and other things or submit to an inspection pursuant to a subpoena served under Rule 45. Rule 34(c).

rules.rule_statement: A party may serve on any other party a request to produce documents and tangible things in the responding party’s possession, custody, or control. Rule 34(a)(1). With a request to produce, the requesting party (or its representative) has the right to inspect, copy, test, or sample the following items: i) Any designated documents or electronically stored information in any medium from which information can be obtained either directly or, if necessary, after translation by the responding party into a reasonably usable form; or ii) Any designated tangible things. Designated documents or electronically stored information in a request to produce includes:

Documents And Tangible Things
A party may serve on any other party a request to produce documents and tangible things in the responding party’s possession, custody, or control. Rule 34(a)(1). With a request to produce, the requesting party (or its representative) has the right to inspect, copy, test, or sample the following items: i) Any designated documents or electronically stored information in any medium from which information can be obtained either directly or, if necessary, after translation by the responding party into a reasonably usable form; or ii) Any designated tangible things. Designated documents or electronically stored information in a request to produce includes:

rules.rule_statement: A party may also serve a request to enter onto another party’s land. Pursuant to this type of discovery request, the demanding party enters another party’s land to inspect, measure, survey, photograph, test, or sample the property, if relevant to the action. A request to inspect property can include a request to inspect a designated object or operation on the responding party’s property. Rule 34(a)(2).

Inspection Of Land

Pursuant to this type of discovery request, the demanding party enters another party’s land to inspect, measure, survey, photograph, test, or sample the property, if relevant to the action. A request to inspect property can include a request to inspect a designated object or operation on the responding party’s property. Rule 34(a)(2).

rules.rule_statement: Generally, the party to whom the request is directed must respond in writing within 30 days after being served, unless a shorter or longer time is stipulated to by the parties or ordered by court. If the request to produce was served before the parties’ first Rule 26(f) conference, the responding party must respond within 30 days after the parties’ first Rule 26(f) conference. This time limitation may be shortened or expanded by stipulation or court order. If a party fails to respond to a document request or to allow inspection, or objects to a request, then the requesting party may move to compel under Rule 37(a). Rule 34(b)(2)(A). The responding party may state that it will produce copies of documents or electronically stored information instead of permitting inspection. The production must be completed no later than the time for inspection specified in the request or another reasonable time specified in the response. Rule 34(b)(2)(B). If a request does not specify a form for the production of electronically stored information, then a party must produce it in a form or forms in which it is ordinarily maintained or in a reasonably usable form or forms. A party need not produce the same electronically stored information in more than one form. Rule 34(b)(2)(E).

Responses And Objections
Generally, the party to whom the request is directed must respond in writing within 30 days after being served, unless a shorter or longer time is stipulated to by the parties or ordered by court. If the request to produce was served before the parties’ first Rule 26(f) conference, the responding party must respond within 30 days after the parties’ first Rule 26(f) conference. This time limitation may be shortened or expanded by stipulation or court order. If a party fails to respond to a document request or to allow inspection, or objects to a request, then the requesting party may move to compel under Rule 37(a). Rule 34(b)(2)(A). The responding party may state that it will produce copies of documents or electronically stored information instead of permitting inspection.
