rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 35(a), if the mental or physical condition (including blood group) of a party is in controversy, then the court may order that person to submit to a physical or mental examination by a “suitably licensed or certified examiner.” Rule 35(a) also permits the examination of a person in the legal custody or control of a party. A suitably licensed or certified examiner includes physicians and other professionals licensed to report on physical or mental conditions, such as dentists and psychologists. California Distinction: Physical Exam An attorney in California must be permitted to attend a physical exam. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 2032.510.
rules.short_rule_statement: Under Rule 35(a), if the mental or physical condition (including blood group) of a party is in controversy, then the court may order that person to submit to a physical or...
rules.source_text:
In General
Under Rule 35(a), if the mental or physical condition (including blood group) of a party is in controversy, then the court may order that person to submit to a physical or mental examination by a “suitably licensed or certified examiner.” Rule 35(a) also permits the examination of a person in the legal custody or control of a party. A suitably licensed or certified examiner includes physicians and other professionals licensed to report on physical or mental conditions, such as dentists and psychologists. California Distinction: Physical Exam An attorney in California must be permitted to attend a physical exam. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 2032.510.

rules.rule_statement: An order for a physical or mental examination of a party (or someone in the party’s custody or control) may be made only upon motion. The requesting party must show good cause. The person to be examined and all parties must be given prior notice specifying the time, place, conditions, and scope of the examination and the identity of the examiner. Rule 35(a)(2).
rules.short_rule_statement: An order for a physical or mental examination of a party (or someone in the party’s custody or control) may be made only upon motion. The requesting party must show good cause....
rules.source_text:
Procedure
An order for a physical or mental examination of a party (or someone in the party’s custody or control) may be made only upon motion. The requesting party must show good cause. The person to be examined and all parties must be given prior notice specifying the time, place, conditions, and scope of the examination and the identity of the examiner. Rule 35(a)(2).

rules.rule_statement: On request by the party or person examined, the party who requested the examination must provide a copy of the examiner’s written report. The examiner’s report must include results of all tests, diagnoses, and conclusions as well as any reports of earlier examinations of the same condition. If the examiner refuses to make a report, then the examiner’s testimony may be excluded at trial. Rule 35(b).
rules.short_rule_statement: On request by the party or person examined, the party who requested the examination must provide a copy of the examiner’s written report. The examiner’s report must include...
rules.source_text:
The Examiner’S Report
On request by the party or person examined, the party who requested the examination must provide a copy of the examiner’s written report. The examiner’s report must include results of all tests, diagnoses, and conclusions as well as any reports of earlier examinations of the same condition. If the examiner refuses to make a report, then the examiner’s testimony may be excluded at trial. Rule 35(b).

rules.rule_statement: After delivery of the examiner’s report, the examined party must provide the party who moved for the examination, on request, a report of all other previous or subsequent examinations of the same condition in that party’s custody or control. Rule 35(b)(3). If the party who was examined obtains the report of the examination or conducts a deposition of the examiner, then the party waives any privilege it may have regarding the testimony of any other person who has examined or subsequently examines the party concerning the same condition. Rule 35(b)(4).
rules.short_rule_statement: After delivery of the examiner’s report, the examined party must provide the party who moved for the examination, on request, a report of all other previous or subsequent...
rules.source_text:
Other Examinations
After delivery of the examiner’s report, the examined party must provide the party who moved for the examination, on request, a report of all other previous or subsequent examinations of the same condition in that party’s custody or control. Rule 35(b)(3). If the party who was examined obtains the report of the examination or conducts a deposition of the examiner, then the party waives any privilege it may have regarding the testimony of any other person who has examined or subsequently examines the party concerning the same condition. Rule 35(b)(4).

rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 36, a party may serve upon any other party a written request for the admission of any relevant, nonprivileged matters discoverable under Rule 26. The requested matters may relate to statements or opinions of fact or to the application of law to fact, including the genuineness of any documents described in the request. Rule 36(a)(1). Each matter for which an admission is requested must be specified separately. A request to admit the genuineness of a document must be accompanied by a copy of the document unless it is, or has been, otherwise furnished or made available for inspection and copying. Rule 36(a)(2). California Distinction: Requests for Admission In California, a party is limited to 35 requests for admission without good cause shown. However, a party may serve an unlimited number of requests for admission of documents. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 2033.030.
rules.short_rule_statement: Under Rule 36, a party may serve upon any other party a written request for the admission of any relevant, nonprivileged matters discoverable under Rule 26. The requested...
rules.source_text:
In General
Under Rule 36, a party may serve upon any other party a written request for the admission of any relevant, nonprivileged matters discoverable under Rule 26. The requested matters may relate to statements or opinions of fact or to the application of law to fact, including the genuineness of any documents described in the request. Rule 36(a)(1). Each matter for which an admission is requested must be specified separately. A request to admit the genuineness of a document must be accompanied by a copy of the document unless it is, or has been, otherwise furnished or made available for inspection and copying. Rule 36(a)(2). California Distinction: Requests for Admission In California, a party is limited to 35 requests for admission without good cause shown. However, a party may serve an unlimited number of requests for admission of documents. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 2033.030.

rules.rule_statement: A matter will be admitted unless the responding party serves the requesting party with a written answer or objection addressed to the matter. A response to a request for admissions must be served within 30 days after being served with the request and must be signed by the party or her attorney. Rule 36(a)(3). If a matter is not admitted, then the answer must specifically deny it or state in detail why the answering party cannot truthfully admit or deny it. A denial must fairly respond to the substance of the matter. When good faith requires that a party qualify an answer or deny only a part of a matter, the answer must specify the part admitted and qualify or deny the rest. Rule 36(a)(4). An answering party may assert lack of knowledge or information as a reason for failing to admit or deny only if the party states:
rules.short_rule_statement: A matter will be admitted unless the responding party serves the requesting party with a written answer or objection addressed to the matter. A response to a request for...
rules.source_text:
Responses And Objections
A matter will be admitted unless the responding party serves the requesting party with a written answer or objection addressed to the matter. A response to a request for admissions must be served within 30 days after being served with the request and must be signed by the party or her attorney. Rule 36(a)(3). If a matter is not admitted, then the answer must specifically deny it or state in detail why the answering party cannot truthfully admit or deny it. A denial must fairly respond to the substance of the matter. When good faith requires that a party qualify an answer or deny only a part of a matter, the answer must specify the part admitted and qualify or deny the rest. Rule 36(a)(4). An answering party may assert lack of knowledge or information as a reason for failing to admit or deny only if the party states:

Under Rule 33(c), an answer to an interrogatory may be used to the extent allowed by Federal Rule of Evidence 801(d)(2), whereby a party’s answer will generally constitute a party admission. However, an interrogatory answer could constitute hearsay if offered against another party.

rules.rule_statement: If a party fails to make the automatic disclosures required by Rule 26(a) or fails to respond to discovery that has been properly served, then the party seeking the information may move to compel such disclosure or discovery. An evasive or incomplete disclosure, answer, or response is treated as a failure to disclose, answer, or respond. Rule 37(a). California Distinction: Motion to Compel A party who believes a response to discovery is incomplete, evasive, or contains baseless objections may move to compel a further response. However, the party must first make a reasonable and good-faith effort to informally resolve the issues. See Obregon v. Superior Court, 79 Cal. Rptr. 2d 62 (Ct. App. 1998).
rules.short_rule_statement: If a party fails to make the automatic disclosures required by Rule 26(a) or fails to respond to discovery that has been properly served, then the party seeking the information...
rules.source_text:
In General
If a party fails to make the automatic disclosures required by Rule 26(a) or fails to respond to discovery that has been properly served, then the party seeking the information may move to compel such disclosure or discovery. An evasive or incomplete disclosure, answer, or response is treated as a failure to disclose, answer, or respond. Rule 37(a). California Distinction: Motion to Compel A party who believes a response to discovery is incomplete, evasive, or contains baseless objections may move to compel a further response. However, the party must first make a reasonable and good-faith effort to informally resolve the issues. See Obregon v. Superior Court, 79 Cal. Rptr. 2d 62 (Ct. App. 1998).

rules.rule_statement: A motion to compel must be served on all parties and be accompanied by a certificate that the movant has in good faith conferred or attempted to confer with the opposing party in an effort to obtain the disclosure or secure the information or material without court action. Rule 37(a)(1). If the subject of the motion to compel is a party, then the motion must be filed with the court in which the action is pending. If the subject of the motion is a nonparty who was subpoenaed under Rule 45, then the motion must be filed in the court that issued the subpoena. Rule 37(a)(2).

rules.source_text:
Procedure
A motion to compel must be served on all parties and be accompanied by a certificate that the movant has in good faith conferred or attempted to confer with the opposing party in an effort to obtain the disclosure or secure the information or material without court action. Rule 37(a)(1). If the subject of the motion to compel is a party, then the motion must be filed with the court in which the action is pending. If the subject of the motion is a nonparty who was subpoenaed under Rule 45, then the motion must be filed in the court that issued the subpoena. Rule 37(a)(2).

If the motion to compel is denied and the court determines that the motion was made without substantial justification, then, after providing an opportunity to be heard, the court must require the movant, the attorney filing the motion, or both to pay the party (or person) who opposed the motion the reasonable expenses (including attorney’s fees) incurred in opposing the motion. Rule 37(a)(5)(B). If the motion is granted in part and denied in part, then the court may apportion fees in its discretion after providing an opportunity for the parties to be heard. Rule 37(a)(5)(C).

rules.rule_statement: If a party fails to obey a court order regarding discovery, then the court may impose any of the following sanctions under Rule 37(b)(2): i) Directing that the matters addressed in the order or other facts be taken as established for the purposes of the action; ii) Prohibiting the disobedient party from supporting or opposing designated claims or defenses, or from introducing designated matters in evidence; iii) Striking pleadings in whole or in part; iv) Staying further proceedings until the order is obeyed;

rules.rule_statement: In addition to the Rule 37(b)(2) sanctions, the court may also require the disobedient party, the party’s attorney, or both to pay the movant’s reasonable expenses (including attorney’s fees) incurred as a result of the failure to comply, except when the failure was substantially justified or imposition of fees would be unjust. A court order for monetary sanctions in the form of reasonable expenses can be made in lieu of or in addition to any other sanctions. Rule 37(b)(2)(C).

A court order for monetary sanctions in the form of reasonable expenses can be made in lieu of or in addition to any other sanctions. Rule 37(b)(2)(C).

rules.canonical_name: Failure To Make Or Supplement Automatic Disclosures
rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 37(c)(1), if a party fails to make or supplement its automatic disclosures as required by Rules 26(a) and (e), then the party will not be permitted to use the undisclosed documents or call the undisclosed witnesses unless the nondisclosure was substantially justified or was harmless. This Rule applies to the use of such evidence at trial and for motions and hearings. It does not apply to the use of such evidence for impeachment purposes because Rule 26(a) does not require disclosure of impeachment evidence or witnesses.

It does not apply to the use of such evidence for impeachment purposes because Rule 26(a) does not require disclosure of impeachment evidence or witnesses.

rules.rule_statement: Sanctions are subject to review under the abuse-of-discretion standard. The abuse-of-discretion standard of review is a highly deferential standard of review under which an appellate court can reverse a trial judge’s discretionary ruling only if it was unreasonable or arbitrary. Generally, the harshest sanctions—dismissal of the action and entry of a default judgment—are reserved for misconduct that is serious, repeated, contumacious, extreme, or inexcusable. Generally, a court should impose lesser sanctions before issuing more severe ones. California Point of Law: Discovery Sanctions California’s sanctions rules are generally similar to the Federal Rules. In California, initial misuse of the discovery process results in a monetary sanction unless the party acted with substantial justification or the sanction would be unjust. Further instances of abuse result in sanctions of escalating seriousness. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 2023.030. A California court must impose a $1,000 sanction (in addition to any other sanctions) upon a party, person, or attorney if, in reviewing a request for sanctions, the court finds any of the following: production of documents or to an inspection demand; ii) The party, person, or attorney produced requested documents within 7 days of a scheduled hearing for a motion to compel production of the records that was filed by the requesting party because of the other party, person, or attorney’s failure to respond in good faith; or iii) The party, person, or attorney failed to confer in person, by telephone, or by letter or other communication in writing with the party or attorney requesting the documents in a reasonable and good-faith attempt to resolve informally any dispute concerning the request.

Standard Of Review
Sanctions are subject to review under the abuse-of-discretion standard. The abuse-of-discretion standard of review is a highly deferential standard of review under which an appellate court can reverse a trial judge’s discretionary ruling only if it was unreasonable or arbitrary. Generally, the harshest sanctions—dismissal of the action and entry of a default judgment—are reserved for misconduct that is serious, repeated, contumacious, extreme, or inexcusable. Generally, a court should impose lesser sanctions before issuing more severe ones. In California, initial misuse of the discovery process results in a monetary sanction unless the party acted with substantial justification or the sanction would be unjust. rules.rule_statement: The court may hold a final pretrial conference to formulate a trial plan, including a plan to facilitate the admission of evidence. The conference must be held as close to the start of the trial as is reasonable. The conference must be attended by at least one attorney who will conduct the trial for each party and by any unrepresented party. The court should issue an order reciting the action taken and may modify the order issued after a final pretrial conference only to prevent manifest injustice. Rule 16(d), (e).

Final Pretrial Conference And Order
The court may hold a final pretrial conference to formulate a trial plan, including a plan to facilitate the admission of evidence. The conference must be held as close to the start of the trial as is reasonable. The conference must be attended by at least one attorney who will conduct the trial for each party and by any unrepresented party. The court should issue an order reciting the action taken and may modify the order issued after a final pretrial conference only to prevent manifest injustice. Rule 16(d), (e).

rules.rule_statement: If counsel or a party fails to appear at a pretrial or scheduling conference, fails to participate in good faith, or fails to obey a pretrial or scheduling conference order, then the court may generally impose the same sanctions as those permitted for failure of a party to comply with a discovery order, including contempt of court or dismissal of an action (see § VII.F.2. Sanctions, supra). Dismissal of an action is a severe sanction. Generally, dismissal is appropriate only when a party’s conduct is serious, repeated, extreme, and otherwise inexcusable. Instead of or in addition to any other sanction, the court must order the party, its attorney, or both to pay the reasonable expenses (including attorney’s fees) incurred because of any noncompliance with this Rule, unless the noncompliance was substantially justified or other circumstances make an award of expenses unjust. Rule 16(f).

rules.rule_statement: Rule 41 provides procedure for obtaining dismissal of a complaint, counterclaim, crossclaim, or third-party claim. Dismissal can be voluntary or involuntary under the Rule.
rules.short_rule_statement: Rule 41 provides procedure for obtaining dismissal of a complaint, counterclaim, crossclaim, or third-party claim. Dismissal can be voluntary or involuntary under the Rule.
rules.source_text:
Dismissal
Rule 41 provides procedure for obtaining dismissal of a complaint, counterclaim, crossclaim, or third-party claim. Dismissal can be voluntary or involuntary under the Rule.

components.content: By ﬁling notice or by stipulation of the parties
components.source_text: 1)      By ﬁling notice or by stipulation of the parties

rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 41(a)(1)(A), a plaintiff may dismiss an action without leave of the court: i) By filing a notice of dismissal at any time before the opposing party serves either an answer or a motion for summary judgment; or ii) By filing a stipulation of dismissal signed by all parties who have appeared in the action. Unless otherwise stated in the notice or stipulation, the voluntary dismissal will be without prejudice. A dismissal is without prejudice when the plaintiff is permitted to sue the defendant on the same claim in the future. However, if a plaintiff voluntarily dismissed a prior state or federal action based on the same claim by filing a notice of dismissal, then a subsequent dismissal of the same claim by notice will be with prejudice. This is referred to as the two-dismissal rule. Rule 41(a)(1)(B). California Distinction: Voluntary Dismissal Voluntary dismissal without prejudice is generally available to the plaintiff as a matter of right before the actual commencement of trial. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 581(c); see Rosen begins, a plaintiff may only dismiss an action with prejudice unless the parties agree otherwise or good cause is shown.

rules.rule_statement: Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 581(e). Except as provided previously, an action may be dismissed at the plaintiff’s request only by court order on terms that the court considers proper. Rule 41(a)(2). A voluntary dismissal by court order is without prejudice unless the order states otherwise. However, when a plaintiff moves for dismissal without prejudice under Rule 41(a)(2), the decision to dismiss with or without prejudice is left to the discretion of the court. Voluntary dismissal without prejudice is not a matter of right. The purpose of Rule 41(a)(2) is primarily to prevent voluntary dismissals that unfairly affect the defendant. If voluntary dismissal under Rule 41(a)(2) would unfairly affect the defendant, then the court may respond to a Rule 41(a)(2) motion to dismiss with an order dismissing the action with prejudice. However, if the court intends to do so, it must provide the plaintiff with notice of intent and an opportunity to respond or withdraw the motion.

Effect Of Dismissal With Prejudice
If the dismissal by an order of the court under Rule 41(a)(2) is with prejudice, then the dismissal operates as an adjudication on the merits.

rules.rule_statement: The “two-dismissal” rule is not generally implicated when the first dismissal is by an order of the court under Rule 41(a)(2) because: i) A dismissal obtained under Rule 41(a)(2) does not pose the same danger of abuse or harassment as does a unilateral Rule 41(a)(1) dismissal by the plaintiff’s notice; and ii) The court has discretion to determine whether a Rule 41(a)(2) dismissal will be with or without prejudice or conditions. However, when a plaintiff has a history of vexatiously delaying the adjudication of dispositive issues by interposing meritless motions or voluntary dismissals of the same claims, courts often find that a voluntary dismissal would unfairly affect the defendant and choose to dismiss the case with prejudice to prevent continued vexatious litigation and impermissible forum-shopping. A voluntary dismissal of a counterclaim, crossclaim, or third-party claim must be made before a responsive pleading is served. If there is no responsive pleading, the voluntary dismissal must be made before evidence is introduced at a hearing or trial.

Application Of The Two-Dismissal Rule
The “two-dismissal” rule is not generally implicated when the first dismissal is by an order of the court under Rule 41(a)(2) because: i) A dismissal obtained under Rule 41(a)(2) does not pose the same danger of abuse or harassment as does a unilateral Rule 41(a)(1) dismissal by the plaintiff’s notice; and ii) The court has discretion to determine whether a Rule 41(a)(2) dismissal will be with or without prejudice or conditions. However, when a plaintiff has a history of vexatiously delaying the adjudication of dispositive issues by interposing meritless motions or voluntary dismissals of the same claims, courts often find that a voluntary dismissal would unfairly affect the defendant and choose to dismiss the case with prejudice to prevent continued vexatious litigation and impermissible forum-shopping. rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 41(b), if the plaintiff fails to prosecute or to comply with the Rules or a court order, then a defendant may move to dismiss the action or any claim against him. Unless the court’s dismissal order specifies otherwise, a dismissal under Rule 41(b) is with prejudice and operates as an adjudication on the merits. However, a dismissal based on a lack of jurisdiction, improper venue, or failure to join an indispensable party under Rule 19 does not operate as an adjudication on the merits. California Distinction: Failure to Prosecute Under California’s “diligent prosecution” statutes, a state court may dismiss an action for failure to bring the matter to trial upon its own motion or a party’s motion. The “diligent prosecution” statutes apply to cases not subject to the “fast track” system (whose rules also include the possibility of dismissal). The court may dismiss an action if 2 years have elapsed since the filing of the complaint and must dismiss after 5 years. If the complaint has not been served, the court must dismiss after 3 years. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 583.110 et seq.

Defaults and default judgments are considered drastic remedies and are strongly disfavored by the courts.

Default
When a party has failed to plead or otherwise defend an action and that failure is shown by affidavit or otherwise, the court clerk must enter the party’s default. Rule 55(a).

rules.rule_statement: Once a default is entered against a party, the plaintiff may seek a default judgment. If the relief sought is a sum certain or an amount that can be made certain by computation, and the defendant who failed to appear is neither a minor nor an incompetent person, the default judgment must be entered by the court clerk on the plaintiff’s request accompanied by an affidavit showing the amount due. Rule 55(b)(1). Otherwise, the plaintiff must apply to the court for a default judgment. If the party against whom a default judgment is sought has appeared personally or by a representative, that party or its representative must be served with written notice of the application at least seven days before a hearing is held. The court may conduct a hearing when it needs to:

Default Judgment
Once a default is entered against a party, the plaintiff may seek a default judgment. If the relief sought is a sum certain or an amount that can be made certain by computation, and the defendant who failed to appear is neither a minor nor an incompetent person, the default judgment must be entered by the court clerk on the plaintiff’s request accompanied by an affidavit showing the amount due. Rule 55(b)(1). Otherwise, the plaintiff must apply to the court for a default judgment. If the party against whom a default judgment is sought has appeared personally or by a representative, that party or its representative must be served with written notice of the application at least seven days before a hearing is held. The court may conduct a hearing when it needs to:

rules.rule_statement: The movant has the burden of persuasion on a motion for summary judgment. Once the movant makes a prima facie showing that summary judgment is appropriate, the burden shifts to the opposing party to provide specific evidence showing the existence of a genuine issue of fact for trial. California Distinction: Burdens in Summary Judgment Unlike Rule 56, the California statute governing motions for summary judgment details the parties’ burdens. When the moving defendant establishes that a cause of action is meritless, or a moving plaintiff shows that there is no defense, the burden shifts to the opponent to show that a triable issue of fact exists. In meeting these burdens, the plaintiff may present evidence negating the opponent’s cause of action or the defense or may show that the opponent has not or cannot obtain the evidence necessary to support the claim or defense. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 437c.
rules.short_rule_statement: The movant has the burden of persuasion on a motion for summary judgment. Once the movant makes a prima facie showing that summary judgment is appropriate, the burden shifts to...
rules.source_text:
Burden Of Proof
The movant has the burden of persuasion on a motion for summary judgment. Once the movant makes a prima facie showing that summary judgment is appropriate, the burden shifts to the opposing party to provide specific evidence showing the existence of a genuine issue of fact for trial. California Distinction: Burdens in Summary Judgment Unlike Rule 56, the California statute governing motions for summary judgment details the parties’ burdens. When the moving defendant establishes that a cause of action is meritless, or a moving plaintiff shows that there is no defense, the burden shifts to the opponent to show that a triable issue of fact exists. In meeting these burdens, the plaintiff may present evidence negating the opponent’s cause of action or the defense or may show that the opponent has not or cannot obtain the evidence necessary to support the claim or defense. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 437c.

rules.rule_statement: In deciding a motion for summary judgment, the court must consider the materials cited by the parties even if they are not presented in a form that is admissible at trial, as long as the facts contained in the submissions are admissible at trial. Examples of such materials include: (1) affidavits, (2) documents, (3) electronically stored information, (4) pleadings, (5) deposition transcripts, (6) interrogatory answers, (7) admissions, and (8) stipulations filed by the parties. The court is not limited to the materials cited by the parties and may also consider other materials in the record. Supporting and opposing affidavits must:

rules.source_text:
Evidence
In deciding a motion for summary judgment, the court must consider the materials cited by the parties even if they are not presented in a form that is admissible at trial, as long as the facts contained in the submissions are admissible at trial. Examples of such materials include: (1) affidavits, (2) documents, (3) electronically stored information, (4) pleadings, (5) deposition transcripts, (6) interrogatory answers, (7) admissions, and (8) stipulations filed by the parties. The court is not limited to the materials cited by the parties and may also consider other materials in the record. Supporting and opposing affidavits must:

rules.rule_statement: ii) Set out facts that would be admissible in evidence; and iii) Establish the affiant’s competency to testify on the matters stated. An unsworn declaration made under penalty of perjury will have the same force and effect as an affidavit if it meets the above requirements and is signed and dated by the person providing the facts. California Distinction: Objections to Evidence A court considering a motion for summary judgment need rule only on the objections to evidence that it deems material to the motion’s disposition. However, objections to evidence that the court does not rule on in granting or denying the motion are preserved for review on appeal. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 437c(q).

Be Made On Personal Knowledge
ii) Set out facts that would be admissible in evidence; and iii) Establish the affiant’s competency to testify on the matters stated. An unsworn declaration made under penalty of perjury will have the same force and effect as an affidavit if it meets the above requirements and is signed and dated by the person providing the facts. California Distinction: Objections to Evidence A court considering a motion for summary judgment need rule only on the objections to evidence that it deems material to the motion’s disposition. However, objections to evidence that the court does not rule on in granting or denying the motion are preserved for review on appeal. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 437c(q).

rules.rule_statement: If a motion for summary judgment is properly made and supported, an opposing party may not rely merely on allegations or denials in their pleading but must provide specific facts showing a genuine dispute for trial. Although there is no explicit requirement in Rule 56 that discovery be complete before summary judgment is entered, granting summary judgment is generally improper unless the nonmovant is given a sufficient opportunity to obtain discovery. If the opposing party shows by affidavit or declaration that for specified reasons they cannot present facts essential to oppose the motion, then the court has discretion to:

rules.source_text:
Response By The Opposing Party
If a motion for summary judgment is properly made and supported, an opposing party may not rely merely on allegations or denials in their pleading but must provide specific facts showing a genuine dispute for trial. Although there is no explicit requirement in Rule 56 that discovery be complete before summary judgment is entered, granting summary judgment is generally improper unless the nonmovant is given a sufficient opportunity to obtain discovery. If the opposing party shows by affidavit or declaration that for specified reasons they cannot present facts essential to oppose the motion, then the court has discretion to:

rules.canonical_name: Defer Considering The Motion Or Deny It
rules.rule_statement: ii) Provide the parties with time to obtain affidavits or declarations or to conduct discovery; or iii) Issue any other appropriate order. Rule 56(d). If a party fails to properly support an assertion of fact or fails to properly address another party’s assertion of fact as required by Rule 56(c), then the court may:

If a party fails to properly support an assertion of fact or fails to properly address another party’s assertion of fact as required by Rule 56(c), then the court may:
topic_path: Defer Considering The Motion Or Deny It

rules.canonical_name: Give An Opportunity To Properly Support Or Address The Fact
rules.rule_statement: ii) Consider the fact undisputed for the purposes of the motion; iii) Grant summary judgment if the motion and supporting materials show that the movant is entitled to it; or iv) Issue any other appropriate order. Rule 56(e). California Distinction: Evidence in Summary Judgment A party moving for summary judgment must file and serve a separate statement listing all material facts claimed to be undisputed, with supporting evidence for each claim. Failure to do so is grounds to deny the motion. The opposing party has a similar requirement—to provide a statement of those facts alleged to be in dispute, along with supporting evidence. The opposing party’s failure to provide this statement and supporting evidence is grounds to grant the motion. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 437c (b)(1).

rules.rule_statement: A partial summary judgment is a court’s judgment for one party and against another on one or more—but not all—issues without a full trial. Even if the court does not grant the full relief requested by a motion for summary judgment, it may enter an order stating any material fact that is not genuinely in dispute and treating the fact as established in the case. Material facts subject to partial summary judgment include an item of damages or other relief. Rule 56(g).
rules.short_rule_statement: A partial summary judgment is a court’s judgment for one party and against another on one or more—but not all—issues without a full trial. Even if the court does not grant the...
rules.source_text:
Partial Summary Judgment
A partial summary judgment is a court’s judgment for one party and against another on one or more—but not all—issues without a full trial. Even if the court does not grant the full relief requested by a motion for summary judgment, it may enter an order stating any material fact that is not genuinely in dispute and treating the fact as established in the case. Material facts subject to partial summary judgment include an item of damages or other relief. Rule 56(g).

rules.rule_statement: Unless a different time is set by local rule, a party may file a motion for summary judgment at any time until 30 days after the close of all discovery. Rule 56(b). California Distinction: Time for Motion In California, a party may move for summary judgment 60 days after the opponent’s general appearance (or earlier, if the court directs). Unlike in the federal system, a movant must serve all papers 81 days before the hearing date. Opposition papers must be filed 20 days before the hearing, and reply papers 11 days before the hearing. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 437c (a), (b).
rules.short_rule_statement: Unless a different time is set by local rule, a party may file a motion for summary judgment at any time until 30 days after the close of all discovery. Rule 56(b). California...
rules.source_text:
Time For Making The Motion
Unless a different time is set by local rule, a party may file a motion for summary judgment at any time until 30 days after the close of all discovery. Rule 56(b). California Distinction: Time for Motion In California, a party may move for summary judgment 60 days after the opponent’s general appearance (or earlier, if the court directs). Unlike in the federal system, a movant must serve all papers 81 days before the hearing date. Opposition papers must be filed 20 days before the hearing, and reply papers 11 days before the hearing. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 437c (a), (b).

rules.rule_statement: A grant of full summary judgment is a final disposition on the merits and is subject to appeal. In general, an order denying summary judgment is not a final disposition and is not subject to immediate appeal. Depending on the grounds for the denial, a party may be required to file a sufficiency-of-the-evidence claim in a Rule 50 motion for judgment as a matter of law to preserve the claim for appeal. Basis for denial Rule 50 motion to preserve appeal Factual grounds Required Legal grounds Not required California Distinction: Appeal of Summary Judgment Grants of summary judgment are reviewable upon a writ of mandamus. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 437c (m).

Appeal
A grant of full summary judgment is a final disposition on the merits and is subject to appeal. In general, an order denying summary judgment is not a final disposition and is not subject to immediate appeal. Depending on the grounds for the denial, a party may be required to file a sufficiency-of-the-evidence claim in a Rule 50 motion for judgment as a matter of law to preserve the claim for appeal. Basis for denial Rule 50 motion to preserve appeal Factual grounds Required Legal grounds Not required California Distinction: Appeal of Summary Judgment Grants of summary judgment are reviewable upon a writ of mandamus. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 437c (m).

rules.rule_statement: A declaratory judgment is a ruling in which the court tells the parties to a dispute what their rights, responsibilities, or obligations are, without awarding damages or ordering the parties to do or refrain from doing anything. Parties generally seek a declaratory judgment to resolve uncertainty and to avoid the possibility of a future lawsuit. Under the federal Declaratory Judgment Act, a federal court may award declaratory relief in actions within the court’s original jurisdiction, with the exception of certain actions concerning taxes, bankruptcy, free trade, or drug patents. Most states have enacted statutes authorizing their courts to issue declaratory judgments. See Mobil Oil Corp. v. Advanced Env’t Recycling Techs., Inc., 203 F.R.D. 156 (D. Del. 2001); Jaskot v. Brown, 167 F.R.D. 372 (S.D.N.Y. 1996). See Mobil Oil Corp. v. Advanced Env’t Recycling Techs., Inc., 203 F.R.D. 156 (D. Del. 2001); see also Chess v. Nieport, 386 F. Supp. 312 (E.D. Cal. 1974). Mich. Surgery Inv., LLC v. Arman, 627 F.3d 572 (6th Cir. 2010); Jaramillo v. Burkhart, 59 F.3d 78 (8th Cir. 1995); Andes v. Versant Corp., 788 F.2d 1033 (4th Cir. 1986). ASX Inv. Corp. v. Newton, 183 F.3d 1265 (11th Cir. 1999); see In re Estate of Migliaccio, 436 F. Supp. 2d 1095 (C.D. Cal. 2006). See, e.g., Russell-Brown v. Jerry, 270 F.R.D. 654 (N.D. Fla. 2010). See, e.g., Hughes v. Holland, 320 F.2d 781 (D.C. Cir. 1963). See Lacy v. Sitel Corp., 227 F.3d 290, 292 (5th Cir. 2000), Berthelsen v. Kane, 907 F.2d 617, 620 (6th Cir. 1990). Anderson v. Liberty Lobby, Inc., 477 U.S. 242 (1986). Celotex Corp. v. Catrett, 477 U.S. 317 (1986); see Scott v. Harris, 550 U.S. 372, 380 (2007) (“When opposing parties tell two different stories, one of which is blatantly contradicted by the record, so that no reasonable jury could believe it, a court should not adopt that version of the facts for purposes of ruling on a motion for summary judgment.”). Stinnett v. Iron Works Gym/Executive Health Spa, Inc., 301 F.3d 610, 613 (7th Cir. 2002). 28 U.S.C. § 1746. See White’s Landing Fisheries v. Buchholzer, 29 F.3d 229 (6th Cir. 1994); Weir v. Anaconda Co., 773 F.2d 1073 (10th Cir. 1985). See, e.g., Ortiz v. Jordan, 562 U.S. 180 (2011). See, e.g., Dupree v. Younger, 598 U.S. 729 (2023). 28 U.S.C. § 2201.

rules.rule_statement: Rule Rule 38 provides that the right of trial by jury as declared by the Seventh Amendment to the U.S. Constitution, or as provided by a federal statute, is preserved to the parties inviolate. Under Rule 38(b), any party may make a written demand for a trial by jury.
rules.short_rule_statement: Rule Rule 38 provides that the right of trial by jury as declared by the Seventh Amendment to the U.S. Constitution, or as provided by a federal statute, is preserved to the...
rules.source_text:
Jury Trial
Rule Rule 38 provides that the right of trial by jury as declared by the Seventh Amendment to the U.S. Constitution, or as provided by a federal statute, is preserved to the parties inviolate. Under Rule 38(b), any party may make a written demand for a trial by jury.

rules.rule_statement: An action at law is an action for damages. An action in equity is typically an action for an injunctive relief. In general, an action at law is tried on demand to a jury, but an action in equity is not. If a new cause of action that was unknown at common law is created, then the court must look to the remedy sought and allow a jury trial if the relief sought is legal rather than equitable. In federal civil cases, the amount in controversy must exceed $20 for there to be a right to a jury trial.
rules.short_rule_statement: An action at law is an action for damages. An action in equity is typically an action for an injunctive relief. In general, an action at law is tried on demand to a jury, but...
rules.source_text:
Right To A Jury Trial
An action at law is an action for damages. An action in equity is typically an action for an injunctive relief. In general, an action at law is tried on demand to a jury, but an action in equity is not. If a new cause of action that was unknown at common law is created, then the court must look to the remedy sought and allow a jury trial if the relief sought is legal rather than equitable. In federal civil cases, the amount in controversy must exceed $20 for there to be a right to a jury trial.

rules.rule_statement: The right to trial by jury is evaluated for each claim. If an action involves both legal and equitable claims, then the jury normally determines the legal claims first, and the court then determines the equitable claims. In these cases, the court is bound by the jury’s findings on the legal claims.
rules.short_rule_statement: The right to trial by jury is evaluated for each claim. If an action involves both legal and equitable claims, then the jury normally determines the legal claims first, and the...
rules.source_text:
Legal And Equitable Claims—Trial Order
The right to trial by jury is evaluated for each claim. If an action involves both legal and equitable claims, then the jury normally determines the legal claims first, and the court then determines the equitable claims. In these cases, the court is bound by the jury’s findings on the legal claims.

rules.rule_statement: With regard to state-law claims in diversity actions, federal law generally determines whether there is a right to a jury trial and whether an issue is legal or equitable. However, federal courts apply state law when considering a motion for a new trial based on excessiveness of the verdict. California Distinction: Jury Trial Right The right to a jury trial in California arises under the California Constitution rather than the Seventh Amendment of the U.S. Constitution. The right to a jury trial is inviolate under Article I, sec. 16 of the state constitution. This right exists in all actions that are legal in nature, such as actions to recover real or personal property, for money due on a contract, and for injuries. Cal. Const. Art. I, § 16.
rules.short_rule_statement: With regard to state-law claims in diversity actions, federal law generally determines whether there is a right to a jury trial and whether an issue is legal or equitable....
rules.source_text:
Diversity Cases
With regard to state-law claims in diversity actions, federal law generally determines whether there is a right to a jury trial and whether an issue is legal or equitable. However, federal courts apply state law when considering a motion for a new trial based on excessiveness of the verdict. California Distinction: Jury Trial Right The right to a jury trial in California arises under the California Constitution rather than the Seventh Amendment of the U.S. Constitution. The right to a jury trial is inviolate under Article I, sec. 16 of the state constitution. This right exists in all actions that are legal in nature, such as actions to recover real or personal property, for money due on a contract, and for injuries. Cal. Const. Art. I, § 16.

rules.rule_statement: When a party makes a written demand for a trial by jury under Rule 38(b), the demand is filed, and the action must be designated on the docket as a jury action. The demand may be made separately or made in a pleading. The demand must be served within 14 days after service of the last pleading directed to the issue that is sought to be tried by a jury and filed with the court within a reasonable time after service. A party waives a jury trial unless the demand is properly served and filed. California Distinction: Jury Trial Demand A party must announce a demand for a jury trial when the case is first set for trial or within 5 days after notice of setting under Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 631. Failure to timely demand a jury trial may result in a waiver of the right. A party must also make a timely deposit of jury fees and expenses.
rules.short_rule_statement: When a party makes a written demand for a trial by jury under Rule 38(b), the demand is filed, and the action must be designated on the docket as a jury action. The demand may...
rules.source_text:
Jury Demand And Waiver
When a party makes a written demand for a trial by jury under Rule 38(b), the demand is filed, and the action must be designated on the docket as a jury action. The demand may be made separately or made in a pleading. The demand must be served within 14 days after service of the last pleading directed to the issue that is sought to be tried by a jury and filed with the court within a reasonable time after service. A party waives a jury trial unless the demand is properly served and filed. California Distinction: Jury Trial Demand A party must announce a demand for a jury trial when the case is first set for trial or within 5 days after notice of setting under Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 631. Failure to timely demand a jury trial may result in a waiver of the right. A party must also make a timely deposit of jury fees and expenses.

rules.rule_statement: A party may specify the issues for which it is demanding a jury trial. Otherwise, a jury trial demand is treated as requesting a jury trial regarding all issues triable by a jury. If a party requests a jury trial for only some issues triable by a jury, then any other party may serve a jury trial demand on any or all remaining issues that are triable by a jury. This party must file its demand within 14 days of being served with the earlier jury trial demand or any shorter time set by the court. Rule 38(c). After a demand is filed, the trial on all issues demanded must be by jury unless:

Issues
A party may specify the issues for which it is demanding a jury trial. Otherwise, a jury trial demand is treated as requesting a jury trial regarding all issues triable by a jury. If a party requests a jury trial for only some issues triable by a jury, then any other party may serve a jury trial demand on any or all remaining issues that are triable by a jury. This party must file its demand within 14 days of being served with the earlier jury trial demand or any shorter time set by the court. Rule 38(c). After a demand is filed, the trial on all issues demanded must be by jury unless:

rules.canonical_name: The Parties Or Their Attorneys Agree To A Nonjury Trial;
rules.rule_statement: ii) On motion or on its own, the court finds that there is no federal right to a jury trial on some or all of those issues. Rule 39(a). Issues on which a jury trial is not properly demanded are to be tried by the court. However, on motion, the court may order a jury trial on any issue for which a jury might have been demanded. Rule 39(b).

The Parties Or Their Attorneys Agree To A Nonjury Trial;
ii) On motion or on its own, the court finds that there is no federal right to a jury trial on some or all of those issues. Rule 39(a). rules.rule_statement: Even in actions in which there is no right to a jury trial, the court has discretion to authorize an advisory jury. The court may submit any issues of fact it chooses to the advisory jury and may either adopt or disregard all or part of the advisory jury’s findings. The findings of the advisory jury are not binding, and instructions given to the advisory jury are not subject to appellate review. Rule 39(c).

Advisory Jury
Even in actions in which there is no right to a jury trial, the court has discretion to authorize an advisory jury. The court may submit any issues of fact it chooses to the advisory jury and may either adopt or disregard all or part of the advisory jury’s findings. The findings of the advisory jury are not binding, and instructions given to the advisory jury are not subject to appellate review. Rule 39(c).

rules.rule_statement: A party may withdraw a jury trial demand with the consent of the other parties. Rule 38(d).
rules.short_rule_statement: A party may withdraw a jury trial demand with the consent of the other parties. Rule 38(d).
rules.source_text:
Withdrawal
A party may withdraw a jury trial demand with the consent of the other parties. Rule 38(d).

rules.canonical_name: Removal Of A Case From State Court
rules.rule_statement: If a case is removed from state court, a party who made a jury trial demand in accordance with state law need not renew the demand. If state law does not require a party to make a jury trial demand, then a party need not make one after removal unless the court orders the parties to do so. A party may request that the court make such an order, and the court must comply. If all necessary pleadings were filed before removal, a party entitled to a jury trial under Rule 38 may make a jury trial demand:

Removal Of A Case From State Court

A party may request that the court make such an order, and the court must comply. If all necessary pleadings were filed before removal, a party entitled to a jury trial under Rule 38 may make a jury trial demand:

rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 48(a), a jury must initially have at least 6 members and no more than 12 members. Once selected, a juror must participate in the verdict unless dismissed for good cause. No provision exists in the Rules for the selection of alternate jurors, as is the practice in some states. California Distinction: Jury Composition In California civil cases, juries must have 12 members unless the parties agree to fewer. If a juror is excused after trial commences, the trial will proceed with fewer than 12 jurors unless a party objects. Cal. Const. Art. I, § 16.

Jury Size
Under Rule 48(a), a jury must initially have at least 6 members and no more than 12 members. Once selected, a juror must participate in the verdict unless dismissed for good cause. No provision exists in the Rules for the selection of alternate jurors, as is the practice in some states. If a juror is excused after trial commences, the trial will proceed with fewer than 12 jurors unless a party objects. Cal. Const. Art. I, § 16.

rules.rule_statement: Voir dire is the process in which the court, the parties, or the parties’ attorneys examine potential jurors about their backgrounds and potential biases before selecting which potential jurors will sit on the jury. Under Rule 47(a), the court may permit the parties or their attorneys to examine prospective jurors or the court may do so itself. If the court examines the jurors, then it must:

Voir Dire
Voir dire is the process in which the court, the parties, or the parties’ attorneys examine potential jurors about their backgrounds and potential biases before selecting which potential jurors will sit on the jury. Under Rule 47(a), the court may permit the parties or their attorneys to examine prospective jurors or the court may do so itself. If the court examines the jurors, then it must:

rules.rule_statement: ii) Ask any of their additional questions that it considers proper.

rules.rule_statement: Each party may exercise three peremptory challenges in civil cases. Several coparties may be considered as a single party for the purposes of making those challenges. Alternatively, the court may allow additional peremptory challenges and permit them to be exercised separately or jointly. California Point of Law: Peremptory Challenges Under California law, a party may not use a peremptory challenge to remove a prospective juror based on an assumption that the juror is biased because of the juror’s race, color, religion, sex, national origin, ethnic group identification, sexual orientation, age, genetic information, or disability. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 231.5. Crossover Topic: Criminal Procedure Peremptory challenges may not be made for racial or gender-based reasons. See Themis Criminal Procedure outline § V.A.3.b.5. Peremptory Challenges, regarding the rules for contesting a discriminatory use of a peremptory challenge. Each party is entitled to an unlimited number of challenges for cause, such as bias or a personal relationship with a litigant. The court rules on these challenges.

rules.source_text:
Challenges To Jurors
Each party may exercise three peremptory challenges in civil cases. Several coparties may be considered as a single party for the purposes of making those challenges. Alternatively, the court may allow additional peremptory challenges and permit them to be exercised separately or jointly. California Point of Law: Peremptory Challenges Under California law, a party may not use a peremptory challenge to remove a prospective juror based on an assumption that the juror is biased because of the juror’s race, color, religion, sex, national origin, ethnic group identification, sexual orientation, age, genetic information, or disability. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 231.5. Crossover Topic: Criminal Procedure Peremptory challenges may not be made for racial or gender-based reasons. See Themis Criminal Procedure outline § V.A.3.b.5. Peremptory Challenges, regarding the rules for contesting a discriminatory use of a peremptory challenge. Each party is entitled to an unlimited numbe

rules.rule_statement: If the jury is to render a general verdict, it is the judge’s duty to give the instructions and explanations necessary to enable the jury to render a general verdict and answer the questions in writing. If the jury is to render specific written findings of fact, it is the judge’s duty to give the instructions and explanations necessary to enable the jury to make its findings. Rule 49(a)(2), (b)(1).
rules.short_rule_statement: If the jury is to render a general verdict, it is the judge’s duty to give the instructions and explanations necessary to enable the jury to render a general verdict and answer...
rules.source_text:
Jury Instructions
If the jury is to render a general verdict, it is the judge’s duty to give the instructions and explanations necessary to enable the jury to render a general verdict and answer the questions in writing. If the jury is to render specific written findings of fact, it is the judge’s duty to give the instructions and explanations necessary to enable the jury to make its findings. Rule 49(a)(2), (b)(1).

rules.canonical_name: Party’S Right To Request Speciﬁc Instructions
rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 51, a party has the right to request that the court give specific instructions to the jury regarding its verdict. At the close of evidence, a party may file and furnish to every other party proposed jury instructions. The court may require that the party do this at an earlier reasonable time, including before trial. After the close of evidence, a party has the right to file requests for instructions on issues that could not reasonably have been anticipated at that earlier time. Additionally, the court may permit a party to file an untimely request. Rule 51(a).
rules.short_rule_statement: Under Rule 51, a party has the right to request that the court give specific instructions to the jury regarding its verdict. At the close of evidence, a party may file and...

Party’S Right To Request Speciﬁc Instructions
Under Rule 51, a party has the right to request that the court give specific instructions to the jury regarding its verdict. At the close of evidence, a party may file and furnish to every other party proposed jury instructions. The court may require that the party do this at an earlier reasonable time, including before trial. After the close of evidence, a party has the right to file requests for instructions on issues that could not reasonably have been anticipated at that earlier time. Additionally, the court may permit a party to file an untimely request. Rule 51(a).
topic_path: Party’S Right To Request Speciﬁc Instructions

rules.rule_statement: Before final arguments, the court must inform the parties of any instructions it proposes to give to the jury regarding its verdict. The court must also inform the parties of its proposed action on the parties’ requests for jury instructions. Rule 51(b).
rules.short_rule_statement: Before final arguments, the court must inform the parties of any instructions it proposes to give to the jury regarding its verdict. The court must also inform the parties of...
rules.source_text:
Court’S Proposed Instructions
Before final arguments, the court must inform the parties of any instructions it proposes to give to the jury regarding its verdict. The court must also inform the parties of its proposed action on the parties’ requests for jury instructions. Rule 51(b).

rules.canonical_name: Party’S Objection To The Court’S Proposed Instructions
rules.rule_statement: The court must provide the parties with an opportunity to object to its proposed instructions on the record and out of the jury’s hearing before the instructions and arguments are delivered. Rule 51(b)(2). If the court fails to do this, a party’s objection is timely if it is made promptly after learning that an instruction has been or will be given or a request has been refused. Rule 51(c)(2). The party’s objection must be made on the record and specifically state the instruction or request objected to and the grounds for the objection. Rule 51(c)(1).

rules.rule_statement: The court may instruct a jury regarding its verdict at any time before the jury is discharged. Consequently, the court may instruct the jury before as well as after the lawyers’ closing arguments. Rule 51(b)(3). Example: After beginning its deliberations, a jury sends the judge a note requesting additional instructions regarding the definition of a complex term of art. The judge is permitted to provide additional jury instructions regarding the term of art.

Timing Of Instructions
The court may instruct a jury regarding its verdict at any time before the jury is discharged. Consequently, the court may instruct the jury before as well as after the lawyers’ closing arguments. Rule 51(b)(3). Example: After beginning its deliberations, a jury sends the judge a note requesting additional instructions regarding the definition of a complex term of art. The judge is permitted to provide additional jury instructions regarding the term of art.

rules.rule_statement: As a general matter, a party cannot raise the matter of jury instructions on appeal unless: i) A party timely and properly objects on the record to an erroneous instruction given or proposed to be given by the court; or ii) There is a timely and proper objection on the record to the court’s failure to give an instruction that was timely requested by a party. However, a plain error regarding the jury instructions that affects substantial rights may be addressed by an appellate court. Rule 51(d).

Appeal
As a general matter, a party cannot raise the matter of jury instructions on appeal unless: i) A party timely and properly objects on the record to an erroneous instruction given or proposed to be given by the court; or ii) There is a timely and proper objection on the record to the court’s failure to give an instruction that was timely requested by a party. However, a plain error regarding the jury instructions that affects substantial rights may be addressed by an appellate court. Rule 51(d).

rules.rule_statement: A juror is required to listen to the evidence presented in court and weigh that evidence fairly when reaching a verdict. Jurors should not consider other matters not formally admitted into evidence or discuss the facts of the case with a nonjuror.

rules.source_text:
Juror’S Duties
A juror is required to listen to the evidence presented in court and weigh that evidence fairly when reaching a verdict. Jurors should not consider other matters not formally admitted into evidence or discuss the facts of the case with a nonjuror.

rules.rule_statement: The court may excuse a juror for good cause not only before the submission of the case to the jury, but also during jury deliberations. Rule 47(c). Example: Sickness, family emergency, or juror misconduct can constitute good cause for excusing a juror. Example: Refusal of a juror to agree with other members of the jury does not constitute good cause for excusing a juror.

Example: Refusal of a juror to agree with other members of the jury does not constitute good cause for excusing a juror.

rules.rule_statement: A verdict is a formal decision rendered by a jury at the conclusion of a trial.
rules.short_rule_statement: A verdict is a formal decision rendered by a jury at the conclusion of a trial.
rules.source_text:
Jury Verdicts
A verdict is a formal decision rendered by a jury at the conclusion of a trial.

rules.rule_statement: The jury verdict must be unanimous unless the parties stipulate otherwise. Rule 48(b). California Distinction: Jury Unanimity California law, unlike federal law, does not require unanimity. A verdict may be rendered if three-fourths of the jurors agree. Cal. Const. Art. I, § 16.

Unanimity Requirement
The jury verdict must be unanimous unless the parties stipulate otherwise. Rule 48(b). California Distinction: Jury Unanimity California law, unlike federal law, does not require unanimity. A verdict may be rendered if three-fourths of the jurors agree. Cal. Const. Art. I, § 16.

rules.rule_statement: The verdict must be returned by a jury of at least six members unless the parties stipulate otherwise. Rule 48(b).
rules.short_rule_statement: The verdict must be returned by a jury of at least six members unless the parties stipulate otherwise. Rule 48(b).
rules.source_text:
Number Of Jurors
The verdict must be returned by a jury of at least six members unless the parties stipulate otherwise. Rule 48(b).

rules.rule_statement: There are three types of permissible verdicts. Rule 49. The type used is subject to the discretion of the court. A special verdict is a written finding made by the jury on each issue of ultimate fact. With a special verdict, the court submits written questions to the jury correlating to each ultimate fact of the case and asks the jury to make a finding on each fact (e.g., “Was the defendant negligent?”). The court may specifically direct the jury’s response to a question (e.g., “Yes or No”) or may permit the jury to respond with a brief answer (e.g., the percentage of a defendant’s fault in a negligence action). The judge then determines the legal consequences of those findings. Rule 49(a)(1). Even in a case based on diversity jurisdiction, the court is not bound by a state law that requires the use of a special verdict.
rules.short_rule_statement: There are three types of permissible verdicts. Rule 49. The type used is subject to the discretion of the court. A special verdict is a written finding made by the jury on each...
rules.source_text:
Form Of Verdict
There are three types of permissible verdicts. Rule 49. The type used is subject to the discretion of the court. A special verdict is a written finding made by the jury on each issue of ultimate fact. With a special verdict, the court submits written questions to the jury correlating to each ultimate fact of the case and asks the jury to make a finding on each fact (e.g., “Was the defendant negligent?”). The court may specifically direct the jury’s response to a question (e.g., “Yes or No”) or may permit the jury to respond with a brief answer (e.g., the percentage of a defendant’s fault in a negligence action). The judge then determines the legal consequences of those findings. Rule 49(a)(1). Even in a case based on diversity jurisdiction, the court is not bound by a state law that requires the use of a special verdict.

rules.rule_statement: A party waives the right to a jury trial on any issue of fact raised by the pleadings or evidence but not submitted to the jury, unless, before the jury retires, the party demands submission of the issue to the jury. Otherwise, the court may make a finding on that issue. When the court does not do so, it is deemed to have made a finding consistent with its judgment on the special verdict. Rule 49(a)(3). A general verdict is typically a decision by the jury regarding the prevailing party and, if the plaintiff is the prevailing party, the amount of damages. General verdicts are the usual form of a jury verdict in a civil case. A general verdict with special interrogatories couples a general verdict with a special verdict. It is used to ensure that the jury independently considered the material facts of the case in arriving at its verdict. If the answers to the interrogatories are consistent with each other but not with the general verdict, then the court may:

Waiver

Otherwise, the court may make a finding on that issue. When the court does not do so, it is deemed to have made a finding consistent with its judgment on the special verdict. Rule 49(a)(3). A general verdict is typically a decision by the jury regarding the prevailing party and, if the plaintiff is the prevailing party, the amount of damages. General verdicts are the usual form of a jury verdict in a civil case. A general verdict with special interrogatories couples a general verdict with a special verdict. It is used to ensure that the jury independently considered the material facts of the case in arriving at its verdict. If the answers to the interrogatories are consistent with each other but not with the general verdict, then the court may:

rules.canonical_name: Approve A Judgment That Is Consistent With The Answers, Notwithstanding The...
rules.rule_statement: ii) Direct the jury to reconsider its answers and verdict, or iii) Order a new trial. In cases of inconsistency between the answers, the court cannot enter a judgment, but must either direct the jury to reconsider its answers or order a new trial. Rule 49(b). Before the dismissal of the jury, if a party believes that the jury has returned an erroneous verdict that can be corrected, the party must raise this issue with the court. If the court believes that the jury did not properly follow its instructions, then the court may set aside the verdict and ask the jury to reconsider the issue. Alternatively, the court may order a new trial.

rules.rule_statement: After the jury returns a verdict and before the jury is dismissed, the court must poll the jurors individually if a party requests. The court may also do so on its own initiative. If there is a lack of unanimity or assent by the number of jurors to which the parties stipulated, the court may order the jury to continue deliberations, or it may order a new trial. Rule 48(c).

Polling
After the jury returns a verdict and before the jury is dismissed, the court must poll the jurors individually if a party requests. The court may also do so on its own initiative. If there is a lack of unanimity or assent by the number of jurors to which the parties stipulated, the court may order the jury to continue deliberations, or it may order a new trial. Rule 48(c).

To obtain a new trial, a party must not only demonstrate that the juror failed to answer honestly a material question on voir dire, but also show that a proper response would have provided a valid basis for a challenge for cause. The court may dismiss a juror or, if necessary, order a new trial if:

rules.canonical_name: The Juror Violates The Confidentiality Of Deliberations
rules.rule_statement: ii) The juror is improperly influenced by nonjurors; or iii) The juror investigates facts outside of those presented at trial. While generally a juror may not impeach one’s own verdict, a juror may testify about whether extraneous prejudicial information was improperly brought to the jury’s attention or whether any outside influence was improperly brought to bear on a juror. Fed. R. Evid. 606(b). Beacon Theatres, Inc. v. Westover, 359 U.S. 500 (1959). Simler v. Conner, 372 U.S. 221 (1963). Gasperini v. Center for Humanities, 518 U.S. 415 (1996). rules.canonical_name: No Right To A Jury Trial Exists;
rules.rule_statement: ii) The right to a jury trial has been waived. As the finder of fact, the court must find the facts specially and state its conclusions of law separately. The findings and conclusions may be stated on the record after the close of evidence or may appear in an opinion or a memorandum of decision filed by the court. The court is not required to state findings or conclusions when ruling on a motion under Rule 12 (motions against the complaint) or Rule 56 (summary judgment) or, unless the Rules provide otherwise, on any other motion. Rule 52(a).

rules.canonical_name: Join For Hearing Or Trial Any Or All Matters At Issue In The Actions
rules.rule_statement: ii) Consolidate the actions; or iii) Issue any other orders to avoid unnecessary cost or delay. Rule 42(a). Conversely, a court may order a separate trial of one or more issues, claims (including crossclaims, counterclaims, and third-party claims) for convenience, to avoid prejudice or to expedite and economize. When doing so, the court must preserve any federal right to a jury trial. Rule 42(b).

rules.rule_statement: A prejudgment attachment provides for seizing a person or property to secure satisfaction of a potential judgment. At the commencement and through the course of an action, a plaintiff may file a motion for prejudgment attachment as allowed by the state where the court is located. Federal statute governs the extent to which the state statute may be applied. Remedies include arrest, attachment, garnishment, replevin, sequestration, and other equivalent remedies. In addition to filing the motion, the plaintiff must give the defendant notice and an opportunity to be heard by preparing a writ of attachment in addition to a summons and complaint. A court will grant a prejudgment attachment only if the plaintiff shows that there is a likelihood of success of recovering an amount equal to the amount being sought to attach. Rule 64.
rules.short_rule_statement: A prejudgment attachment provides for seizing a person or property to secure satisfaction of a potential judgment. At the commencement and through the course of an action, a...
rules.source_text:
Prejudgment Attachment
A prejudgment attachment provides for seizing a person or property to secure satisfaction of a potential judgment. At the commencement and through the course of an action, a plaintiff may file a motion for prejudgment attachment as allowed by the state where the court is located. Federal statute governs the extent to which the state statute may be applied. Remedies include arrest, attachment, garnishment, replevin, sequestration, and other equivalent remedies. In addition to filing the motion, the plaintiff must give the defendant notice and an opportunity to be heard by preparing a writ of attachment in addition to a summons and complaint. A court will grant a prejudgment attachment only if the plaintiff shows that there is a likelihood of success of recovering an amount equal to the amount being sought to attach. Rule 64.

rules.canonical_name: Judgment As A Matter Of Law
rules.rule_statement: Rule 50 provides procedural requirements for challenging the sufficiency of the evidence in a civil jury trial. The Rule establishes two separate but related stages for such challenges: motion for judgment as a matter of law and motion for judgment notwithstanding the verdict. A motion for summary judgment as a matter of law is a motion that is made under Rule 50(a) before submission of the case to the jury. It is also called a motion for a directed verdict. A motion for judgment notwithstanding the verdict is a renewed motion for judgment as a matter of law. A motion for judgment notwithstanding the verdict is made under Rule 50(b) by a party after the verdict and entry of judgment.
rules.short_rule_statement: Rule 50 provides procedural requirements for challenging the sufficiency of the evidence in a civil jury trial. The Rule establishes two separate but related stages for such...
rules.source_text:
Judgment As A Matter Of Law
Rule 50 provides procedural requirements for challenging the sufficiency of the evidence in a civil jury trial. The Rule establishes two separate but related stages for such challenges: motion for judgment as a matter of law and motion for judgment notwithstanding the verdict. A motion for summary judgment as a matter of law is a motion that is made under Rule 50(a) before submission of the case to the jury. It is also called a motion for a directed verdict. A motion for judgment notwithstanding the verdict is a renewed motion for judgment as a matter of law. A motion for judgment notwithstanding the verdict is made under Rule 50(b) by a party after the verdict and entry of judgment.
topic_path: Judgment As A Matter Of Law

rules.canonical_name: Motion For Judgment As A Matter Of Law (“Directed Verdict”)
rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 50(a), once a party has been fully heard on an issue at a jury trial, the court may grant a motion for judgment as a matter of law resolving the issue against a party if the court finds that there is insufficient evidence for a jury reasonably to find for that party. The court may also grant such a motion against the party on any claim or defense that is dependent on a favorable finding on that issue.
rules.short_rule_statement: Under Rule 50(a), once a party has been fully heard on an issue at a jury trial, the court may grant a motion for judgment as a matter of law resolving the issue against a...

Motion For Judgment As A Matter Of Law (“Directed Verdict”)
Under Rule 50(a), once a party has been fully heard on an issue at a jury trial, the court may grant a motion for judgment as a matter of law resolving the issue against a party if the court finds that there is insufficient evidence for a jury reasonably to find for that party. The court may also grant such a motion against the party on any claim or defense that is dependent on a favorable finding on that issue.

Motion For Judgment As A Matter Of Law (“Directed Verdict”)

rules.rule_statement: The court must view the evidence in the light most favorable to the opposing party and draw all reasonable inferences from the evidence in favor of the opposing party. The court may not consider the credibility of witnesses or evaluate the weight of the evidence, and it must disregard all evidence favorable to the moving party that the jury is not required to believe. If reasonable persons can draw different inferences, then the issue is for the jury to decide, and the motion cannot be granted.

Standard

The court may not consider the credibility of witnesses or evaluate the weight of the evidence, and it must disregard all evidence favorable to the moving party that the jury is not required to believe. If reasonable persons can draw different inferences, then the issue is for the jury to decide, and the motion cannot be granted.

rules.rule_statement: A motion for judgment as a matter of law may be made at any time before the case is submitted to the jury. The motion must specify the judgment sought and the law and facts that entitle the movant to the judgment.

rules.rule_statement: In a nonjury trial, a defendant, instead of making a motion for a judgment as a matter of law, can move for a judgment on partial findings if the plaintiff failed to prove his case. Rule 52(c). California Distinction: Motion for Nonsuit/Directed Verdict A defendant may bring a motion for nonsuit after the plaintiff’s opening statement or case- in-chief. The standard for a directed verdict applies, and the motion may be partial. If the motion is denied, the defendant may still present evidence or may later bring a motion for directed verdict. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 581c.

Judgment On Partial Findings
In a nonjury trial, a defendant, instead of making a motion for a judgment as a matter of law, can move for a judgment on partial findings if the plaintiff failed to prove his case. Rule 52(c). California Distinction: Motion for Nonsuit/Directed Verdict A defendant may bring a motion for nonsuit after the plaintiff’s opening statement or case- in-chief. The standard for a directed verdict applies, and the motion may be partial. If the motion is denied, the defendant may still present evidence or may later bring a motion for directed verdict. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 581c.
