rules.canonical_name: Renewed Motion For Judgment As A Matter Of Law (Jnov)
rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 50(b), if the court does not grant a motion for judgment as a matter of law, then the court is considered to have submitted the action to the jury subject to the court’s later deciding the legal questions raised by the motion. The movant may file a renewed motion for judgment as a matter of law no later than 28 days after the entry of judgment. If the motion addresses a jury issue not decided by a verdict, then the renewed motion must be filed no later than 28 days after the jury was discharged. A post-verdict motion under Rule 50(b) is only a renewal of the earlier motion made at the close of the evidence, and it can be granted only on grounds advanced in the pre-verdict motion. Therefore, filing a motion under Rule 50(a) for a judgment as a matter of law before the case is submitted to the jury is a requirement for consideration of a renewed motion for judgment as a matter of law under Rule 50(b). If a party fails to move for a judgment as a matter of law before the case goes to the jury, then an appellate court will not review whether a district court erred in refusing to grant the party’s renewed motion for judgment as a matter of law. In ruling on the renewed motion, the court may:

rules.source_text:
Renewed Motion For Judgment As A Matter Of Law (Jnov)
Under Rule 50(b), if the court does not grant a motion for judgment as a matter of law, then the court is considered to have submitted the action to the jury subject to the court’s later deciding the legal questions raised by the motion. The movant may file a renewed motion for judgment as a matter of law no later than 28 days after the entry of judgment. If the motion addresses a jury issue not decided by a verdict, then the renewed motion must be filed no later than 28 days after the jury was discharged. A post-verdict motion under Rule 50(b) is only a renewal of the earlier motion made at the close of the evidence, and it can be granted only on grounds advanced in the pre-verdict motion. Therefore, filing a motion under Rule 50(a) for a judgment as a matter of law before the case is submitted to the jury is a requirement for consideration of a renewed motion for judgment as a matter of law under Rule 50(b). Under Rule 50(c), if the renewed motion for judgment as a matter of law is granted and the party had alternatively moved for a new trial, the court must also determine whether the motion for a new trial should be granted if the judgment is reversed or vacated on appeal. California Distinction: JNOV A motion for JNOV in California is based on the same standard as motions for directed verdict and nonsuit, but a motion for JNOV is made after a decision is rendered and before the entry of judgment. Therefore, even if a party lost an earlier motion for directed verdict or nonsuit, the party can still move for JNOV. A party need not move for a directed verdict before moving for JNOV, which differs from federal procedure. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 629. Reeves v. Sanderson Plumbing Prods., Inc., 530 U.S. 133 (2000). See, e.g., Catlett v. Local 7370 of United Paper Workers Int’l Union, 69 F.3d 254 (8th Cir. 1995); Firestone Tire & Rubber Co. v. Pearson, 769 F.2d 1471 (10th Cir. 1985).

rules.rule_statement: In general, a judgment should grant a party the relief to which the party is entitled, even if the party has not demanded such relief in its pleadings. However, a default judgment must not differ in kind or exceed in amount what is demanded in the pleadings. Rule 54(c).

Agreement With Pleadings
In general, a judgment should grant a party the relief to which the party is entitled, even if the party has not demanded such relief in its pleadings. However, a default judgment must not differ in kind or exceed in amount what is demanded in the pleadings. Rule 54(c).

rules.rule_statement: Unless a federal statute, rule, or court order provides otherwise, the prevailing party is allowed court costs, other than attorney’s fees, without needing to file a motion. The court clerk may tax costs on 14 days’ notice. A party may challenge the clerk’s action by serving a motion within seven days. Rule 54(d)(1). By serving on an opposing party an offer to allow judgment on specified terms at least 14 days before the date set for trial, a defending party may limit those costs to the costs then accrued if:

Costs
Unless a federal statute, rule, or court order provides otherwise, the prevailing party is allowed court costs, other than attorney’s fees, without needing to file a motion. The court clerk may tax costs on 14 days’ notice. A party may challenge the clerk’s action by serving a motion within seven days. Rule 54(d)(1). By serving on an opposing party an offer to allow judgment on specified terms at least 14 days before the date set for trial, a defending party may limit those costs to the costs then accrued if:

rules.canonical_name: Motion To Amend Or Make Additional Findings
rules.rule_statement: In a nonjury trial, a party may make a motion for the court to amend its findings or to make additional findings. This motion must be made within 28 days of the entry of judgment and may be combined with a new trial motion. Rule 52(b). On appeal, a court’s finding of facts can be set aside only if clearly erroneous. The reviewing court must give consideration to the trial court’s opportunity to judge the witnesses’ credibility. Rule 52(a)(6).

Motion To Amend Or Make Additional Findings
In a nonjury trial, a party may make a motion for the court to amend its findings or to make additional findings. This motion must be made within 28 days of the entry of judgment and may be combined with a new trial motion. Rule 52(b). On appeal, a court’s finding of facts can be set aside only if clearly erroneous. The reviewing court must give consideration to the trial court’s opportunity to judge the witnesses’ credibility. Rule 52(a)(6).

rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 59(a), on motion by a party or on its own, the court may grant a new trial on all issues or concerning only certain issues or parties. Rule 59 does not specifically list the grounds that will justify a new trial. In practice, the overarching justification for a new trial is that the court may grant a new trial to prevent a miscarriage of justice. Some of the reasons that have been held to justify a new trial include:

Grounds
Under Rule 59(a), on motion by a party or on its own, the court may grant a new trial on all issues or concerning only certain issues or parties. Rule 59 does not specifically list the grounds that will justify a new trial. In practice, the overarching justification for a new trial is that the court may grant a new trial to prevent a miscarriage of justice. Some of the reasons that have been held to justify a new trial include:

Under Rule 61, the court must disregard all errors and defects that do not affect any party’s substantial rights. This is the “harmless error” rule. After giving the parties notice and an opportunity to be heard, the court may grant a timely motion for a new trial for a reason not stated in a party’s motion. The court must specify the reasons in its order.

rules.rule_statement: A motion for remittitur is a request to reduce a jury award of excessive damages. If the court determines that a verdict was excessive, then it may offer a reduction of the verdict, known as a remittitur, and grant a new trial on the condition that the remittitur is not accepted. If the court determines that the verdict was inadequate, then it only has the option of ordering a new trial. An additur (enhanced judgment) is not permitted. California Distinction: Additur A judge in California who believes that a jury’s verdict on compensatory damages is inadequate may offer the party the option of (1) a new trial or (2) an enhanced judgment as determined by the court. The party has 30 days from the clerk of court’s service of the conditional order to accept or reject the addition of damages unless the order includes a deadline. Failure to respond to the order is deemed a rejection of the addition, and a new trial limited to the issue of damages will be granted automatically. A party accepting the addition of damages must also serve and submit to the court a proposed amended judgment reflecting the modified judgment amount and any other uncontested judgment awards. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 662.5. Additur is not recognized under the Federal Rules.

rules.rule_statement: Rule 60(a) allows a court to correct a clerical or other mistake resulting from oversight or omission whenever one is found in a judgment, order, or other part of the record. Rule 60(a). An example of such a mistake would be when a court meant to enter a judgment for the plaintiff for $100,000, and it appeared in the written judgment as $10,000. The court may make such a correction on motion by a party or on its own initiative, with or without notice. However, once an appeal from the judgment or order has been docketed in the appellate court, such a correction can be made only with leave of the appellate court. Id.

Rule 60(A)—Correction Of Judgment

The court may make such a correction on motion by a party or on its own initiative, with or without notice. However, once an appeal from the judgment or order has been docketed in the appellate court, such a correction can be made only with leave of the appellate court. Id.

Fraud on the court is “limited to fraud which seriously affects the integrity of the normal process of adjudication.” “[O]nly the most egregious misconduct, such as bribery of a judge or members of a jury, or the fabrication of evidence by a party in which an attorney is implicated, will constitute a fraud on the court.” California Distinction: Relief from Default or Dismissal California has a mandatory rule providing for relief from defaults and dismissals entered against a party if it was caused by the mistake or negligence of the party’s attorney. This provision even protects against mistakes made by in-house counsel. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 473; Gutierrez v. G & M Oil Co., Inc., 108 Cal. Rptr. 3d 864 (Ct. App. 2010). Kemp v. United States, 142 S. rules.rule_statement: Under § 1291, the federal courts of appeals have jurisdiction over appeals of the final judgments of the district courts. A final judgment is a decision by the court on the merits that leaves nothing for the court to do but execute the judgment. Judicial economy alone is insufficient grounds to expand the appellate court’s jurisdiction to include nonfinal judgments. Parties must demonstrate that review of the nonfinal judgment together with the appealable order will assist the court in reviewing the appealable order. Generally, any order or other decision that adjudicates fewer than all of the claims or the rights and liabilities of fewer than all of the parties is not a final order regarding any of the claims or parties. Such an order or decision may be revised at any time before the entry of a judgment adjudicating all of the claims and all of the parties’ rights and liabilities. However, if more than one claim is presented in a case, or if there are multiple parties, then the district court may direct entry of a final judgment regarding one or more issues or parties if the court “expressly determines that there is no just reason for delay.” Rule 54(b). California Distinction: Finality No express determination is required in California. Unlike under the Federal Rule, a judgment entered by a California trial court against one of multiple parties that leaves that party with no further issues for resolution is deemed a final judgment and is immediately appealable. Morehart v. County of Santa Barbara, 7 Cal. 4th 725, 732 (1994).

rules.rule_statement: An interlocutory order is a trial court order that is typically considered nonfinal because it is issued while a case is ongoing. Most interlocutory orders, such as the denial of a summary judgment motion, a motion to dismiss, or the granting of a new trial motion, are not immediately appealable. However, certain equitable orders are reviewable immediately as of right, including:

Interlocutory Orders
An interlocutory order is a trial court order that is typically considered nonfinal because it is issued while a case is ongoing. Most interlocutory orders, such as the denial of a summary judgment motion, a motion to dismiss, or the granting of a new trial motion, are not immediately appealable. However, certain equitable orders are reviewable immediately as of right, including:

rules.canonical_name: An Order Granting, Modifying, Refusing, Or Dissolving An Injunction
rules.rule_statement: ii) An order appointing or refusing to appoint a receiver; and iii) A decree determining the rights and liabilities of the parties to admiralty cases in which appeals from final decrees are allowed. A court of appeals also has discretion to permit an appeal to be taken from such order if a district court certifies in writing that: i) An order involves a controlling question of law as to which there is substantial ground for difference of opinion; and ii) An immediate appeal from the order may materially advance the ultimate termination of the litigation. California Distinction: Appeal of Interlocutory Orders The following interlocutory orders are also immediately appealable under California law:

Upon a petition for a writ of mandamus, an appellate court can immediately review an order of a lower court that is an abuse of judicial authority. rules.rule_statement: A court of appeals has the discretion to permit an appeal from a district court order granting or denying class action certification. If the appeal is permitted, it does not stay proceedings in the district court unless the district court or the court of appeals so orders. Rule 23(f). California Distinction: Class Action Certification In California, an order granting class certification is not immediately appealable, regardless of whether the class is limited or restricted. However, a trial court’s denial of class certification as to all claims is immediately appealable. Linder v. Thrifty Oil Co., 2 P.3d 27 (Cal. 2000).

rules.rule_statement: The standard of review focuses on the degree to which an appellate court defers to a decision by the trial court or jury. It is a procedural matter that is governed by federal law, even in an action based on diversity jurisdiction.
rules.short_rule_statement: The standard of review focuses on the degree to which an appellate court defers to a decision by the trial court or jury. It is a procedural matter that is governed by federal...
rules.source_text:
Standards Of Review
The standard of review focuses on the degree to which an appellate court defers to a decision by the trial court or jury. It is a procedural matter that is governed by federal law, even in an action based on diversity jurisdiction.

rules.canonical_name: Review Of A Trial Court’S Factual Findings—Clearly Erroneous
rules.rule_statement: A trial court’s findings of fact, including a master’s findings that have been adopted by the court, may not be set aside unless “clearly erroneous.” The appellate court must give consideration to the trial court’s opportunity to judge the witnesses’ credibility. Rule 52(a). A finding is clearly erroneous when, although there is evidence to support it, the appellate court is left with the definite and firm conviction based on the entirety of the evidence that a mistake has been committed. A party may challenge the sufficiency of the evidence supporting the findings on appeal even though the party took no action in the trial court regarding them. Rule 52(a)(5). California Distinction: Standard of Proof In California, appellate review of a finding of fact must consider the standard of proof by which the fact must have been proven at trial. The default standard of proof in civil cases is the preponderance of the evidence, which requires the trier of fact to believe that the existence of a fact is more probable than its nonexistence. Cal. Evid. Code § 115. The standard of proof by clear and convincing evidence, which often applies when particularly important individual interests or rights are at stake, requires the evidence to be so clear as to leave no substantial doubt. See Weiner v. Fleischman, 286 Cal. Rptr. 40 (1991); In re Angelia P., 171 Cal. Rptr. 637 (1981). An appellate court reviewing a finding of fact made under the clear and convincing standard must determine whether the record as a whole contains substantial evidence from which a reasonable fact finder could have found it highly probable that the fact was true. Conservatorship of O.B., 266 Cal. Rptr. 3d 329 (2020).

rules.rule_statement: In general, appellate review of legal rulings is de novo. While an appellate court relies on the record created in a trial court and does not entertain the admission of additional evidence, the appellate court reviews the evidence and law without deference to the trial court’s legal rulings. Therefore, the appellate court may reach its own independent conclusions regarding the applicable law.
rules.short_rule_statement: In general, appellate review of legal rulings is de novo. While an appellate court relies on the record created in a trial court and does not entertain the admission of...
rules.source_text:
Review Of Legal Rulings—De Novo
In general, appellate review of legal rulings is de novo. While an appellate court relies on the record created in a trial court and does not entertain the admission of additional evidence, the appellate court reviews the evidence and law without deference to the trial court’s legal rulings. Therefore, the appellate court may reach its own independent conclusions regarding the applicable law.

rules.canonical_name: Review Of Discretionary Rulings—Abuse Of Discretion
rules.rule_statement: A trial court’s rulings on discretionary matters are generally subject to review under the abuse-of-discretion standard. Examples of such discretionary matters include the admissibility of evidence, sanctions for violation of discovery rules, or the granting or denial of a motion to transfer venue or to sever actions.

A trial court’s rulings on discretionary matters are generally subject to review under the abuse-of-discretion standard. Examples of such discretionary matters include the admissibility of evidence, sanctions for violation of discovery rules, or the granting or denial of a motion to transfer venue or to sever actions.

components.content: Different approaches to sufﬁciency of evidence
components.source_text: 1)      Different approaches to sufﬁciency of evidence

rules.rule_statement: In determining whether to set aside a jury verdict, it is not sufficient that the verdict is against the weight of the evidence. Some appellate courts refuse to set aside a jury verdict if there is substantial evidence supporting the verdict. Other courts require only sufficient evidence to sustain a jury verdict. Still others uphold a jury verdict unless there is no evidence to support the verdict. To preserve for appeal a challenge to the sufficiency of the evidence supporting a jury verdict, a party must: i) Properly renew a motion for directed verdict with a motion for judgment notwithstanding the verdict under Rule 50(b); or ii) Raise a motion for a new trial under Rule 59. If a party fails to do so, there is no basis for review of the challenge to the overall sufficiency of the evidence in a court of appeals, and appellate review will instead be limited to any specific claims of error that were properly preserved. Catlin v. United States, 324 U.S. 229(1945). Swint v. Chambers County Comm’n., 514 U.S. 35 (1995). 28 U.S.C. § 1292(a). 28 U.S.C. § 1292(b). Mohawk Indus., Inc. v. Carpenter, 558 U.S. 100, 105 (2009) (holding that a disclosure order whose enforcement would allegedly violate attorney-client privilege did not qualify for review under the collateral-order doctrine). Eisen v. Carlisle & Jacquelin, 417 U.S. 156 (1974). Compare Kerr v. U.S. Dist. Court, 426 U.S. 394 (1976) (mandamus inappropriate to vacate a lower court’s discovery orders) with Dairy Queen, Inc. v. Wood, 369 U.S. 469 (1962) (mandamus appropriate when a trial court denied the right to a jury trial on a legal issue). United States v. U.S. Gypsum Co., 333 U.S. 364 (1948). GE v. Joiner, 522 U.S. 136 (1997). Lavender v. Kurn, 327 U.S. 645 (1946). Unitherm Food Sys. v. Swift-Eckrich, Inc., 546 U.S. 394 (2006).

rules.rule_statement: Rule Article IV, Section 1 of the U.S. Constitution provides that “Full Faith and Credit shall be given in each State to the public Acts, Records, and judicial Proceedings of every other State.” The clause is invoked primarily to enforce the judgment of one state court in another state.

Full Faith And Credit
Rule Article IV, Section 1 of the U.S. Constitution provides that “Full Faith and Credit shall be given in each State to the public Acts, Records, and judicial Proceedings of every other State.” The clause is invoked primarily to enforce the judgment of one state court in another state.

rules.rule_statement: A party against whom enforcement is sought may collaterally challenge the original state judgment based on lack of personal jurisdiction or subject matter jurisdiction only if the jurisdictional issues were not litigated or waived in the original action.

rules.canonical_name: Preclusive Effects Of The Original State Judgment
rules.rule_statement: The requirement of full faith and credit extends the preclusive effects of the original state judgment. Therefore, if the original state judgment would bar a subsequent action in the original state, then it acts to bar a subsequent action in any other state. This is the case even if the subsequent action would otherwise be permitted in the other state. See § IX.D. Claim Preclusion (Res Judicata), infra, and § IX.E. Issue Preclusion (Collateral Estoppel), infra, for a discussion of preclusion generally.

rules.rule_statement: Under § 1738, federal courts must also give full faith and credit to state-court judgments. The same rules as discussed previously for states apply regarding a federal court.
rules.short_rule_statement: Under § 1738, federal courts must also give full faith and credit to state-court judgments. The same rules as discussed previously for states apply regarding a federal court.
rules.source_text:
Federal To State
Under § 1738, federal courts must also give full faith and credit to state-court judgments. The same rules as discussed previously for states apply regarding a federal court.

rules.rule_statement: Under federal law, the rules of res judicata determine the effects of a judgment of a federal court. However, federal common law requires that the preclusive effect of a judgment by a federal court sitting in diversity must be governed by the law of claim preclusion of the federal court’s forum state unless the state law is incompatible with federal interests. Even if the federal court has federal question jurisdiction, the preclusive effect of a prior state-court judgment will be governed by the law of that state under the Full Faith and Credit Clause. Durfee v. Duke, 375 U.S. 106 (1963). Restatement (Second) of Judgments, § 87. Semtek Int’l Inc. v. Lockheed Martin Corp., 531 U.S. 497 (2001). Migra v. Warren City Sch. Dist. Bd. of Educ., 465 U.S. 75 (1984).
rules.short_rule_statement: Under federal law, the rules of res judicata determine the effects of a judgment of a federal court. However, federal common law requires that the preclusive effect of a...

State To Federal
Under federal law, the rules of res judicata determine the effects of a judgment of a federal court. However, federal common law requires that the preclusive effect of a judgment by a federal court sitting in diversity must be governed by the law of claim preclusion of the federal court’s forum state unless the state law is incompatible with federal interests. Even if the federal court has federal question jurisdiction, the preclusive effect of a prior state-court judgment will be governed by the law of that state under the Full Faith and Credit Clause. Durfee v. Duke, 375 U.S. 106 (1963). Restatement (Second) of Judgments, § 87. Semtek Int’l Inc. v. Lockheed Martin Corp., 531 U.S. 497 (2001). Migra v. Warren City Sch. Dist. Bd. of Educ., 465 U.S. 75 (1984).

rules.rule_statement: Rule The doctrine of claim preclusion (res judicata) bars successive litigation of an identical claim in a subsequent action when: There is a valid final judgment on the merits; and The original and subsequently brought claims are sufficiently identical.

rules.rule_statement: The judgment must also be final, meaning that there is nothing further for the court to do but order entry of judgment. California Distinction: Res Judicata In California, a judgment is not considered final for res judicata purposes until (1) the appeal concludes or (ii2) if no appeal is taken, when the time for an appeal has expired. Under federal practice, a judgment is final for res judicata purposes when rendered. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 1049.

rules.rule_statement: A voluntary dismissal without prejudice expressly reserves a party’s right to sue again on the same claim in the same court if the statute of limitations has not expired. Unless the notice of dismissal states otherwise, a voluntary dismissal will be without prejudice. Rule 41(a)(1)(B).

Voluntary Dismissal Without Prejudice
A voluntary dismissal without prejudice expressly reserves a party’s right to sue again on the same claim in the same court if the statute of limitations has not expired. Unless the notice of dismissal states otherwise, a voluntary dismissal will be without prejudice. Rule 41(a)(1)(B).

rules.rule_statement: A voluntary dismissal with prejudice is treated as a judgment on the merits and will have a preclusive effect in the court that issued the order of dismissal. A dismissal on the merits under Rule 41(b) will bar a plaintiff from refiling the claim only in the same federal court, not in state court.

Voluntary Dismissal With Prejudice
A voluntary dismissal with prejudice is treated as a judgment on the merits and will have a preclusive effect in the court that issued the order of dismissal. A dismissal on the merits under Rule 41(b) will bar a plaintiff from refiling the claim only in the same federal court, not in state court.

rules.rule_statement: Unless otherwise provided by the order of dismissal, an involuntary dismissal on nonjurisdictional grounds will constitute an adjudication on the merits. Rule 41(b). While such an involuntary dismissal bars refiling of the claim in the same federal court, it does not preclude refiling of the claim in state court.

While such an involuntary dismissal bars refiling of the claim in the same federal court, it does not preclude refiling of the claim in state court.

rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 41(b), a dismissal on jurisdictional grounds, or for lack of venue or failure to join a party under Rule 19, is without prejudice because a court that has no jurisdiction cannot adjudicate a matter on the merits.

Dismissal For Lack Of Jurisdiction
Under Rule 41(b), a dismissal on jurisdictional grounds, or for lack of venue or failure to join a party under Rule 19, is without prejudice because a court that has no jurisdiction cannot adjudicate a matter on the merits.

rules.rule_statement: If a federal court with diversity jurisdiction over an action issues a judgment, then federal common law provides that a state court must give that judgment the same claim-preclusion effect that the judgment would have been given by the courts of the state where the federal court was located.

Diversity Jurisdiction
If a federal court with diversity jurisdiction over an action issues a judgment, then federal common law provides that a state court must give that judgment the same claim-preclusion effect that the judgment would have been given by the courts of the state where the federal court was located.

rules.rule_statement: A divorce decree must generally be given a preclusive effect as long as the court had jurisdiction, which usually requires only a long-term connection of the plaintiff-spouse with the forum state rather than in personam jurisdiction over the defendant-spouse. However, a decision involving property rights, including alimony or child support, or child custody is not entitled to a preclusive effect unless the court had in personam jurisdiction over the defendant-spouse.
rules.short_rule_statement: A divorce decree must generally be given a preclusive effect as long as the court had jurisdiction, which usually requires only a long-term connection of the plaintiff-spouse...
rules.source_text:
Family Law Decisions
A divorce decree must generally be given a preclusive effect as long as the court had jurisdiction, which usually requires only a long-term connection of the plaintiff-spouse with the forum state rather than in personam jurisdiction over the defendant-spouse. However, a decision involving property rights, including alimony or child support, or child custody is not entitled to a preclusive effect unless the court had in personam jurisdiction over the defendant-spouse.

rules.rule_statement: The original and subsequently brought claims must be sufficiently identical to be barred under claim preclusion. Federal courts apply a transactional approach under which they bar a subsequent claim with respect to all or any part of the transaction, or series of connected transactions, out of which the original claim arose. If a plaintiff sues on less than all claims arising from a transaction, then the unraised claims merge with the judgment if the plaintiff wins, and they are barred if the plaintiff loses. Factors that are considered in determining what constitutes a transaction or series of transactions include:

Sufﬁciently Identical Claims
The original and subsequently brought claims must be sufficiently identical to be barred under claim preclusion. Federal courts apply a transactional approach under which they bar a subsequent claim with respect to all or any part of the transaction, or series of connected transactions, out of which the original claim arose. If a plaintiff sues on less than all claims arising from a transaction, then the unraised claims merge with the judgment if the plaintiff wins, and they are barred if the plaintiff loses. Factors that are considered in determining what constitutes a transaction or series of transactions include:

rules.canonical_name: Whether The Facts Are Related In Time, Space, Origin, Or Motivation
rules.rule_statement: ii) Whether the facts form a convenient trial unit; and iii) Whether treating the facts as a unit conforms to the parties’ expectations. California Distinction: Primary Rights Doctrine In California, unlike in federal practice, courts do not apply the modern “transactional” approach in analyzing the cause-of-action prong under res judicata. Instead, California courts apply the minority “primary rights doctrine” under which causes of action are based on individual harm, and redress may be sought for one harm in each suit. For example, a plaintiff injured in an automobile accident may sue in one action for personal injury and in a separate action for property damage. Each injury is an invasion of a different “primary right.” Craig v. County of Los Angeles, 271 Cal. Rptr. 82 (Ct. App. 1990).

Whether The Facts Are Related In Time, Space, Origin, Or Motivation

California Distinction: Primary Rights Doctrine In California, unlike in federal practice, courts do not apply the modern “transactional” approach in analyzing the cause-of-action prong under res judicata. Instead, California courts apply the minority “primary rights doctrine” under which causes of action are based on individual harm, and redress may be sought for one harm in each suit. For example, a plaintiff injured in an automobile accident may sue in one action for personal injury and in a separate action for property damage. Each injury is an invasion of a different “primary right.” Craig v. County of Los Angeles, 271 Cal. Rptr. 82 (Ct. App. 1990).

rules.rule_statement: For claim preclusion to apply, the claimant and the defendant must be the same (and in the same roles) in both the original action and the subsequently filed action. Therefore, a similar action by a different party is generally not precluded. New Hampshire v. Maine, 532 U.S. 742, 748 (2001). Semtek Int’l Inc. v. Lockheed Martin Corp., 531 U.S. 497 (2001). Semtek Int’l Inc. v. Lockheed Martin Corp., 531 U.S. 497 (2001). Semtek Int’l Inc. v. Lockheed Martin Corp., 531 U.S. 497 (2001). Estin v. Estin, 334 U.S. 541 (1948).

Therefore, a similar action by a different party is generally not precluded. New Hampshire v. Maine, 532 U.S. 742, 748 (2001). Semtek Int’l Inc. v. Lockheed Martin Corp., 531 U.S. 497 (2001). rules.rule_statement: Rule The doctrine of issue preclusion (collateral estoppel) precludes the relitigation of issues of fact or law when the same issue was already litigated and necessarily determined in an earlier final, valid judgment.
rules.short_rule_statement: Rule The doctrine of issue preclusion (collateral estoppel) precludes the relitigation of issues of fact or law when the same issue was already litigated and necessarily...
rules.source_text:
Issue Preclusion (Collateral Estoppel)
Rule The doctrine of issue preclusion (collateral estoppel) precludes the relitigation of issues of fact or law when the same issue was already litigated and necessarily determined in an earlier final, valid judgment.

rules.rule_statement: Unlike claim preclusion, issue preclusion does not require strict mutuality of parties; it requires only that the party against whom the issue will be precluded was a party to the original action. Therefore, offensive use of collateral estoppel is permitted. Trial courts have broad discretion to determine whether offensive collateral estoppel should be applied. If a plaintiff could easily have joined in the earlier action or if offensive estoppel is found to be unfair to a defendant, then a trial judge should not allow it.
rules.short_rule_statement: Unlike claim preclusion, issue preclusion does not require strict mutuality of parties; it requires only that the party against whom the issue will be precluded was a party to...
rules.source_text:
In General
Unlike claim preclusion, issue preclusion does not require strict mutuality of parties; it requires only that the party against whom the issue will be precluded was a party to the original action. Therefore, offensive use of collateral estoppel is permitted. Trial courts have broad discretion to determine whether offensive collateral estoppel should be applied. If a plaintiff could easily have joined in the earlier action or if offensive estoppel is found to be unfair to a defendant, then a trial judge should not allow it.

Same Issue
The issue sought to be precluded must be the same as that involved in the prior action. The facts relevant to the particular issue and the applicable law must be identical for issue preclusion to apply.

rules.rule_statement: The issue must have been actually litigated in the prior action for issue preclusion to apply. California Distinction: Issue “Actually Litigated” Unlike in federal practice, default and consent judgments in California are given issue- preclusive effect as to material issues actually raised in the pleadings that were necessary to the judgment. Harmon v. Kobrin (In re Harmon), 250 F.3d 1240 (9th Cir. Cal. 2001); California State Auto. Assn. Inter-Ins. Bureau v. Superior Court, 50 Cal.3d 658, n.2 (1990).

Final, Valid Judgment

rules.rule_statement: The determination of the issue must have been essential to the prior judgment. Generally, an issue that constitutes a necessary component of the decision reached will be considered essential to the judgment.

rules.source_text:
Essential To The Judgment
The determination of the issue must have been essential to the prior judgment. Generally, an issue that constitutes a necessary component of the decision reached will be considered essential to the judgment.

rules.rule_statement: Issues decided under a court’s equitable power, such as a bankruptcy court’s avoidance of preferential transfers, can be precluded from relitigation just as legal issues can, if all the requirements for collateral estoppel are met.
rules.short_rule_statement: Issues decided under a court’s equitable power, such as a bankruptcy court’s avoidance of preferential transfers, can be precluded from relitigation just as legal issues can,...
rules.source_text:
Equitable Issues
Issues decided under a court’s equitable power, such as a bankruptcy court’s avoidance of preferential transfers, can be precluded from relitigation just as legal issues can, if all the requirements for collateral estoppel are met.

rules.rule_statement: Issues determined in a criminal prosecution in favor of the prosecution are generally preclusive in a civil action against the defendant based on the same conduct as in the criminal prosecution. Issues determined in a criminal prosecution in favor of the defendant are not preclusive in a civil action against the defendant based on the same conduct as in the criminal prosecution because the plaintiff in the civil action was not a party to the criminal prosecution. Parklane Hosiery Co. v. Shore, 439 U.S. 322 (1979). Taylor v. Sturgell, 553 U.S. 880 (2008). Comm’r of Internal Revenue v. Sunnen, 333 U.S. 591 (1948). Stoll v. Gottlieb, 305 U.S. 165 (1938) (issue preclusion applies to findings of jurisdiction that are fully litigated). See Katchen v. Landy, 382 U.S. 323 (1966). Restatement (Second) of Judgments § 85.

rules.source_text:
Criminal Prosecution
Issues determined in a criminal prosecution in favor of the prosecution are generally preclusive in a civil action against the defendant based on the same conduct as in the criminal prosecution. Issues determined in a criminal prosecution in favor of the defendant are not preclusive in a civil action against the defendant based on the same conduct as in the criminal prosecution because the plaintiff in the civil action was not a party to the criminal prosecution. Parklane Hosiery Co. v. Shore, 439 U.S. 322 (1979). Taylor v. Sturgell, 553 U.S. 880 (2008). Comm’r of Internal Revenue v. Sunnen, 333 U.S. 591 (1948). Stoll v. Gottlieb, 305 U.S. 165 (1938) (issue preclusion applies to findings of jurisdiction that are fully litigated). See Katchen v. Landy, 382 U.S. 323 (1966). Restatement (Second) of Judgments § 85.
